They observed that a significantly greater number of neurons died in the presence of the progranulin-deficient microglia when compared to unmodified microglia. Other experiments revealed the process’ underlying mechanism. Microglia are the CNS’s first line of defense. When the microglia sense toxins or injury, they trigger protective inflammation—which can become toxic to neurons if left unchecked. Dr. Farese’s team discovered that progranulin works by tempering the microglia’s response, thereby minimizing inflammation. Without progranulin, the microglia are unrestricted—and induce prolonged and excessive inflammation that leads to neuron damage—and can contribute to the vast array of symptoms that afflict sufferers FTD and other fatal forms of brain disease.

**

“However, we found that boosting progranulin levels in microglia reduced inflammation—keeping neurons alive and healthy in cell culture,” explained Dr. Farese. “Our next step is to determine if this method could also work in live animals. We believe this to be a therapeutic strategy that could, for example, halt the progression of FTD. More broadly, our findings about progranulin and inflammation could have therapeutic implications for devastating neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s and MS.”**

Chewing ability linked to reduced dementia risk

Chewing ability linked to reduced dementia risk

### chocolate — 2012-10-28T04:09:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-60506>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/10/challenging-parkinsons-dogma-14628](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/10/challenging-parkinsons-dogma-14628)

> In the October 11 issue of Nature, Sabatini and co-authors Nicolas Tritsch and Jun Ding reported that midbrain dopamine neurons release not only dopamine but also another neurotransmitter called GABA, which lowers neuronal activity. The previously unsuspected presence of GABA could explain why restoring only dopamine could cause initial improvements in Parkinsonâ€™s patients to eventually wane. And if GABA is made by the same cells that produce other neurotransmitters, such as depression-linked serotonin, similar single-focus treatments could be less successful for the same reason.
>
>
>
> â€œIf what we found in the mouse applies to the human, then dopamineâ€™s only half the story,â€￾ said Sabatini.
>
>
>
> The surprising GABA story began in the Sabatini lab with a series of experiments designed to see what happens when cells release dopamine. The scientists used optogenetics, a powerful technique that relies on genetic manipulation to selectively sensitize cells to light. In laboratory dishes, researchers tested brain tissue from mice engineered to show activity in dopamine neurons. Typically in such experiments, other neurotransmitters would be blocked in order to highlight dopamine, but Tritsch, a postdoctoral fellow in the Sabatini lab, decided instead to keep the cell in as natural a state as possible.
>
>
>
> When Tritsch activated the dopamine neurons and examined their effects on striatal neurons, he naturally expected to observe the effects of dopamine release. Instead, he saw rapid inhibition of the striatal neurons, making it clear that another neurotransmitter â€“ which turned out to be the quick-acting GABA â€“ was at work. This was so unusual that the team launched a series of experiments to confirm that GABA was being released directly by these dopamine neurons.

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T00:43:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-63713>

Scientists reveal key protein interactions involved in neurodegenerative disease

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/scientists-reveal-key-protein-interactions-involved-in-neurodegenerative-disease-14887](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/scientists-reveal-key-protein-interactions-involved-in-neurodegenerative-disease-14887)

> The scientists used what they called structure class analysis, looking at groups of structures, which revealed subtle differences not apparent looking at them individually.
>
>
>
> “From a structural point of view, these different proteins appear to be very similar, but the biochemistry shows that the results of their binding to JNK were very different,” he said.
>
>
>
> LoGrasso and his colleagues were responsible for creating and solving the crystal structures of the three peptides (JIP1, SAB, and ATF-2) with JNK3 using a technique called x-ray crystallography, while Nettles handled much of the data analysis.
>
>
>
> **All three peptides have important effects, LoGrasso said, inducing two distinct inhibitory mechanisms—one where the peptide caused the activation loop to bind directly in the ATP pocket, and another with allosteric control (that is, using a location on the protein other than the active site). Because JNK signaling needs to be tightly controlled, even small changes in it can alter a cell’s fate.**
>
>
>
> “Solving the crystal structures of these three bound peptides gives us a clearer idea of how we can block each of these mechanisms related to cell death and survival,” LoGrasso said. “You have to know their structure to know how to deal with them.”

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T00:48:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-63715>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/link-found-between-child-prodigies-and-autism-14858](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/link-found-between-child-prodigies-and-autism-14858)

Scientists reveal key protein interactions involved in neurodegenerative disease

> Father with child photo by Kristin MolinaroA new study of eight child prodigies suggests a possible link between these children’s special skills and autism.
>
>
>
> Of the eight prodigies studied, three had a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorders. As a group, the prodigies also tended to have slightly elevated scores on a test of autistic traits, when compared to a control group.
>
>
>
> In addition, half of the prodigies had a family member or a first- or second-degree relative with an autism diagnosis.
>
>
>
> The fact that half of the families and three of the prodigies themselves were affected by autism is surprising because autism occurs in only one of 120 individuals, said Joanne Ruthsatz, lead author of the study and assistant professor of psychology at Ohio State University’s Mansfield campus.
>
>
>
> “The link between child prodigies and autism is strong in our study,” Ruthsatz said. “Our findings suggest child prodigies have traits in common with autistic children, but something is preventing them from displaying the deficits we associate with the disorder.”
>
>
>
> **The study also found that while child prodigies had elevated general intelligence scores, where they really excelled was in working memory – all of them scored above the 99th percentile on this trait.**
>
>
>
> Ruthsatz conducted the study with Jourdan Urbach of Yale University. Their results were published in a recent issue of the journal Intelligence.

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T00:50:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-63717>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/feel-good-hormone-helps-to-jog-the-memory-14889](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/feel-good-hormone-helps-to-jog-the-memory-14889)

Feel-good hormone helps to jog the memory

> The feel-good hormone dopamine improves long-term memory. This is the finding of a team lead by Emrah DÃ¼zel, neuroscientist at the German Center for Neurodegenerative Diseases (DZNE) and the University of Magdeburg.
>
>
>
> The researchers investigated test subjects ranging in age from 65 to 75 years, who were given a precursor of dopamine. Treated subjects performed better in a memory test than a comparison group, who had taken a placebo. The study provides new insights into the formation of long lasting memories and also has implications for understanding why memories fade more rapidly following the onset of Alzheimer’s disease. The results appear in the “Journal of Neuroscience”.

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T18:07:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-63830>

[http://www.nature.com/news/infant-stress-affects-teen-brain-1.11786](http://http://www.nature.com/news/infant-stress-affects-teen-brain-1.11786)

Nature | News

Infant stress affects teen brain

Two-decade study reveals neural connection between early stress and anxiety and depression in girls.

> The researchers scanned the brains of fifty-seven 18-year-olds while they lay quietly for 7 minutes. This procedure measures ‘resting-state functional connectivity’, or the synchrony of spontaneous activity between brain regions. Regions that are humming in sync at rest are thought to be strongly connected at other times (though scientists still have much to learn about the so-called resting brain: see ‘Idle minds’).
>
>
>
> The study showed that 18-year-old girls who had had high cortisol levels at age 4 have weak connectivity between the amygdala, a deep nub of the brain known for processing fear and emotions, and the ventromedial prefrontal cortex, an outer region involved in curbing the amygdala’s stress response.
>
>
>
> But without taking cortisol into account, early stress, in itself, is not significantly correlated with the differences in brain activity seen at age 18. “So what really matters is individual differences for individual children,” notes Kathleen Thomas, a developmental psychologist at the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis, who was not involved in the work.

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T18:11:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-and-brain-stuff-new-discoveries-and.482/post-63831>

[http://www.nature.com/news/2011/110517/full/news.2011.298.html](http://http://www.nature.com/news/2011/110517/full/news.2011.298.html)

Stress can shorten telomeres in childhood

Children in orphanages have chromosome changes that could affect future health.

> A long-term study of children from Romanian orphanages suggests that the effects of childhood stress could be visible in their DNA as they grow up.
>
>
>
> Children who spent their early years in state-run Romanian orphanages have shorter telomeres than children who grew up in foster care, according to a study published today in Molecular Psychiatry1. **Telomeres are buffer regions of non-coding DNA at the ends of chromosomes that prevent the loss of protein-coding DNA when cells divide. Telomeres get slightly shorter each time a chromosome replicates during cell division, but stress can also cause them to shorten. Shorter telomeres are associated with a raft of diseases in adults from diabetes to dementia.**
>
>
>
> The study is part of the Bucharest Early Intervention Project, a programme started in 2000 by US researchers who aimed to compare the health and development of Romanian children brought up in the stressful environment of an orphanage with those in foster families, where they receive more individual attention and a better quality of care.

### Nick — 2013-05-17T10:20:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vote-for-the-book.485/post-86300>

All Done! Really sorry it took so long. BTW I now have three copies of the book so I can lend them out.

This book is an amazing repository of priceless information for anyone concerned about any health issues. I have used this information to massively change my health direction in the last 18 months. I have been battling through Dr Kruse's blogs and working through the various strands of biology, medicine, chemistry, physics and electronics which approaches health from ground up. This book however is a godsend for anyone that just wants to get the big picture quickly and build understanding from the top down.

First Dr Kruse discusses where medicine is at which is quite horrifying, then he talks about the many types of testing available to the public and what they mean to an individual. It is scary to discover the chasm between science in last 10 years and what your ordinary GP understands of this. Almost nothing! The centre half of the book is devoted to the various disease types, what to test specifically and how to action these results. The last quarter of the book ties together many of these themes into one fascinating hypothesis of how mammals evolved and through the last 65 million years and how this affects us in a practical way now, including the amazing biological trick of Cold Thermogenesis.

This is the closest thing I have seen to a 'How aTo' manual for health in the 21st century, it is eye-opening, practical and positive. Dr Kruse is one of a kind, a Dr that has time to not only work through cutting edge science in many different fields but cares enough to share this information for almost nothing in return. I think his works, including this book are almost priceless and will be looked upon in the future as a seminal, ground changing, body of information.

How has this changed me? I am in my mid forties and have changed in 18 months from being overweight, tired, brain fogged and feeling pessimistic about my health future to being a healthy weight, clear and fast thinking, a strong as I have ever been and with as much energy as I had when I was sixteen. This rocked my world! I changed my diet, changed the timing of when I ate, changed my sleep patterns and got used to being in a bath full of ice, none of which was very hard. For a bit of effort I am a new person and I think that when you look through the reviews and forums you will see I am just one of many that has easily changed for the better.

I can't thank Dr Kruse enough, besides my parents and my immediate family no one has had a bigger positive influence on me.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vote-for-the-book.485/post-86303>

Something has been bothering me all night - and I finally remembered something ......
In the back of my mind I always thought most reviews were bogus - bought and paid for........
I really thought people are paid to write reviews and so I never really pay attention to them ---
just like restaurant reviews - actually that is where this comes from ....restaurants were my world and reviews were always bought and paid for in some fashion .......I would never have hung my hat on a review .....

### QuantumRadical — 2013-05-27T00:56:06+0300

Just posted my review on Amazon! Here it is below.

Evolutionary Biology meets Quantum Biology, May 26, 2013
By Randall W. Chapman (Clifton, VA USA)
Amazon Verified Purchase(What's this?)
This review is from: Epi-paleo Rx: The Prescription for Disease Reversal and Optimal Health (Paperback)
I have been reading Dr. Kruse's Blog posts since August of 2011. I tried his protocols. And they worked. His book covers it all. He approaches health through the lens of evolutionary biology. Is there any other way? Beyond his book, and now being covered in detail in his current blog posts, he is now addressing health through the lens of Quantum Biology. Is there any other way? Ultimately, the combination of "how we got to be how we are" and "what science really says about our lives" will revolutionize health, medicine, and nutrition. In fact, I think it will impact how we do business, how we train for sports, and, well, just about all aspects of our lives. Don't knock it till you try it. I did. I am a believer. Way to go Jack!

Q'Rad Randy

# Thread: What breaks or slows cold adaptation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-breaks-or-slows-cold-adaptation.487/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-03-28T04:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-breaks-or-slows-cold-adaptation.487/post-7515>

I'm looking for an official answer, if one exists. Here is the kind of info I'm looking for.

I'm at stage X and I have food Y, this will set me back Z amount.

e.g.

1\. I'm at the cold tub stage for 20 minutes every day, I am eating a ketogenic diet with no starch/fruit and I had 10oz of ice cream over the weekend at a wedding. This will set me back 3 days while my body forgets that I ate something sweet.

2\. I'm at the compression shirt stage, up to 40 minutes, I am eating a ketogenic diet but due to work I had to look at the computer for 3-4 hours after sunset 3 days in a row. This will set me back 3 days while my body forgets about the SCN and focuses on the cold receptor...

That above examples are phony but point out the issue, in my opinion.

thanks,

jesse

### hellojtm — 2012-03-28T07:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-breaks-or-slows-cold-adaptation.487/post-7602>

Scenario time again

3\. I'm at the face dunking stage, skin temps of 49f and can stay under 25s at a time for a total of 5 minutes underwater. I am eating a ketogenic diet with no starch/fruit. I wear only shorts and a t-shirt independent of weather, a/c, etc... I took a ten minute hot shower 2 days ago. This will set me back 1 day.

(?? I have no clue how much these things impact the adaptation period or the maintenance of adaptation).

Another question, i know that there is the sweet sensor on the tongue that is important for the ancient pathway. Put a banana in that mouth and your brain wires for warm adaptation. But is it just the sweet taste? It seems that the diet is ketogenic too, the recommended one. Is that just an artifact of removing all the sweet things? I don't think so, you could be ketogenic while eating minimal sweets as long as your total glucose consumption is below your bodies threshold. So then, coming back around, what if I ate a non-ketogenic diet, but had nothing sweet to the taste, if I ate 150g of bland carbohydrates a day in the form of veggies and tubers. No sweet, will I remain adapted? Will I slow my adaptation?

I'm hoping there is an official answer.

thanks,

jesse

### hellojtm — 2012-03-28T07:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-breaks-or-slows-cold-adaptation.487/post-7606>

Another fun confounder. Eggs. Seems like one of the perfect foods for the cold adaptation scenario. But in nature eggs would only be available during spring. Does that make them a suspect for breaking cold adaptation? Surely the ingestion of an egg would indicate to the body that it's hatching season and not the dead of winter. But the only thing I've seen from Jack is ketogenic + the sweet sensor and the fat sensor in the mouth.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-28T20:24:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-breaks-or-slows-cold-adaptation.487/post-7832>

I'm pondering what cold adaptation would look like based on normal seasonal patterns. In the fall, the temperatures fall, fruits & veggies start to disappear, but tubers are still available if you know where to dig. As the season progresses, tubers disappear or the ground freezes and they are unavailable, and the cold intensifies. It makes sense to me to ease into CT in terms of both cold and diet. You'd naturally cut back carbs gradually in the fall as the temperatures drop. So, as you start doing CT, you can use warmer water and still eat some carbs to mimic the natural progress of fall, but as you adapt, you need to lower the water temperature and cut back on carbs so you are living in a true winter environment.

Furthermore, the cutback in carbs would be non-linear. You might be no-carb for a while and then come upon a stash of tubers and eat them and leave ketosis temporarily. Same with cold - the temps go down, you start to adapt, then you have an Indian summer period where it is warm again before you get more cold weather. I think as long as you trend toward lower carbs and ketosis, you should be good as you are starting CT.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-29T06:28:04+0300

I don't really have any weight, pain, or symptomatic issues to deal with. Only an elevated CRP I have not been able to figure out, and long standing thyroid disease. I still don't know if I should be taking thyroid meds if I'm cold adapted, and if my labs become meaningless if my brain is taking control over from my thyroid. If I can't be convinced the cold takes care of the thyroid completely, I expect I'll "see a summer" this summer but maintain CT so it's not hard to get back into it.

Maybe if we remove the oxygen from our homes all will become clear

You should look into it, auto-immune reactions and cross-reactivity can make you appear allergic to foods when those allergies will disappear in the lack of leaky gut induced by grains/legumes. apologies if you've already been through this.
>
>
>
> August wrote:** I'm also wonder if it makes the most sense to measure setbacks (things that break or slow cold adaptation) in days . . . I was thinking maybe it would make more sense to talk about how counterproductive or harmful those things would be . . . and maybe there is some measure . . but I'm not certain that it is a function of time exactly . . . thoughts?

Interesting. All mismatches seem to drive epigenetic hunting, which would shorten telomeres and waste stem cells and mitochondria... So eating fruit in the summer and not doing CT would be ideal. However, if you wanted to do CT then you'd have to keep your body believing it was winter, so that means CT + dark exposure + little/no fructose.... I mean it makes sense but that's the problem with a lot of things, they make sense but aren't true

jesse

My few attempts at real sauerkraut were not very good. I have to learn from some expert.... But as usual we all have our goodies....so I can brag and say that we live in rural area and have our own olive orchards for oil, and I am pretty good at pickling olives!!!

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-28T08:49:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-7624>

Gemstar, it's really hard to go wrong with kraut if you have a crock with a moat. I have a 7.5Lt Harsh, but there are other brands.

[http://www.amazon.com/Harsch-Gairtopf-Fermenting-Crock-Pot/dp/B000H6PB0Q](http://http://www.amazon.com/Harsch-Gairtopf-Fermenting-Crock-Pot/dp/B000H6PB0Q)

[http://www.amazon.com/Boleslawiec-Polish-Fermenting-Crock-Pot/dp/B006WC0NWK/ref=pd\_sim\_sbs\_k\_1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Boleslawiec-Polish-Fermenting-Crock-Pot/dp/B006WC0NWK/ref=pd_sim_sbs_k_1)

I shred a large cabbage, put it in a plastic tub with 5 tablespoons of salt, wait for it to start sweating (with a bit of 'massage'), then pack it in the crock. Once the lid is on, and the moat is filled with water, there's nothing left to do but wait. Anywhere from 3 to 6 weeks later you have delicious sauerkraut.

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-28T19:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-7804>

Coconut milk kefir sounds to die for. I'm going to have to try that since I'm currently avoiding all dairy.

### rbu5@sssnet.com — 2012-03-30T00:41:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-8493>

> **donkjellberg;7670 wrote:** I have kefir grains ready to go. Any tips on making coconut milk kefir from scratch?

I use the Thai coconut milk but also make coconut 'milk' from scratch that I kefired. It is more economical and works in a pinch if I haven't got any canned available.

Take 2 cups of shredded organic non sweetened coconut, add 3 cups of very warm (but not boiling) water, stir and let set for an hour. Then I put the hydrated coconut through a fine sieve or cheesecloth. Sqeeze out as much of the liquid as possible.

Take the squeezed coconut fibers and add 1 more cup of vw water and repeat above.

Put in fridge overnight and then take off the chunk of 'oil' that hardens on the surface. SAVE this because it is the same thing you buy by the jar when you get Nutiva coconut oil. Eat that when ever--num-num!

Put the milk grains in the coconut milk. (I have never used water grains...think it would kill them). Set on counter and check in 12 to 24 hours depending on warmth of kitchen. When thick refrigerate and enjoy. Make sure to catch it so it doesn't separate alot (over kefired).

I like to add a little vanilla bean in the milk.

Sometimes I add the Thai organic canned milk to it if it isn't as thick and creamy as I like. I do store the grains in between ferments in whole milk. Just rinse them quick in tepid filtered water before adding them to coconut milk.

I re-dehydrate the left over coconut then in an oven with light on overnight to dry. Spread them out thinly on a cookie sheet. Then put in my bullet to grind into coconut flour. That then gets put in freezer for when I make any goodies that call for coconut flour. No waste!

### JoeBranca — 2012-03-30T01:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-8508>

I make it at home using milk kefir grains and Native Forest coconut milk or the Aroy-D brand in the box. Love it. The baby gets some every morning mixed with a couple of raw pastured egg yolks. On a lot of mornings in addition to bacon or meat I'll throw together a little bowl of the kefir and mix in: ceylon cinnamon, cacao powder, cacao nibs for crunch, either a few frozen cranberries or blueberries or rasberries, a couple of raw egg yolks, ginger powder, turmeric, and a dash of vanilla extract. It's completely awesome, I love the loud tartness of it.

### Tizmal — 2013-09-04T17:10:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-94801>

I don't do dairy for about a year and I also make coconut milk kefir and drink coconut milk, its veery useful - here's the article [about the nutritional value of coconut milk](http://juicycoco.com/nutritional-value-of-coconut-milk-vitamins-and-minerals/) and its benefits. I've tried to make my own one because I'm sure its healthier than store-bought commercial milk with preservatives and additives, but unfortunately i like the taste of canned milk more.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-09-04T18:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-94810>

A long time ago I grew milk kefir grains the size of an egg, and I also had water kefir grains (tibi grains). I know that the kefiran gel produced by the milk grains is supposed to be beneficial, though I don't believe the commercial milks have any kefiran in them. Kefir grains can be used to culture coconut and soymilk, for example, but the grains will suffer for it over time. You may need to get new ones or switch them back to real milk from time to time.

If you eat dairy and want to try a "fun" culture, get some villi culture. It forms a thick gelatinous culture overnight at room temperature on the kitchen counter. No heating required,and all it takes is 1 tablespoon of the previous culture stirred into a new bowl of milk and left overnight covered with a cloth. Villi is not sour. Another fun, no-heat type is caspian sea yogurt.

### kathylu — 2013-09-06T19:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cultured-coconut-milk.488/post-95048>

> [GlenPDQ said:](/goto/post?id=94810)
>
> A long time ago I grew milk kefir grains the size of an egg, and I also had water kefir grains (tibi grains). I know that the kefiran gel produced by the milk grains is supposed to be beneficial, though I don't believe the commercial milks have any kefiran in them. Kefir grains can be used to culture coconut and soymilk, for example, but the grains will suffer for it over time. You may need to get new ones or switch them back to real milk from time to time.
>
> If you eat dairy and want to try a "fun" culture, get some villi culture. It forms a thick gelatinous culture overnight at room temperature on the kitchen counter. No heating required,and all it takes is 1 tablespoon of the previous culture stirred into a new bowl of milk and left overnight covered with a cloth. Villi is not sour. Another fun, no-heat type is caspian sea yogurt.

I keep kefir grains in milk. Of course, I don't drink dairy anymore, so my novel use for them is: rinse grains, add them to a quart of RO water, let sit overnight. Then you will have a quart of thick "kefiran" gel, filled with probiotics. I drink a bit of it and use the rest to take a bath in. You heard me right, a bath! Our skin can use a probiotic lift now and then too! Eliminates minor skin problems (itchiness, dryness) and this is subjective, but I think my allergies have improved a bit...less inflammation. Store kefir grains in milk until the next use.

### endless — 2013-09-06T20:20:52+0300

I make coconut milk kefir....I just dump the grains in a jar of coconut milk and leave it on the counter for a couple of days

### Owl — 2012-03-28T07:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/poll-what-is-your-core-temp.489/post-7608>

Core temp is only taken in your esophagus, or anally. Neither is very convenient!

Your basal temp is good enough, taken under your tongue. It'll be a little lower than your core temp.

### Inger — 2012-03-28T08:09:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/poll-what-is-your-core-temp.489/post-7615>

It always was so high as long as I remember and I hate it cause I guess it makes me age faster.

I want it to get lower.. I do hope CT will do it eventually.

I am 35 and do have some wrinkles already. I am healthy though and normal weight so for that I am thankful.

# Thread: talking to teachers about fitness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talking-to-teachers-about-fitness.490/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-28T06:48:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talking-to-teachers-about-fitness.490/post-7598>

Does anyone have specific ideas or experience about talking with teachers (my children are 11 and 7) about fitness? Both my kids are being told that 'cross country' training is beginning (their version of daily PE right now), with my 11 year old having to do long runs daily, building up to a several mile course in a couple of months. I am aware of the information about chronic cardio increasing cortisol and decreasing telomere length, but I've not seen anything specific to children. New Zealand has this huge anti-obesity thing going right now... worried we're going to end up like the US!... and keeping kids 'active' is huge. My kids play sports, walk/scooter to school, walk the dog, etc. But I'm concerned about the move toward 'cross country' running. Thoughts?

### MJ* — 2012-04-18T15:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talking-to-teachers-about-fitness.490/post-16188>

> **KiwiLauren;7636 wrote:** Does anyone have specific ideas or experience about talking with teachers (my children are 11 and 7) about fitness? Both my kids are being told that 'cross country' training is beginning (their version of daily PE right now), with my 11 year old having to do long runs daily, building up to a several mile course in a couple of months. I am aware of the information about chronic cardio increasing cortisol and decreasing telomere length, but I've not seen anything specific to children. New Zealand has this huge anti-obesity thing going right now... worried we're going to end up like the US!... and keeping kids 'active' is huge. My kids play sports, walk/scooter to school, walk the dog, etc. But I'm concerned about the move toward 'cross country' running. Thoughts?

Just saw this...I feel your pain, my kids are very active. I also feel that talking to your children's teachers about this is a good idea, so long as you are prepared to do a ton of educating. In my experience most people do not know what Paleo is let alone telomeres...you may not change their minds all at once so you have to be prepared for that. If you really don't want your kids participating in it, you could try getting your doctor on board and then just have him/her not sign off on this activity for them (maybe discuss with your children's doc first). I do though think that education is key and most teachers are willing to at least listen to what you have to say. They are your children, and it's your right to question what's being asked of them...

The first blog that got me really thinking, and in turn made me do the Leptin-Rx was [http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/](http://http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/)

The 2nd blog-post that really made me up my game was [http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/](http://http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/)

I loved this post because it made me realize how many neolithic thoughts and "truths" I was still hanging onto to!

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-28T07:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-jk-blog-post-changed-your-life.491/post-7613>

I stumbled across Jack when he was in the middle of his Osteoporosis series. My mum has it; spine like an s-bend, two hip replacements, waiting for knee replacements. I knew I had to do something different to what she had done, but didn't know what. I'm at further risk because I don't have a thyroid; Jack got my attention with that one!

What's Leptin?? I had to go back and read his Leptin series, and started the Leptin Rx immediately. It felt as though the more I read of Jack, the more I had to read just to make it all make sense. And it always made sense in the end!

I don't have a favourite blog. What changed my life was the fact that Jack was willing to put it all out there, and I found it.

Also the leaky gut rx explained so much for me. My overall health is okay, but I started to feel those little tiny aches that just don't belong in my body. Most of it is gone. It just takes time to adjust yourself.

Warm, or cool regards,

Petra

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-28T14:05:36+0300

### nzgal — 2012-03-28T19:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7797>

This morning I had 3 eggs, with 5 oz chicken and some vegetables. I think thats a little more than 50 grams protein???

I had a dessertspoon of coconut oil.

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-28T19:50:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7799>

nzgal, are you just starting? If so, you will experience hunger for maybe 1-2 weeks after breakfast. I would be practically starving by 10 a.m. But now I can generally eat lunch at 2-3 p.m. and skip dinner. (I prefer this to skipping lunch and eating dinner.)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-28T19:50:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7800>

> **nzgal;7839 wrote:** This morning I had 3 eggs, with 5 oz chicken and some vegetables. I think thats a little more than 50 grams protein???
>
>
>
> I had a dessertspoon of coconut oil.

I have a couple of Tbsp of CO with my breakfast to keep me full. So maybe you need more. Also you may need to up your protein to keep you satiated. 50-75 gms is OK for breakfast.

Magnesium makes some people sleepy so if you feel sleepy taking it you may have to only take it at night.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-28T20:02:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7809>

Agree with the others, but up your CO slowly! If your gut isn't perfectly healthy, increasing CO can lead to die-off of nappy bacteria & friends, so I'd go up by maybe a teaspoon a day to avoid constant diarrhea.

### Vicster — 2012-03-28T20:51:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7852>

+1 to all of the above. Dr. K says if you're hungry before lunch add more fat and protein at breakfast. You're really not getting that much fat. MamaGrok is right, ease up slowly with the CO as it can have adverse effects if your gut is wacky. You could try GF butter or ghee for a fat source(if you tolerate dairy). Dr K has stated that if you need to you can increase your protein to 75 grams initially until you knock out the hunger.

Hang in there, the benefits are so worth it!

### Jim — 2012-03-28T20:59:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7853>

> **nzgal;7761 wrote:** This is day one for me.
>
>
>
> I'm already dealing with hunger after two hours of eating breakfast.
>
> I heard Magnesium can really help with this???
>
>
>
> What kind of magnesium should I take??? Is Magnesium Citrate okay??? That's what I have.
>
> 400mg right???
>
>
>
> Thanks.

I use Magesium Malate about 1000 mg a few hours before bed. I have also started adding Epsom Salts into my CT bath water. Be careful with the magnesium though, when you get too much it can cause loose stools. The same with the CO. Start slowly and see how you do before you ramp up or you are liable to incapacitate yourself for a time and do serious damage to your toilet paper inventory.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-29T01:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-7995>

NZGAL,

> **Vicster;7894 wrote:** +1 to all of the above. Dr. K says if you're hungry before lunch add more fat and protein at breakfast. You're really not getting that much fat. MamaGrok is right, ease up slowly with the CO as it can have adverse effects if your gut is wacky. You could try GF butter or ghee for a fat source(if you tolerate dairy). Dr K has stated that if you need to you can increase your protein to 75 grams initially until you knock out the hunger.
>
>
>
> Hang in there, the benefits are so worth it!

Agree. I'd add that chicken, for me anyway, does not kill the hunger like meat from a grass-fed ruminant. My MO for BAB is to cook a giant roast in the crock pot on Sunday and then I use that meat for the next 3-4 days as the protein for my BAB with some eggs usually. At the beginning of the Leptin Rx, some people really need to stuff themselves at breakfast to kill the hunger. It will be tamed eventually. At least it did for me, and I was a total 90-120minute-insulin-rollercoaster-carbcrazed-snackmonster prior to my quest for optimal.

Good luck.

### nzgal — 2012-03-29T02:21:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.494/post-8017>

Thanks guys!!!

Yeah today is my first day!!! I went to Leptin Reset because I wasn't losing and still dealing with cravings on primal.

I have had some hunger all day but am going to try upping the fats and taking the magnesium.

Thanks for all your help!!!!

# Thread: How low - fasting blood glucose ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-03-28T17:22:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-7731>

Since CT my FBG is down to 60 and after eating average of 72. Is this too low? I been having some dizziness in the mornings and wake up very thirsty. Not sure if that is related to FBG.

Before CT my FBG was always around 100.

### healthnut — 2012-03-29T05:08:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-8102>

That's pretty low....wow!

### primat — 2012-03-29T15:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-8226>

A lot more stable after starting CT

(I'm on the Leptin Rx)

Fasting usually 65-75 (06am),

After breakfast is around 90. I usually don't bother measuring it since the strips are expensive and glucose doesn't seem to peak at all. Eating 60-70g protein, 60-100g fat and max 10g carb

### Adria — 2012-03-29T16:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-8244>

Today mine was 64 @1hr after breakfast.

### healthnut — 2012-03-29T17:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-8269>

You guys amaze me, I am just hoping to get FBG down below 100. (diabetic here, w/dawn phenomenon) Was 105 this morning, the 1 hour post prandial was 82.

I ran lower BG's during bodybuilding competition & my body adjusted to the lower readings after a while. At first I felt shaky/cold if I fell below 70, but the body kind of adjusts to lower ranges. Whenever I would hit 50's, I could feel I was going too low....my whole body would shake and lips would go blue.

### Adria — 2012-03-30T15:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-low-fasting-blood-glucose.495/post-8715>

FGB is still @ 60. The thirst and dizziness I had earlier this week is now gone.

# Thread: Dr. K - help us manage electrolytes while going keto
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-03-28T17:56:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-7742>

I \*think\* electrolytes are behind a lot of weird stuff I'm seeing right now. And I bet a lot of us could use some general guidance on managing them while doing a ketogenic diet. I know Taubes mentions in WWGF that one of the biggest difficulties when you first go keto is electrolyte loss, resulting in orthostatic hypotension until you replace them.

I'm looking for broad principles we can all use, and then we on the forum can help each other apply the principles to our specific cases.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-28T17:58:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-7744>

I'll start with my specific case!

I never ever had edema except during first pregnancy, where I had pitting edema - started eating more salt and tailor-sitting and it went away.

Starting last summer, a year after going low-carb paleo, and right after starting the LR (but still struggling with bingeing every couple of weeks), I had **pitting edema. It was usually just one leg at a time, and came and went over a month or two. Then it disappeared with the fall.

I continued to have low BP as I always have, and to battle water retention on the body every now and then, but not pitting.

**I just got it again last week (without any change in diet; been ketogenic for quite a while now) - first both my calves looked very puffy, then the pitting started. Mostly the left leg. Then they returned to their normal measurements, and then the pitting gradually went away. AND THEN, I got woozy. Pulse ranged from 96 sitting to 130 standing, for several days, and I got weaker and weaker muscles**, with shaking arms and hardly able to sing without wavering.

I removed the 2 caps of Thyrogold I was taking, in case it was hyperthyroid, and added some potassium chloride (I already use lots of real salt & magnesium). Improvement was complete, but gradual, over two days. I added thyrogold back again, in a lesser amount.

Then two days later, the pitting edema started again! I don't want to continue this cycle of flushing out the water retention, losing all my electrolytes, and having to crank up the potassium, sodium, & magnesium over and over again.**

I need some prevention tips! I'm concerned about supplementing potassium (not getting enough through diet?) - enough, but not too much.

I'd love any clues to help me prevent the pitting edema (what is causing it?) and subsequent (it seems) loss of electrolytes.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-29T15:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8213>

Anyone else having issues with electrolytes? Anyone? Bueller?

edema is slightly down again today and guess what ... pounding pulse when I stood up this morning (even though it was

### Vicster — 2012-03-29T15:29:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8223>

I just started having issues as well. Swelling of the hands/ankles and severe leg cramps at night. I've been keto for about 4 weeks now and I keep my diet very tight so I don't think it's a food. I supplement with magnesium and potassium and use salt liberally on my food, but I don't know if all that is in the right amount and I know it's a delicate balance. I'm curious to see if others will chime in and give us some insight.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-29T15:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8232>

RIght now I'm doing 800mg Mg Glycinate in the afternoon/evening, ~500mg potassium chloride in the morning, and 2-3tsp real unrefined salt in the morning + salted food throughout the day.

I have no idea if this is reasonable or not. I do know that my twitches and cramps hardly make an appearance anymore, so I think I'm good on the magnesium front.

### Vicster — 2012-03-29T16:09:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8239>

I'm taking Mg Citrate in the am and Natural Calm in the evening(makes me sleep like a log!) but now I remember Dr K saying that if you have gut issues Glycinate is the better form to take. Maybe I'm not absorbing the Mg that I need? I take the same amount of K and slightly less salt as you.

Just when I think I have a plan and realize I'm on the wrong one!!  I'm going to keeping reading and adjusting because I am bound and determined to feel better!! Thanks for your insight.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-29T16:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8247>

Natural Calm also got rid of my cramps, but it was always a dance between getting enough and overdoing and getting diarrhea. Apparently Mg Glyc is less likely to give the runs.

### shilohman — 2012-03-29T19:00:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8338>

I dilute epson salt in a spray bottle and then at night spray my legs and torso. Epson salts has magnesium chloride. Somewhere I read that when applied topically your body will absorb only what it needs. Of course I believe every thing I read

.

### ColdBren — 2012-03-29T19:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8340>

I think my palpitations might be caused by electrolyte changes too. Not sure how to balance them on Keto

### BJK77 — 2012-03-29T21:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8429>

I think I'm dealing with an electrolyte imbalance here as well. I LOVE salt and probably overdo it with each meal. I've had my K levels tested twice in the last year and they are in range, but in the lower end of the range. I do get swelling in my lower extremities and major muscle weakness that seems to be attributable to K deficiency.

I just started supplementing with K, but only very small amounts. I prefer to get my nutrients from food and have also added in a daily avocado. I do notice a slight improvement when my meals include the avo. I track my food in my fitness pal on my phone and have noticed my sodium is always high and my potassium is low and that doesn't even include my extra teaspoon of sea salt with each meal!

Wish I could supplement with Mg too since I know I'm very deficient in it, but it always gives me kidney pain and then I end up with black stools . . . no one has ever been able to explain what causes this and I've tried all forms of it ??

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-29T21:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8434>

Can excess sodium actually deplete your potassium, or is it just a matter of not enough potassium? I think I remember perhaps that too much sodium will cause your body to send fluids out to rid it, and the potassium goes out along with it?

My heart beat is calmer now, \*and\* the pitting edema is down still, so crisis averted for now. I need to know what's happening so I can deal with it, and more importantly, prevent it, in the future.

I'll have a potassium lab added to my labs I'm getting in the next couple of days.

I can't have an avocado wah wah wah! Most of the potassium foods are FODMAPs (bloating) or fruits (cravings/non-seasonal) or MEAT (which I'm already pretty much maxed out on).

Have you tried magnesium oil on the skin?

### BJK77 — 2012-03-30T19:54:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-8857>

It's my understanding based on the reading I've been doing lately that excess sodium can deplete potassium. I'm assuming my love of salt is the culprit in my case.

I had a little "incident" this morning after my BAB where I probably salted a bit too much and didn't have my avocado. I don't get the palpitations that I've read others experience, but I get a super shakey feeling inside and just feel "off." Hard to describe, but I ended up drinking a few ounces of coconut water and seemed fine then. I just don't like to rely too much on the coc water though because of the higher sugar/carb content.

I have done mag on the skin and I don't think I have any negative reaction to it that way, but it burns like something fierce! I've read that's just further proof that I need it. I used to take nightly epsom salt baths, but of course they were hot detox baths, so I stopped that since CT'ing and ended up forgetting about the mag oil I have. I suppose I'll have to remember to go back to using that again.

### tweety — 2012-03-31T01:59:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-9027>

Hmmm. I have been able to successfully get rid of heart palpitations with magnesium glycinate. But after a year of dosing that well I am now having cramping in my calf muscles. Its subsiding, and I can't help but think the CT may have been throwing off my balances. Its improving now, so maybe I am rebalancing? Don't know.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-06T17:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-12159>

On another thread it was being discussed how palpitations can be related to nocturnal cortisol issues... In the context of the posts it was waking at night with pounding heart etc. are your palpitations during the day after eating? I get that every so often too...

### quelsen — 2012-04-06T18:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-12176>

what about heavy metals?

i personally found that my electrolites didnt seem to change as i went keto. but i did need to up my salt.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-06T18:50:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-12180>

When I had them, it was usually worst in the morning on rising, and either went away, or didn't, lol, during the day. Stayed at a higher level at night, but not as high.

I've been taking potassium for two weeks now, with no return of palpitations. I've also been off thyrogold again, till yesterday. Starting back at a very low level and slowly moving up (again) while taking the potassium and continuing salt & magnesium to see what I can see.

### Petra — 2012-04-09T12:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-12929>

isn't coconut water fixing this issue?

### Glamorama — 2012-04-09T12:44:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-12930>

> **Petra;12998 wrote:** isn't coconut water fixing this issue?

I drink coconut water 3-4 times weekly and haven't had any issues. In fact, prior to introducing coconut water, I used to often feel dizzy, especially in the afternoons.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-09T19:50:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-13040>

Coconut water didn't help me at all, and just gave me the usual woozy feeling of having too many carbs. BUT - it would be easy for me to imagine that if you're close to the right balance of potassium, that coconut water would be enough to keep you there.

I think it's possible I was just too far in the hole for that to help. 1/4tsp of "lite salt" (curse the processed crap that it is) does it for me without the wooze, at least for now.

I still don't know if electrolytes were the source of the problems I had two weeks ago, but am still working on managing it.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-06-29T19:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-34389>

> **MamaGrok;7785 wrote: I never ever had edema except during first pregnancy, where I had pitting edema - started eating more salt and tailor-sitting and it went away.
>
>
>
> Starting last summer, a year after going low-carb paleo, and right after starting the LR (but still struggling with bingeing every couple of weeks), I had pitting edema. It was usually just one leg at a time, and came and went over a month or two. Then it disappeared with the fall.**
>
>
>
> I'd love any clues to help me prevent the pitting edema (what is causing it?) and subsequent (it seems) loss of electrolytes.

I thought I'd restart this thread. I've been eating paleo since April 23 and doing CT 4 to 6 days per week. I had been experiencing pitting edema in my left ankle at various times throughout the past 2 years, for which I could never figure out the cause, although I noticed that my chair at work would cause my left leg to feel prickley, like it was falling asleep. After going paleo, it seemed like any excess edema was gone, and I thought I was good, but now I've noticed that it begins again right before the start of my menstrual cycle. Did you ever figure out whether this was attributed to a real electrolyte issue or something else? For 3 menstrual cycles I've noticed this, and it does go away a few days after the start. Just curious if anyone else got this figured out.

### freshveggies — 2012-06-29T19:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-34405>

I have issue too. I was looking into this progduct. You taking them according to the taste. If they don't taste good, you don't take them. [http://www.bodybio.com/storecategory110.aspx](http://http://www.bodybio.com/storecategory110.aspx)

### Michael — 2012-06-30T19:54:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-34634>

> **Petra;12998 wrote:** isn't coconut water fixing this issue?

Interesting comment.

There's an old blog post of Mike Eades' where he says WRT to cramps that playing around with different electrolytes can help, but that truth to tell just a little carbohydrate can make 'em go away.

A 225 ml glass of coconut water provides, IIRC, provides about 20 grams of carbohydrate. That's quite a belt of carbs. Maybe that's why it helps.

Not joining the high-carb regiment! Just wondering if a few more are temporarily helpful for some people on occasion.

### MamaGrok — 2012-07-06T22:05:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-k-help-us-manage-electrolytes-while-going-keto.497/post-35935>

No solutions here yet. Taking daily potassium \*seems\* to have helped with the pulse stuff, but IDK why it started in the first place. Pitting edema still comes and goes in left leg.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-06T22:13:04+0300

# Thread: Hack My Labs, Please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-please.498/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Owl — 2012-03-28T18:45:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-please.498/post-7763>

I would really appreciate some help on my labs. Any ideas are appreciated.

Some background â€“

Female, 53 years old, 5â€™7â€, 134lb. Lost 75lb on hcg 2 years ago, regained 15lb, started Leptin Reset last July, lost 13lb. I have been eating post leptin rx for 2 months.

I have asthma but am not on any meds right now, allergies to many trees and pollen, gluten sensitive (gluten free for 1.5 yr.), hypothyroid, leaky gut, and have had mild gastro problems for about 2 years â€“ loose stools. Eating paleo has not helped, so I decided to have more blood work and the Metametrix GI-Effects test done, with results below. Iâ€™ve got a doctorâ€™s appt. next week, and would like to have some of this figured out before then.

Thyroid : I am on a total of 30mcg of T3 and 1 Â½ grains NatureThroid tablets per day, split morning and afternoon.

Last 3 test results in Nov, Dec, and most recent, March 8, 2012:

TSH \_\_.006 , .006, .006 (.450-4.5)

FT3 \_\_6.0, 3.3, 4.1 (2.0-4.4)

FT4 \_\_1.42, 0.73, 0.78 (0.82-1.77)

RT3 \_\_344, 201, 134 (90-350)

Two problems â€“ high lactobacillus â€“ â€œOvergrowth of Lactobacillus sp. could produce D-lactic

aciduria in those with short bowel syndrome. Limit intake of simple carbohydrates.â€ I donâ€™t think I have this. I attribute this to the probiotics - maybe I didnâ€™t need to supplement this one, and now itâ€™s too high.

And Prevotella â€“ â€œIf Prevotella sp. is in the 5th quintile suspect possible oral/

But I do have a non-human parasite. I have had loose stools for about 2 years.

â€œThe DNA probe identified kingdom protozoan, but genus and

species probes for known human pathogens were negative.

Suspect that the protozoan identified is likely NOT a human

pathogen, and probably a transient, non-colonizer of the

human GI. Evaluate patient symptoms and inflammatory

markers on the GI Effects test. If symptoms are consistent

with a parasite infection, consider treatment.

Treatment Options:

Address other abnormal results in the GI Effects test first,

with the expectation that a rare parasite will be crowded out

when healthy conditions are restored.

â€¢ Consider exposures such as pets, sushi, camping, or

foreign travel

â€¢ If presentation is consistent with parasite infestation, use

a broad spectrum antiparasitic treatment followed by pre and

probiotics

High cholesterol could mean, â€œIf elevated cholesterol, suspect malabsorption, high

dietary intake or increased mucosal cell turnover resulting from inflammation.â€

So in conclusion, it looks like Iâ€™ve got to investigate my gallbladder, liver, pancreas, B12, and parasites.

Iâ€™d really like to hear any other opinions. Thanks!

with a parasite infection, consider treatment.

with the expectation that a rare parasite will be crowded out

when healthy conditions are restored.

• Consider exposures such as pets, sushi, camping, or

foreign travel

• If presentation is consistent with parasite infestation, use

a broad spectrum antiparasitic treatment followed by pre and

probiotics

• Botanicals\* “

Page 3 - Acetate is high, Propionate is low. I’m not sure what these mean. It looks like I’m still getting gluten from somewhere.

Page 4 – Elastase is somewhat low, so I started taking pancreatic enzymes a few days ago. This could be from pancreatitis or gallstones.

High cholesterol could mean, “If elevated cholesterol, suspect malabsorption, high

dietary intake or increased mucosal cell turnover resulting from inflammation.”

### Dextery — 2012-03-31T22:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-please.498/post-9357>

Owl,

Looking at your T3-reverse T3 ratio....according to [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) page you need to divide the T3 by the Reverse T3 to get your ratio. You have a FT3 level of 4.1 and a rt3 level of 134. So divide 4.1 by 134 and you get a ratio of .03 This is assuming the units are the same.

let us know if the units for Ft3 and rt3 are the same.

# Thread: How was your CT today?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2388
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### differentstory — 2012-03-28T19:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7782>

Can we have a monster thread on CT? I wanted a thread where I could just post the little tweaks I am making from day to day regarding my CT protocol and also see others' progress, so hopefully this thread will accomplish that. There are so many of you that I try to follow but we are posting on lots of different threads and it's hard to keep up!

Just wanted to say too that I find am so happy we have this group!! We are an amazing group of people!!

How are you guys doing??

### differentstory — 2012-03-28T19:19:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7784>

Cold-Adapted? Yes

CT Baths or Icing or Both? Baths

Duration? 60 minutes 5-6 times a week

Temperature? 53-55 Degrees

When do you CT? At night, before bed (8pm-9pm)

How long have you been doing CT? Experimenting 4 weeks; CT baths for 2 solid weeks

Last week I found that if I moved my limbs around in the water, I would get very cold, but if I stayed totally still, I was fine. Last night, for the first time, I noticed I could move my legs back and forth 3-4 inches and still be warm. I keep my arms and hands out of the water - I tried putting them in but found they would get an achy feeling and plus I am often reading a book, so I need use of my hands. Currently, the water level only goes up to my mid-breast.

I am CT'ing at night since I work during the day. I use booties on my feet because they are just getting too darned cold.

I made three changes last night that were huge successes for me:

1\. I filled up the bath before getting in. Previously, I was getting in and filling up the tub ("croak soak") which was good in the beginning, but now that I am cold-adapted I find that the little cold water splashes (while the tub fills up) and the noise of the water irritate the heck out of me. So, last night I turned on the water, left the room while it filled, came back and took the plunge. I was SO nervous! But I just started counting up as soon as I got in and found after 43 seconds, I was completely fine. YAY me! A MILLION TIMES BETTER THAN BEFORE!

2\. I've been fighting to get the water temp down, so finally last night I used a gallon milk jug filled with water that I had frozen solid. This seem to get the water down 2 degrees. I didn't even notice a difference in the water temp (yay me!!). I'm probably going to need 3 jugs though to get to the 50 degree mark. I'm hovering at 53-55 right now. (Fortunately, I do have a big whirlpool tub - funny though, it was used maybe twice in the past year and now I've used it about 15 times in the past two weeks. HA!) We also have well water and I'm starting to wonder if I'm going to run our well dry. Guess I'll have to ask DH.

3\. Sadly, unlike others, it is taking me a good 6+ hours to warm up and that is the miserable part of CT for me. I am not in good shape. My starting weight was 203 (4 weeks ago). I am down to 196. I cannot exercise for very long at all without being exhausted (maybe 5 minutes??). my 6+ hour warm-ups are turning my 1-hour CT baths into 7-hour sessions of misery and that is making me dread CT. So, to keep me going, I have decided to use "modern tools" to help if I am still cold after an hour of natural warm-up. Last night was the first night I did this and I was SOOOOOOOOOO relieved. I ended up wrapping myself in an electric blanket after thawing for an hour. Even with the blanket, I didn't fully warm up until 3:00am! I'm going to try and track this and hope it gets better.

### David — 2012-03-28T19:27:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7787>

Cold-Adapted? Doubtful

CT Baths or Icing or Both? Showers and body icing

Duration? shower 30 minutes daily; icing 45 minutes last five days

Temperature? shower measures 63 Degrees F

When do you CT? shower 9-10 AM, icing evenings (7pm-8pm)

How long have you been doing CT? 3 weeks (started with face dunks)

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-03-28T19:58:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7806>

> **differentstory;7826 wrote:** 1. I filled up the bath before getting in. Previously, I was getting in and filling up the tub ("croak soak") which was good in the beginning, but now that I am cold-adapted I find that the little cold water splashes (while the tub fills up) and the noise of the water irritate the heck out of me. So, last night I turned on the water, left the room while it filled, came back and took the plunge. I was SO nervous! But I just started counting up as soon as I got in and found after 43 seconds, I was completely fine. YAY me! A MILLION TIMES BETTER THAN BEFORE!
>
>
>
> 3\. Sadly, unlike others, it is taking me a good 6+ hours to warm up and that is the miserable part of CT for me. I am not in good shape. My starting weight was 203 (4 weeks ago). I am down to 196. I cannot exercise for very long at all without being exhausted (maybe 5 minutes??). my 6+ hour warm-ups are turning my 1-hour CT baths into 7-hour sessions of misery and that is making me dread CT. So, to keep me going, I have decided to use "modern tools" to help if I am still cold after an hour of natural warm-up. Last night was the first night I did this and I was SOOOOOOOOOO relieved. I ended up wrapping myself in an electric blanket after thawing for an hour. Even with the blanket, I didn't fully warm up until 3:00am! I'm going to try and track this and hope it gets better.

HI Different Story! Congrats on being able to get right in...OMG, I still have a hard time. AND YAY on the weight loss!! I did things differently last night as well and did my CT earlier. I really had to talk myself into 40 minutes at 55-60. I was so cold and at the end I started to shiver, which hasn't really happened before. Afterward I was shivering so hard that I was thinking I must be getting a great ab workout! LOL But here is my confusion...do you want to shiver and stay cold after CT? I guess I don't understand if the benefit of CT is just the time in the tub or the time after the tub? I, too, want to warm up asap. So, last night I bared through being super cold (mostly because my house is only about 55 degrees) for IDK, an hour or more and then went to bed under blankets with the room at 66 degrees. In the middle of the night I woke up hot. So, I am wondering if it is less-optimal to help yourself warm up?

Also, I want to caution you about sleeping with an electric blanket. I did so for YEARS and after reading Dr K's CT7 blog, I realized that I was really messing with the whole hormonal processing that happens at night. I also realized that the blanket has probably been causing me to become more sensitive to cold. I asked Dr K about it, saying "the electric blanket hasn't really been helping me" and he responded "no, it actually has been hurting you." SO, I have taken it off my bed for good!

### vkiernan — 2012-03-28T20:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7819>

Cold-Adapted? I don't think so but man I want to be so bad cuz I can't stand the cold.

CT Baths or Icing or Both? Baths -Croak method but I don't do the hot, just slowly fill, might have to try your method, fill first.

Duration? Just worked up to 45 min (up to ribs only) 30 min (upper body too)

Temperature? 50 degrees -well water

When do you CT? Was doing morning but since I need more than 30 min I am doing them at night right after I eat.

How long have you been doing CT? This is my fifth week

Pounds lost? One

I am also drinking more cold water, dropping the temp in vehicle and wearing lighter clothes to work.

I have adrenal fatigue (OAT), Candida and hormone issues to fix. Here's to hoping that this is the magic pill along with LRx!!!

Nice thread by the way. Thanks for starting it.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-28T20:12:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7822>

Cold-Adapted? getting there - I am warming up more easily every time I do CT

CT Baths or Icing or Both? Baths

Duration? 45 minutes every day

Temperature? Straight out of tap, probably around 48-50 (haven't measured recently)

When do you CT? Varies, sometimes mid-afternoon, other times after dinner

How long have you been doing CT? 21 days of baths, a week of face dunks beforehand

I started with neoprene booties but traded those for wool socks a few days ago with no problems. Putting my arms in tends to make me cold but I want to get to as much full immersion as I can, especially on the upper back where I hear there should be BAT. Yesterday I took a cool shower first, then filled the tub with cold water and I lay back in it and let it get to full depth. I shivered intermittently throughout but never shook too badly. Also yesterday I ditched the compression wear and noticed my body got a bit pinker than usual. I am also wearing wool socks on my hands to keep them warmer and am not yet putting them in the water.

### hellojtm — 2012-03-28T20:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7830>

I am very pleased with my CT today:

Cold-adapted: no

Baths:15-25 minutes, 4 total so far

Temp 50-53f San Francisco (Hetch Hetchy) tap water.

I'm pleased because I had completely talked myself out of doing it, because I didn't wanna.. but I tricked myself by just filling the tub anyway and then thought well I will just do 5 minutes to give me a head start on tonite where I will really do it. Ended up doing 20 minutes of which the majority was full torso coverage. The legs are easy now, I could have done them indefinitely I feel, but had some work pressure so I hopped out. Shivered the whole time, shivered for about an hour afterwards. Had a rare opportunity to drive for 15 minutes in the morning after that so I cranked the a/c... Felt colder than the bath! For those air adapters I would suggest fast moving air. My arms and legs both turned red in response. And the 53f rainy day that greeted me felt warm....

Jesse

### BJK77 — 2012-03-28T22:02:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-7888>

> **differentstory;7826 wrote: Cold-Adapted? Yes
>
>
>
> 1\. I filled up the bath before getting in. Previously, I was getting in and filling up the tub ("croak soak") which was good in the beginning, but now that I am cold-adapted I find that the little cold water splashes (while the tub fills up) and the noise of the water irritate the heck out of me. So, last night I turned on the water, left the room while it filled, came back and took the plunge. I was SO nervous! But I just started counting up as soon as I got in and found after 43 seconds, I was completely fine. YAY me! A MILLION TIMES BETTER THAN BEFORE!
>
>
>
> We also have well water and I'm starting to wonder if I'm going to run our well dry. Guess I'll have to ask DH.
>
>
>
> 3\. Sadly, unlike others, it is taking me a good 6+ hours to warm up and that is the miserable part of CT for me. I am not in good shape. My starting weight was 203 (4 weeks ago). I am down to 196. I cannot exercise for very long at all without being exhausted (maybe 5 minutes??). my 6+ hour warm-ups are turning my 1-hour CT baths into 7-hour sessions of misery and that is making me dread CT. So, to keep me going, I have decided to use "modern tools" to help if I am still cold after an hour of natural warm-up. Last night was the first night I did this and I was SOOOOOOOOOO relieved. I ended up wrapping myself in an electric blanket after thawing for an hour. Even with the blanket, I didn't fully warm up until 3:00am! I'm going to try and track this and hope it gets better.
>
>
> How do we know that we are cold adapted? I assumed the cold water would not bother us anymore AND we would warm up much faster after the bath, but really what are the signs? I must have missed that part of the post.
>
>
>
> I started out with the "croak soak" too and did it maybe 2-3 times, but now I just fill the tub and plop right in. It takes _forever_ for my tub to fill up so it was excruciating feeling every little inch creep up on me! It's so much easier to just drop in and get it over with.
>
>
>
> I worry about our well going dry too! My tub holds a lot of water and I'm trying to do the baths 5-6 times a week so we're talking a good amount. DH doesn't seem too concerned though.
>
>
>
> Jennifer;7848 wrote:** do you want to shiver and stay cold after CT? I guess I don't understand if the benefit of CT is just the time in the tub or the time after the tub? I, too, want to warm up asap.
>
>
>
> So, I am wondering if it is less-optimal to help yourself warm up?

I've been wondering about all of this too. Wish we could get some more guidance from Dr. K. I'd like to know that I'm not doing anything that is inadvertently decreasing the benefit I'm trying to obtain here . . . certainly don't want to freeze my tushy for nothing
