### Toni1675 — 2012-04-12T18:50:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-14265>

> **Jennifer;7848 wrote:** HI Different Story! Congrats on being able to get right in...OMG, I still have a hard time. AND YAY on the weight loss!! I did things differently last night as well and did my CT earlier. I really had to talk myself into 40 minutes at 55-60. I was so cold and at the end I started to shiver, which hasn't really happened before. Afterward I was shivering so hard that I was thinking I must be getting a great ab workout! LOL But here is my confusion...do you want to shiver and stay cold after CT? I guess I don't understand if the benefit of CT is just the time in the tub or the time after the tub? I, too, want to warm up asap. So, last night I bared through being super cold (mostly because my house is only about 55 degrees) for IDK, an hour or more and then went to bed under blankets with the room at 66 degrees. In the middle of the night I woke up hot. So, I am wondering if it is less-optimal to help yourself warm up?
>
> Also, I want to caution you about sleeping with an electric blanket. I did so for YEARS and after reading Dr K's CT7 blog, I realized that I was really messing with the whole hormonal processing that happens at night. I also realized that the blanket has probably been causing me to become more sensitive to cold. I asked Dr K about it, saying "the electric blanket hasn't really been helping me" and he responded "no, it actually has been hurting you." SO, I have taken it off my bed for good!

Hi there I have been doing CT for 7 weeks now with full submersion at 50 degree water for 45 min plus everytime. In the beginning it was terrible afterwards it always took like 2 hours to warm up. At the time I was doing same thing putting on lots of clothes and blankets to help, but what I have found it actually makes it worse for me. I found that the less clothes I wear after CT the faster I warm up. I no longer shiver after CT and I crave my daily cold baths!!

I did not have time for breakfast, as I overslept and rushed to work. got back and filled up my late tub. it was at 59F .I added a bowl of ice and got it down to 58F. sat in it for 35 min submerged to chest. I never shivered before. this is my first CT without a high fat meal prior. i wonder if there is a relationship? still shivering after 20 min.

### AKMan — 2012-04-16T05:25:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15500>

Tried something different today. Summer in the Arctic comes on fast! Today it got up to 60, last week it was -10. The snow is melting fast, and too slushy for barefoot walks in the snow as I've been doing the past few weeks.

I filled my bathtub with 50 degree water (comes out of the tap at 39 degrees!). All I can say to you that have been doing this--WOW! 50 degrees is friggin' cold. Anyway, I got in, didn't have any trouble adjusting, and settled in up to my armpits. Stayed 20 minutes. The only thing I found painful was my ankles, I had to lift them out of the water a couple times. Shrinkage was unbelievable!

After I got out, I air-dried sitting in the bright sun for an hour.

Here's the funny thing - I didn't shiver at all, my skin was bright cherry red, and none of my fingers or toes went numb.

Recently, I set my outdoor hottub to 70 degrees. Sitting in that with the air temp at 30 degrees, I will start to shiver almost immediately. Being indoors with air temp in the 70's didn't make me shiver, even though the water was 20 degrees cooler. Air temp must be a big factor as we lose so much heat through our head.

### Birdy — 2012-04-16T06:14:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15511>

This weekend I went for long walks in the morning cold. Yesterday it was in the 30's, and I wore regular clothing and a vest with gloves. By the afternoon it had warmed up a bit, but the wind was fierce. Today it was in the 40's, and I just wore gloves with regular indoor clothing. By early afternoon I wished I had brought a tank top.

### Mei — 2012-04-16T06:25:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15513>

I'm tired of the tub as it's so small that staying submerged requires me to wedge myself against the walls, creating and maintaining tension in most of my body for the full tub time. I find that it interferes with the calm state of mind I need in order to accept and enjoy the cold, instead of feeling like I'm fighting against it (I like showers for this reason - I can move, I'm standing, and I can relax completely). This state of affairs, in turn, makes recovery really \*\*\*\*ty - as many have mentioned, I can be shivery, exhausted, and kind of stupid for hours on end after a long tub session. SO to deal with this, and also to fulfill my intense hunger for nature which has only seemed to increase since paleo/CT type activities have taken over my life, I've been jumping in every river or lake I can find (I drive all over the place for work). I think at this time of year around here they're around 6 - 8 degrees C, maybe colder so.... 40 - 46, I guess, for all y'all Americans. I'm not staying in as long but finding the detox to be way more intense, the recovery virtually unnoticeable, and the benefits immediately noticeable.

how's this for a CT session: ride horse bareback at sunset to river. get naked. get in river (horse is too much of a weenie, leave tied to tree). splash around for as long as you like. get dry-ish. take ages to get tight pants back on cold, damp skin while horse stares at you like you're insane (maybe you are). ride back at a brisk canter just as it's getting dark. feel very smug. drive home with the windows down and the A/C on.

Or today: jumping in at a glacial river headwaters while a group of unsuspecting hikers gasps and claps.

Way more fun than tub-time. Just sayin'.

### Mei — 2012-04-16T06:27:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15514>

@AKMan ... I hear you about the ankles...they used to throb/seize up to the point that I was CTing in a V-shape trying to keep them out of the water!

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-16T06:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15515>

I've slowly lowered my bath temp down and now for the last week I've been @ 50-55 deg. for 40-50 minutes. Well, today I bought a 150 gallon rubbermaid tub and put it outside and filled with the hose. Odd thing was how straight cold from the inside tap is 50 deg. but from the outside tap it was 44! Boy, did those extra few degrees make a huge difference, especially combined with 50 deg. air temp, a breeze and it was raining on me. I only lasted 10 minutes before my skin was bright red, my toes weren't moving so well and I started to feel stabbing pains. I've never had to give up so fast! However, recovery was super fast. I think I didn't have enough time in the tub to make a dent in my core temp.

### quelsen — 2012-04-16T16:05:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15555>

Lucked Out.

JK.... I found a local guy selling a chipped ice maker form 750$. 400 pounds of ice in a little over 24 hours we got two baths out of it yesterday and one this morning. in addition to the cost of the equipment i needed another 50$ to tie it into the water and drain it out of the garage.

Thank god as Sunday morning my back was totally out. i could not stand and had to crawl into the bathroom and the tub. Thanks god for the SO who ferried 3 5gallon buckets of ice into the tub. Took 40 minutes to dull the pain and interestingly my core temp didnt drop as much as usual.

that got me semi mobile. then took another bath in the afternoon after re-plumbing the freezer. Woke this morning to a chest full of ice and got another 3 buckets for my morning bath.

Before enlightenment - chop wood, carry (ice)water. After enlightenment - chop wood, carry (ice)water. ~Zen Buddhist Proverb

### Susan M. — 2012-04-16T20:17:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15647>

I have been taking 40 minute 53-54 degree soaks for about four weeks now. I was taking one every day, but since going on a four day vacation I have had trouble getting back in sync with CT and with my diet. I think I am finally back on track. After each of my soaks I have always had toes go numb after about ten minute and stay numb for about an hour. Yesterday was a little different, however. When I got out of the tub my toes were numb much sooner than usual. I looked at the very bottom of my feet and was surprised to see that while most of the bottom of my feet were a light cherry color, I also had rather large blotches of yellowish/white spots. I have never noticed these before. They went away eventually with a lot of massaging on my part. It took about two hours for my feet to return to normal. I make sure that I eat a fat-filled snack and drink a quart of ice water before getting in the tub. Any ideas anyone?

### vkiernan — 2012-04-16T20:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15658>

> **Susan M.;15717 wrote:** I have been taking 40 minute 53-54 degree soaks for about four weeks now. I was taking one every day, but since going on a four day vacation I have had trouble getting back in sync with CT and with my diet. I think I am finally back on track. After each of my soaks I have always had toes go numb after about ten minute and stay numb for about an hour. Yesterday was a little different, however. When I got out of the tub my toes were numb much sooner than usual. I looked at the very bottom of my feet and was surprised to see that while most of the bottom of my feet were a light cherry color, I also had rather large blotches of yellowish/white spots. I have never noticed these before. They went away eventually with a lot of massaging on my part. It took about two hours for my feet to return to normal. I make sure that I eat a fat-filled snack and drink a quart of ice water before getting in the tub. Any ideas anyone?

I am doing what you are doing for time and temp and my feet turn the same way. Kinda weird looking but I don't worry about it. My toes no longer go numb though although sometimes it's a bit hard to walk. The toes only did that for about a week. I have been doing this for 7 or 8 weeks. Lost track. Will have to look it up again. Maybe instead of a snack you should eat your meal then do CT. Is your snack a meal replacement? You might need more than you are eating. I always do this within an hour of my last meal if possible as I don't have a lot of fat so I need to do it prime time or I will pay for sure.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-16T21:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15660>

It's colder and way more rain today and I'm not feeling particularly motivated to do today's bath even though my body aches (heavy gardening and post hold digging all this week), even though I usually crave it.

### Susan M. — 2012-04-16T22:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15681>

> **vkiernan;15728 wrote:** I am doing what you are doing for time and temp and my feet turn the same way. Kinda weird looking but I don't worry about it. My toes no longer go numb though although sometimes it's a bit hard to walk. The toes only did that for about a week. I have been doing this for 7 or 8 weeks. Lost track. Will have to look it up again. Maybe instead of a snack you should eat your meal then do CT. Is your snack a meal replacement? You might need more than you are eating. I always do this within an hour of my last meal if possible as I don't have a lot of fat so I need to do it prime time or I will pay for sure.

Thanks Vkiernan,

I am glad to hear that this is not specific to my way of doing the CT. Hopefully these symptoms will not remain much longer. Perhaps you are correct in that I need to have more than a fatty snack with my ice water. I usually do my soaks around mid day when the sun is up (when it is even showing it's face) after a lunch which is a little more substantial than a snack. The weird looking white spots made me a little nervous as I had blocks of frozen fat on my upper arms after my first icing session when I didn't notice that the cloth I was using as a barrier between me and the ice had slipped. It took a couple of weeks for that insult to my body heal. I really don't want to do any more damage because I am not paying attention.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-16T22:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15683>

I can see myself running the hose through the upstairs window into the bathroom ....

Last night I finally got my water below 60 for the first time. It actually got to 48-52F with about 30lb of frozen water bottles, left to thaw for an hour. The good news was I got it down low; the bad news was that it was mostly b/c my tub was slowly draining and only left two inches of water in by the time I got there!

I was amazed at the response, though. It was obviously harder to get into than my typical water that is only 62 after mucho ice going into it, but once in, my legs felt invigorated and my back felt \*hot\* (I leaned down so my back was in the water). Hot! I guess some of y'all have probably experienced that?

It only took 5 minutes for the water to heat up to 60+ with me in it, sadly, but it was a great 5 minutes!

### Susan M. — 2012-04-16T23:35:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15720>

When I get my tub full by the croak-soak method my water reads 53 - 54 degrees. After sitting in the tub for 40 minutes, the temperature is up to 55. Does this mean that my thermostat hasn't kick on yet? Should my water be reading warmer than 50? I don't have a proper thermometer to check my actual skin temperature.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-16T23:37:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15721>

IDK if you're responding to what I said, but my tub was only two inches deep, and most of that was full of the water bottles I had frozen, so very little water was actually in the tub, which is why I heated it up so quickly.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-17T02:54:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15776>

Had a great session today. I got the water temp to my outdoor cold tub down to 62 degrees finally!! (it has been avg 70 degrees) Spent 40 mins with the last 5 in a mild shiver...( I blew up my ketosis over the Easter week...but I am back at it) It only took 40 pounds of ice. CT sucks in FL. I have all my problems with the tub solved but one and that is the temp.

The tub has been plumbed for easy drainage, I have a 10x10 sun shade to keep direct sunlight off of it, I hung a mosquito net for bug free CTing, for now I keep a tarp over it when not in use to keep the water clean (change it out once a week)

The last thing is keeping the water at a cooler temp when not using it so I do not need to use so much ice. My thought is to build an insulated top for it and spray it with insulating foam. This will be the most expensive part so far.

100 gallon tub.... $60

drain parts.... $ 10

sun shade.... already owned one

mosquito net(amazon) $20 including shipping

insulation system ??? est. $100-150

amount of water used and ice bought....???? I don't want to know...LOL

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-17T11:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15836>

Won't be able to do a proper cold bath until tomorrow, but have been having cold showers every day - have also been unable to spot ice, but I'm hoping the showers will keep everything ticking over. I noticed this morning that, for the first time, I was able to get into a completely cold shower (I usually add a tiny bit of heat first - not enough that my OH registers it as anything other than "cold", but just enough to take the edge off when I get in) without wincing! In fact, I tried to turn it down, and was disappointed when it wouldn't! Tap water is coming out at 5C, so that's quite a cold shower!

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-17T16:36:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15877>

Last night I got up to 40 minutes immersion in 53F water. At this point, I feel like I could do 60+ minutes, my fingers and toes no longer go numb, and I feel comfortable in there. I really wish I could read, but I'm worried about dropping my kindle in the water --we have a large tub (my wife's I'm stealing!) and everything is submerged but my head, hands, and feet. Getting bored of the same old podcasts.....

Weightloss has plateaued - lost 7 llbs pretty quickly, now I'm stuck. But it appears I'm getting cold adapted - no flinching anymore!

### Pamela — 2012-04-17T21:21:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15953>

I continue to feel good after my cold baths. I bring in 2 buckets of ice and add them to the water once I am in so I don't feel the cold getting colder. Today I took a walk after I was done and it helped me warm up faster since I really do not like the cold shivering feeling. One thing I noticed is itching skin on stomach and back, like annoying itching that doesn't seem to be dry skin. Any thoughts on what this is?

### vkiernan — 2012-04-17T21:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15963>

Today was a strange day but totally my fault and won't be doing this again in this fashion. Took my CT bath, 53 degree water for 40 min. including arms, etc. and realized I really needed to wash my hair when I was finished. So - off into the shower I go with semi-cool water to wash my hair. That's when I started to shiver out of control. Was pretty bad. I am assuming I lost a ton of heat from my head by washing my hair. I always take my CT with a scarf wrapped around my head. I won't be doing this again. CT only,. screw the hair.

Susan, My water doesn't warm up much either with me in it ( I have a very large spa tub - feel guilty having to fill it everyday). I think it all depends on the size of the tub (how much water you have to put int it), the size of the person and yes to some degree if one is cold-adapted or not. I am pretty sure I am not but I am having lot's of signs of improvements. I do feel my legs warm up now after the 5 min. mark. Pretty cool to experience that. I think I actually enjoyed it the last 2 days. I am also not hungry yesterday or today. Good signs I think along with I did drop a pound or something as my pants are loose now from just 3 days ago. Don't have a scale so just go by how clothes fit. I also lost a pound in the first 2 weeks.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-17T21:54:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15964>

I have a cold/flu (from my kids or patients?) and still doing CT albeit without laps or any physical exertion. I went to work yesterday after CT while sick and the sick part was more like an inconvenience than an illness. Last night was rough; sore throat hindered sleep. Today, going to do some CT, work on a paper, and finish my taxes!

CT helps me make it through the day when multiple stressors are afoot!

### AKMan — 2012-04-17T23:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15980>

30 minutes in 48 degree tub, submerged to the chest, arms in the water for the most part. Didn't dry off, did some chores outside at around 50 degree air temp for about 30 minutes--started shivering when I came in, lasted about an hour. 2 fingers turned white and went numb for a couple hours.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-17T23:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-15989>

> **AKMan;16050 wrote:** 30 minutes in 48 degree tub, submerged to the chest, arms in the water for the most part. Didn't dry off, did some chores outside at around 50 degree air temp for about 30 minutes--started shivering when I came in, lasted about an hour. 2 fingers turned white and went numb for a couple hours.

Wow, awesome job! (except for the white fingers of course )

### shilohman — 2012-04-18T02:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16052>

> **AKMan;16050 wrote:** 30 minutes in 48 degree tub, submerged to the chest, arms in the water for the most part. Didn't dry off, did some chores outside at around 50 degree air temp for about 30 minutes--started shivering when I came in, lasted about an hour. 2 fingers turned white and went numb for a couple hours.

Your core body temp was raised from the CT and by the time you went inside your core had cooled down. At least thats my take on it.

### AKMan — 2012-04-18T02:09:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16060>

> **shilohman;16122 wrote:** Your core body temp was raised from the CT and by the time you went inside your core had cooled down. At least thats my take on it.

In Wim Hof's book, "Becoming the Iceman", he calls it 'afterdrop'.

### Susan M. — 2012-04-18T02:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16065>

Hubby and I are both recovering from a small cold that we got from the grand baby, and I have just not felt like getting in a cold tub. Skipped the tub yesterday, but will be icing tonight. There is an urge in the back of my mind that keeps telling me "gotta do it, gotta do it" and I just don't feel settled until it is done - either the bath or an ice session.

I have also found that this cold is pretty wimpy compared to prior colds and is disappearing quickly. I'm not worried that CT'ng will make my cold worse, but the cold already has me chilled so I am not motivated to voluntarily get colder at the moment.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-18T03:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16093>

Crazy stressful day - In between normal work, I had a 4 hour tv/film call where I was going to have to be in front of a tv camera. NOT something I enjoy. It was for a client, so I did it. So to prepare, and hopefully make me look better I did a 20 min face soak before hair/makeup. Then I did a 30 min soak at about 65 degrees to try and relax and get into the spirit.

Then when day was finally over I am now sitting in the tub at about 55 degrees, on min 13 now. I have been saving the new CT 10 posting to read - and now I shall go do that. I want to try and make it to 60 mins.

My dilemna is do I get out when starting to get shivery? I don't really want to be up half the night freezing. It was a super stressful day and I'm hoping it relaxes me.

I also got off the eating plan today, I was so nervous and busy this morning I did not do BAB, and thought I would try to power through with bullet proof coffee and a not-so-big-assed-breakfast. Then before I even realized what I did (very distracted) I was munching down on a bunch of almonds. All in all, probably ate less during the day than I normally do for BAB, but it was not high protein really nor circadian ideal.

Ate a big steak for dinner along with some green beans and here I am. Ready to tackle the new blog post and debating on when to get out.

That's my story and I'm sticking to it.

Once this piece airs (dateline nbc) I will let you all know. There are shots of me before I started RX/CT and shots today about 6 weeks later. Can't wait to see if anybody can tell the difference.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-18T03:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16104>

did 30 min in 56-58 F its definitely getting easier, takes about 45 min to warm up. haven't really noticed much improvements so far. eating a low carb pale diet, and more seafood. its been only 2 weeks of baths. I know its a very short time. i'll be patient.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-18T14:27:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16179>

Cavemam, I get out when I start to get shivery. I know I won't last long. It is sometimes hard to get out anyway as things are cold. I am still not able to go 60 mins. especially if I do the upper body too.

What are you doing for NBC? Sounds very interesting along with the fact that it is on tv no less. You could start a thread if you wanted to tell us all about it. I'm sure others would be interested too. Or, just post out here. I frankly don't care but some are techy that way and want to keep the threads clean. Thanks.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-18T21:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16313>

I feel like I'm not progressing with CT. I suppose it is something that I am sitting in 50 degree water for over 45 mins at least 5X a week. I think I may change my method a little and get out when starting to shiver, and do less bath and try to do more spot icing on BAT area. Doc confirmed that icing the upper back does have an impact on BAT production.

So my tv gig is Dateline following my client (I'm a realtor) around, part of his life story right now is finding a house. Not sure how they are going to spin it in production so I can't really say more than that. I beg to all the gods that I'm a minor character in this. I had no idea what I was getting into when I agreed to do it.

There will be some idiotic shots of me taking him into houses and pointing to things like toilets and kitchens, as if he wouldn't realize that's what it was! I think there are also some gem's like me saying to a father of 3 small kids, one of which has ran out into the street multiple times during house showings "so, is this super busy street a deal breaker? You don't want your children run over?"

today for some reason I am NOT motivated to get into the bath. I know I should...but I just don't want to... ok...time to get off my ass and get into the cold tub...

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-18T23:30:38+0300

### Lyndra — 2012-04-19T02:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16393>

I've been doing 45 minutes at around 50 degrees and struggle when I get to 30 minutes, but I finish (and shiver like mad afterwards). Today I went for 40 minutes at 68 degrees and what a difference! The tub didn't feel much warmer but at 30 minutes in, I wasn't cold like usual. Warm-up was easier, too. I am going to back off a bit and use warmer water until I feel like I am adapting more. I'm telling myself this is not a race, I don't have to get to the finish line "first"...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-19T02:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16412>

> **quelsen;16409 wrote:** get a colder bath. i can do 36f now for less time but it made an impact

Interesting strategy, I may work on this. I adapted to 32 degree face water for 20 mins a shot within 2 weeks. The real question is shivering counter productive, or does it signal that there is a change in environment and help adapt?

Care to tell me how much Ice I need in my garden tub to bring the water from 50 down to 32-35 degrees?

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T03:03:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16420>

Today was weird! I spent less time in the pool than usual, and my fingers and hands turned white. One finger was numb. I have spent more time in the pool when it was colder @ 62 degrees and this 66 degree swill makes my hands turn white?!

Go figure. This has me confounded.

Anyway, a few minutes after I noticed it, my hands turned back to normal.

I'm hopping back in . . .

### nonchalant — 2012-04-19T05:25:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16480>

Maybe it's my imagination, but my toes seem to be tolerating the cold water better. Today I lifted them out of the water out of habit, but then I kept dunking them back in as I moved around. The last minutes I kept them in, and was surprised.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-19T17:40:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16598>

I was only able to fit in a 15 minute cold shower (53F) this morning.

But my furnace feels like it is rev'd up today and my face is hot like wasabi.

### Larry — 2012-04-19T18:02:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16601>

> **Nonchalant;16550 wrote: Maybe it's my imagination, but my toes seem to be tolerating the cold water better. Today I lifted them out of the water out of habit, but then I kept dunking them back in as I moved around. The last minutes I kept them in, and was surprised.**

**

My toes have been the toughest nut to crack as well since they go numb after** CT not during. Last night I kept them in the water almost the whole time & they responded better than they any time previously. I guess the key is to just keep at it.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-19T18:17:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16605>

> **Larry;16671 wrote: My toes have been the toughest nut to crack as well since they go numb after** CT not during. Last night I kept them in the water almost the whole time & they responded better than they any time previously. I guess the key is to just keep at it.

I have one stubborn toe on my right foot that has been known to go numb about 20 minutes after I've finished my CT- I've never had this issue with ice packs, but only with immersions and sometimes showers.

Overtime it has gotten better and better, and maybe has only done it once this last week.

When it was at its worst, I started doing vigorous windmill/scissor kicks (bracing my upper body with my arms - between two counter tops). I would do about 100 of these. And if I did that right after CT, I never had the toes go numb. I did a similar approach to numb fingers, but the toe was the most stubborn of all.

### Larry — 2012-04-19T18:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16612>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;16675 wrote:** I have one stubborn toe on my right foot that has been known to go numb about 20 minutes after I've finished my CT- I've never had this issue with ice packs, but only with immersions and sometimes showers.
>
>
>
> Overtime it has gotten better and better, and maybe has only done it once this last week.
>
>
>
> When it was at its worst, I started doing vigorous windmill/scissor kicks (bracing my upper body with my arms - between two counter tops). I would do about 100 of these. And if I did that right after CT, I never had the toes go numb. I did a similar approach to numb fingers, but the toe was the most stubborn of all.

Villjamur, I have asked Dr. Kruse about this very thing & you will find his response below. I had to look up a few words though!  Good luck!!

Larry Says:

April 3rd, 2012 at 3:15 pm

Dr. Jack, quick question for you. When I CT my toes go numb after I am done (not during) even though I don’t put them in the water. I do put the bottom half of my foot in the water though. I was curious about why it is after that they go numb plus why would a body part not submerged experience this effect. As always, thanks so much!

Jack Says:

April 3rd, 2012 at 3:51 pm

@Larry its common…..I write about this in my book. It is because our spinothalamic tract covers three dermotomes above and below the decussation of where the pain and temperature fibers cross in the spinal cord. Its normal.

### AKMan — 2012-04-19T18:51:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16619>

Took the ultimate ice bath last night. Filled tub with 38 degree water, than added ice until it was 32 degrees. Stepped in, water was up to my knees. Felt like needles stinging my feet, ankles were pretty painful. Stood there maybe 30 seconds and sat down. WOW! That was chilly. Water was up to my armpits. I laid back and took assessment of what was happening. Head was all that was out of the water. I could see all skin was turning bright red. Feet and hands were hard to move after about 2 minutes. I focused hard on breathing normally. After 5 minutes, it was all kind of numb. I would continually cross and un-cross my legs to keep circulation going. I kept my arms and hands under for the most part. After 10 minutes, the water temp was 33 degrees.

At the 15 minute point, I felt like I had lost most motor control in fingers and toes, so I opened the drain and turned the hot water on. I raised the bath temp to 55 degrees and it felt like I was in a very warm bath. Amazing experience! Feeling and control came back to fingers and toes. Bright redness diminished. I spent another 30 minutes at 55 degrees because it felt so good.

Got out after 45 minutes (15 at 33 degrees/30 at 55 degrees), drip-dried in 75 degree greenhouse while tending seedlings. My legs were pretty stiff-could barely walk normally. I never shivered until I left the greenhouse and went to fetch water--when I hit the 60 degree air, I started shivering--pretty hard. When I'd go back into the greenhouse, I'd stop shivering. After about 30 minutes I was dried off, put on some sweats and heavy socks and went about my business as usual.

So, I'd always wondered what 32 degree water would feel like, now I know. Don't think I will do it again anytime soon. But I have a new appreciation for 55 degree water--it's not so bad.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-19T18:56:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16625>

> **AKMan;16689 wrote:**
>
> So, I'd always wondered what 32 degree water would feel like, now I know. Don't think I will do it again anytime soon. But I have a new appreciation for 55 degree water--it's not so bad.

This sounds pretty cool. You've inspired me, I'm going to try it out this weekend!

### Qodesh — 2012-04-19T19:00:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16627>

I got my bath down to 54 degrees for 30 minutes. My skin was a nice rosy red.

Staying naked and drip drying seem to be the ticket for me. When I first got out, it felt like the room was quite warm. The shivering didn't start until I towel dried. That seemed to bring attention to my skin. The warm feeling came and went for about 20 mins. When I touched my skin with anything, ie towel, clothing, blanket, etc..... is when I felt cold.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-19T20:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16661>

Great experiment, AKMan! To all those watching along at home ... do not try this without a spotter!

I mean, seriously, I don't want to hear about one of you found dead in the tub because hypothermia confused you and you thought you should go under the water instead of get out of it.

I did 50 degrees last week and still didn't turn the slightest bit pink. What does it take?

### shilohman — 2012-04-19T20:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16674>

> **Qodesh;16697 wrote:** I got my bath down to 54 degrees for 30 minutes. My skin was a nice rosy red.
>
> Staying naked and drip drying seem to be the ticket for me. When I first got out, it felt like the room was quite warm. The shivering didn't start until I towel dried. That seemed to bring attention to my skin. The warm feeling came and went for about 20 mins. When I touched my skin with anything, ie towel, clothing, blanket, etc..... is when I felt cold.

Sounds like you are cold adapted. As you do it more the warm feeling should last longer. For me, it lasts 45-60 minutes.

### shilohman — 2012-04-19T20:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16679>

> **MamaGrok;16731 wrote:** I did 50 degrees last week and still didn't turn the slightest bit pink. What does it take?

It took me awhile before I turned pink. Now my whole torso/body does, so just keep on trucking, it will happen.

### AKMan — 2012-04-19T20:41:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16681>

>

>

Does your skin turn pink if you spot-ice? That was the first time I noticed it. For me 50 degree water doesn't seem to do much, but man that 32 degree water turned my very red. It went away when I upped the temp to 55 pretty quick.

Spotter - good idea, but also don't try unless you can do 50 degrees or so for 30 minutes +. Work your way up to it. This week did a cold bath every night for 5 days (starting at 55, then 50, then 45 for 3 nights) because I really wanted to see what 32 degree water felt like.

Noticing any extra signs it had some kind of ct-detox or benefit effect? Did your pants fall down?! Did you break out in a rash?! Anything today you notice?

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T00:33:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16767>

> **Cavemam;16829 wrote:** Noticing any extra signs it had some kind of ct-detox or benefit effect? Did your pants fall down?! Did you break out in a rash?! Anything today you notice?

I can't say I noticed anything different today. My legs are still sore from the weighted squats I did yesterday morning. If anything, I feel a little more 'shivver-y' today, but I have been drinking ice water and working in a 65 degree room all day.

I have been CT'ing hard since mid-Feb. I have lost a few pounds in that time, but I probably would have anyway. My feet got pretty tough from all the barefoot snow walks I did in March. I caught a cold on an airplane that lasted 3-4 days, about normal for me. I can say my toes stay much warmer than usual, and I wake up a night very hot and have to throw the covers off.

I don't know if you are following my thyroid bio-hack, but I have been off my Synthroid for 5 weeks now. I go in next week for TSH/T4 labs. I have a feeling they will be high(TSH)/low(T4), but I feel great. I'll keep you posted.

Basically, I must say CT'ing this winter for me was a lot like taking Vit D. I was very deficient, but felt fine. I took Vit D3 for several months and got my levels way up there--and feel no different. But I know it's good for me!

### Mott — 2012-04-20T01:32:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16783>

After two days off CT... I felt the call of the wild and got back at it 20 min w/tub water a consistent 42..20 more minutes at 50 degrees..Lake Erie is getting close to 50 so pretty soon I will take my first plunge of the season...

### Susan M. — 2012-04-20T06:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16915>

I am not sure that I want to go colder than the 54-55 degrees I do now. I didn't soak today, so will do icing this evening. Yesterday was probably my 23 soak, and for whatever reason, the middle finger on my left hand went totally white and numb. This has not happened before. I keep my feet in the water for the entire time now, although I am using swimming togs on my feet. My toes still go numb about 10 minutes after I CT, but I find that if I jiggle up and down and bounce back and forth on my feet for a while that the numbing doesn't last as long, many be about and hour instead of two. That is progress, I think.

It took me several soaks before my torso began to get the red look. I do not shiver anymore and I am warming up more quickly, in less than 40 minutes I guess. I am still not noticing the heated up effect that so many of you seem to experience.

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T07:40:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-16923>

Tonight I did my last CT for the season. With green stuff popping up all around and nearly 20 hours of sun, I figured it's time to transition to summer.

I filled the tub with cold well water and a trashbag full of ice. It was 34 degrees. I plopped in and stayed for 45 minutes. It was 37 when I got out. No warm-water cheating tonight! I practiced my Wim Hof breathing and 'oil-pulled' with sesame oil for 20 minutes while in the water. If any of you have thought about oil-pulling (see [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oil\_pulling](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oil_pulling) ), the cold tub is a good place to do it. It focuses your mind on something different than the cold.

During the 45 minutes, all water exposed skin turned beet red, 2 fingers on one hand and one on the other went numb, I didn't shiver at all until I dried off and started doing stuff around the house. Lasted about and hour.

Today was my fasting day--I haven't eaten since dinner last night, 27 hours ago, and I feel fantastic. No hunger or weird feelings from not eating and I worked out today--walked 3 miles and did some squats. I usually fast once a week.

My plan for this summer, eat lots of fish and fresh veggies. Take a cold swim in a river or lake when the opportunity arises. Enjoy life. I'll still check this forum daily, because I have a boring job where I can get on-line several times a day- ha!

### Guest — 2012-04-20T18:27:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-17116>

AKman...you are something else. I really enjoy reading your posts.

Thanks to AKman and Souldanzer for the inspiration, I finally got full immersion in the bathtub yesterday & today. There was shivering off and on, and I cut my time back to 35 minutes, but oddly enough, I warmed up much faster afterwards. Maybe it has to do with the contrast between warm, unexposed skin and that which is cold from the tub. Since more skin was cold, there was less contrast between warm & cold. Whatever, I am back to feeling excited about CT.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-22T22:59:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-17881>

I did it!!!

Water at 32-33 degrees! Tub, legs out, torso up to neck! 30 mins! Wooot!

ok, so the suprise was. . .it didn't feel as uncomfortable as the 50 degree water. I also didn't start to shiver ask quickly. only started shivering at about 25 mins. Stayed for 30 mins.

Took 50 lbds of ice. The hardest part was actually getting in. Once I sat down, it was a bit shocking in that it actually felt better after a few mins than warmer water. Skin got bright cherry and numb - so it was easy to stay. I tried putting my lower legs in, but that was painful quickly and I decided not to push it.

Wwooooo!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-23T05:20:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-17974>

My CT today was FREAKING AWESOME!

I finally got it together to get into the Chattahoochee River - the water released from the bottom of the dam at Lake Lanier and according to the National Park Service (although an Orvis website says 63F at midday), "rarely gets above 50 degrees any time of the year."

I felt hot most of the day today, despite it being 63 with a COLD wind - every other year I can remember, this kind of weather is AWFUL for me, but I was wearing a tank & shorts & loving it. Anyway, I decided that after hiking through the woods, it was the perfect day for a cold swim.

Went down to the river, sat down on the submerged part of the boat ramp, and sat in the 50(?) degree water for 10m (then had to get to an appointment). I haven't had any CT colder than 62 for longer than a minute, really, up to this point. The water was thrilling and refreshing. I felt warm, while hikers went by in pants and long shirts and the wind blew hard. Went shoulder-deep and had DH time my stay.

Afterwards, I walked wet to the car and felt very warm and exhilarated. Kept feeling that way for the next 10-20m, like I'd just gotten off a roller coaster and wanted to go again. I'd love to do this every day! but feel like dh would need to be with me (it's a cold river and sometimes the current gets strong fast when they release water).

Anyway, it rocked, just wanted to share!

### MartiD — 2012-04-24T17:49:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18371>

I also discovered last night that having the water warmer is not easier! I have had either a re-lapse on a cold from a week ago or having some nasty detox in sinuses from CT so I hadn't done a bath for 3 days. So I decided last night to do one but was worried about going straight tap water (approx 58 degrees) so I added a bit of hot water. I think the temp was around 70-73 degrees and I was miserable! Shivered more than usual, hard recovery and didn't stay in long. This morning I went with straight tap and had no issues and the only reason I didn't stay in longer was I had to got work! I do find the mornings ones much easier than evening regardless of water temps. I think most of that is because of my BAB and also I will shower first to wash hair, usually have water cool in shower so I am already 'cold' before I enter the bath.

### indigogirl — 2012-04-25T02:22:42+0300

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-25T02:29:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18534>

Mine was very cool, lol. 1 hour in the pool. At the 35 minute mark I noticed my body initiate small shivers over my chest. The radius and intensity increased until my whole body was involved. It was interesting seeing my head go from stable to shaking over 25 minutes. I got out for 1 minute midway through to test my response in air, the shivering left immediately, I no longer felt cold, so back in until the 1 hour mark. The longest so far. Afterwards, I went inside, rearranged/cleaned the living room (made the dw happy), and topped it off with some optimized coffee.

### Tucker — 2012-04-25T02:36:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18539>

> **Cavemam;17952 wrote:** I did it!!!
>
>
>
> Water at 32-33 degrees! Tub, legs out, torso up to neck! 30 mins! Wooot!
>
>
>
> ok, so the suprise was. . .it didn't feel as uncomfortable as the 50 degree water. I also didn't start to shiver ask quickly. only started shivering at about 25 mins. Stayed for 30 mins.
>
>
>
> Took 50 lbds of ice. The hardest part was actually getting in. Once I sat down, it was a bit shocking in that it actually felt better after a few mins than warmer water. Skin got bright cherry and numb - so it was easy to stay. I tried putting my lower legs in, but that was painful quickly and I decided not to push it.
>
>
>
> Wwooooo!

Cavemam,

I totally agree with you that the colder the water is the easier it is to sit in the tub. I have been doing 30 min, full immersion CT at 54 degrees for over a month now. After listening to the webinar I decided it was time to do some deep CT. Today I did 25 minutes, total immersion (up to neck) at 40 degrees. I can't believe I went that long. Before I got in I was just hoping to be able to pull off 10 minutes. I sat in the 40 degree water and kept on thinking WOW this feels invigorating and I could go forever. Where as when I"m CTing in 54 degree water I"m constantly looking at my watch and thinking I can't wait to get out. I don't know if other people have experience a much more enjoyable experience at the colder temps but for me today it was like night and day. I'm obviously going to keep doing my CT at 40 degrees and will post back and let you know if I continue to feel this way about the colder water.

Today was the first time I experienced the red, cherry skin after getting out of the water. It almost looked like I had a deep tan. It took me about 2 hours to warm up and I did shiver for awhile afterward. It felt like I was doing involuntary, full body isometrics for the next couple hours, do you think that is why people get better body composition from CT?

Anyhow, I totally suggest everyone consider doing the deep CT. I"m completely surprised, in a good way with how it went. I don't know why it is just easier.

I have Hashimotos and adrenal fatigue. I always feel like a million bucks after my CT. For some reason it really helps me with my stomach. My stomach always feels more comfortable after CTing, like it helps with my digestion and I don't feel any bloating. Also, my brain fog is completely gone due to the CT.

I have some issues with rT3 and will be getting tested in a few more months to see if my numbers change. However, no testing is needed for me at this point to convince me that something good is happening. Deep down inside I feel this protocol is going to heal my autoimmunity.

So people get your deep CT on!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-25T02:56:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18550>

For my bath today I finally decided to go down past the 60 deg that comes straight outta the tap. I found out that my business acct's debit card had been hacked and several hundred dollars charged to my small business, $700 may not seem like much to some but it is a big deal to me, in addition to the "violated" feeling. After dealing with bank and disputing the charges, stopping the debit card, and CRYING......I decided GODDESS up and get my #&(# together. Runny nose and teary eyed still, I picked up 4 bags of ice @ 7-11, brought the temp down to 47 and I cursed a blue streak at that @\*&#^%@% @\*#&^\*%-ing sunnava-#^&% and plopped my butt in! Feet, arms out. Water up to the nips.

Well, let's say my anger was displaced immediately LOL I was re-reading Way of the Peaceful Warrior to get my head and heart right, I noticed at about 20 min I couldn't focus on reading anymore. Cold. Book away, I focused on breathing. @ 28:00 I realized my pointer fingers on both hands were stark white. Started to shiver, couldn't "will" myself to stop. got out @ 30:00

I feel quite accomplished at having gone in for so long and so cold. More importantly, I am no longer stressing over the debit card, the bank will investigate and everything will be alright.

### ealachan — 2012-04-25T02:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18552>

Legs in 55 degree water for 20 minutes. Skin was 80 deg going in, 58 coming out. Only doing my legs because my tub is not fat-girl-friendly...

### colleencoble — 2012-04-25T03:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18564>

I just hopped out of mine. I think I'm addicted.  I read and relax in my cold retreat.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-25T03:48:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18573>

Ok! Did my 2nd round of deeper CT today. Water started at 30 degrees (I had added salt to get temp down) and it warmed fairly quickly I think. My skin got down to 34-35 at the start, and 41 by the end.

I did 45 mins, and that was a little too long. I was a major shivering, but I had been blasting my tunes and the time seemed to go quickly. I could have stayed longer, but had told myself in advance that 45 was IT. Glad I got out when I did. I was woozyish and MAJOR full body shivers and a little out of it. It only took about 2 hrs to get back to normal. Also super thirsty and sick to my stomach. I won't be doing it before bed for a while as I was running around cleaning and doing stuff to move and generate heat.

I kept sticking my lower legs/feet in and out, but they really felt it at about 2-3 mins a pop. I don't think I'm ready to jump full body into the freezing ocean yet. But my torso was a trooper! Could have gone longer!

The non extremities that were in actually enjoyed it. I loved seeing skin turn bright red and the numbness. Hard beats helped.

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-25T04:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18578>

I'm amazed at how cold some of your CT baths are! Do you get crazy detox as a result? I've done 17 cold baths/showers and so far 62F is where I'm comfortable at, 59F was the coldest I attempted. I don't have much of detox going on, except for sinus allergies that's quite annoying but I blame it on the crazy weather this spring. I'm planning to lower the water temperature gradually but I'm kinda curious what will happen if I just step into 50F water tomorrow. I don't want crazy detox though - if that's in the cards I'd rather take it slow.

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-25T04:31:46+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-25T16:28:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-18680>

For me, I want to lose 60 lbds - so I'm choosing to experiment a little on myself. I also have some medical things that just don't apply to everyone. Also, for me, it's getting into summer and I want to squeeze in as much good CT as possible. I think Jack mentioned in the webinar that it's more effective in winter. I am stepping it up personally to see if if I can spur something along before summer is in full swing.

This is above and beyond what his CT prescription is - and I'm not advocating it for anyone else! Just sharing  It's so exciting to know that one can push the edges. As mentioned, 45 mins was too long. I was sketchy coming out, so I'm sure a fitter/thinner person would not last as long!

Someone ask how long I've been CTing. Here is my list so far:

1st-2nd week of March: Progressive face dunks in 32 degree water going 20 mins (using a snorkel) by the end. I've kept these at least 3X a week for pure vanity reasons.

Moved on to spot icing for a week or two.

Tried 1st bath, water at 47 degrees, went in with lower torso on first try, did 15 mins. Worked my way up to 60 mins at water anywhere from 50-65 depending on how it came out of the tap. Did about 5X a week.

Started a colder bath for the first time Sunday. Have done 2X. I'm hoping to get adapted enough to go swimming outside in colder water.

I'm going to keep trying to bring the temp down and get my feet adapted more so I can go whole body in the colder water - then swim! I'm hoping I can find some cold water swimming areas up in the mountains around here.

The take-away from this is: Colder isn't "harder!".

### ealachan — 2012-04-27T02:47:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19197>

20 min, 50 degree water (it was coming out of the tap at 47), legs only.

Skin was 85 degrees going in, 53 coming out. 10 minutes later, skin still feels very cold to the touch but I have no sensation of my legs being cold, if that makes sense.

Oral temp before I went in: 98.6

Oral temp after 12 minutes in water: 99.1

Oral temp at end of 20 min: 99.1

HOLY MOTHER OF SNOWBALLS! Coldest I've done so far. It felt like a million pins and needles stabbing my skin, especially my feet, the whole time. My skin turned lobster-red within 5 minutes. About 10 minutes post-soak, skin is mostly back to normal color even though it's still very cold to the touch.

Felt really tired getting out of the tub, like I'd just hiked a mountain and done a bazillion sit-ups.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-27T04:23:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19228>

Ah, just crawled out. I'm all nice and pink and cold.  Who would have thought that ME, the queen of "I'm cold, turn up the heat" would actually like a cold bath. Amazing!

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-27T04:27:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19230>

It was colder and rainy today so as soon as I got into the pool, my body began to shiver. The cold receptors on my head really kicked in the shivering early.

I am also moving around the pool since the flow reduces the insulating barrier naturally occurring while stationary making me colder faster. I am now convinced that total submersion will provide the most effect so I am going to buy a snorkel and try to find out what its like.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-27T04:59:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19241>

Brr, that sounds cold! I don't like my head to be cold.  I get earaches.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-27T05:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19245>

Argh.... Two days in a row with no ct. Too much going on with kids programs etc. I must get these babies to sleep soon so I can get some ice packs on me. Gettin in my pool for sure tomorrow though!

Loved seeing the chicken livers with the ducky

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-27T15:27:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19297>

Interesting about the cold head, Colleen; I used to get deep chest coughing any time I was in cold water or really cold air ... and just realized that isn't happening her \*at all\*.

**Just a friendly reminder to everyone, especially any newbies - USE CAUTION.

\- spotter if you drop the water temp or duration a lot, or immerse your head, because hypothermia makes you confused and that's when you die ... you stay in longer, or go underwater or even stupider.

\- check your core temp if you're at all unsure, based on shivering or skin color or any other signs, that you might have gone too far.** Best way is, erm, rectal.

_Just because someone else is going colder/longer doesn't mean you have to, should, or are ready to._ We all go our own pace. I LOVE the stories of going hard core here, but that has to be done when your body & gut tell you you are ready, and with the right precautions.

No one should even think about going way cold without gradual increase first. I'd think a gradual change in temp down to 50 over a few weeks, then a plunge colder might be fine (with a spotter), but NOT just hitting it hard right from the start.

As for me, I've been doing 65-70 degree baths (because that's as cold as I can usually get my tub, even with gobs of ice), with tons of shivering and cold in and out of the water at times, but when I took the 50 degree river plunge, it instantly felt great & invigorating, and left me \*warm\* afterward, not cold. I loved it. (still no pink/red skin,, though, without spot icing. and even the ice packs no longer feel cold after they've been on for more than 5 seconds. I keep having to check to see if they're still there/cold.)

+

40 minutes and in 58 degrees......pretty chilly plus the air was chilly.

### LinD — 2012-04-28T04:43:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19512>

No cold bath since last weekend, I believe, but spot icing, plus ending morning shower with cold water.

Water out of faucet, though, is only 69 degrees, no thanks to Texas. NOT cold enough.

As we prepare to sell our home, this week was full of home improvement with workers in my bathrooms, kitchen, & family room. Now a mess to clean up after granite counter tops in master bath & guest bath, plus new plumbing fixtures, wallpaper removal in kitchen w/ texture replacement & paint, etc... And still more to do to get the house ready.

After all is said and done, I really need CT BAD.... The worst part of it is that my sweet Sheltie, 14-yo died this morning. I knew this day would come. I am so blue

Trying to get the water colder.... Now that I have my tub back, tomorrow morning I'll add all the ice jacks and frozen bottles we have. I'll have to make do with my ice pack snuggle tonight. Maybe that will help my mood....

RIP Trixie

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-28T20:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19611>

15m in 50 degree river with a nice current. By the end, my legs & trunk couldn't tell the water was cold at all, and not because they were numb - they weren't. It just didn't feel cold after the first minute. The current was pretty good, and so freshly cold water was constantly flowing by, but it just didn't feel cold - unlike my 60 degree tub where I constantly threaten dh with death if he causes so much as a ripple.

My forearms & hands were cold, though - very. I finally was able to get hands in, and so was submerged up to my chin in fresh, flowing COLD water, and was feeling very comfortable, and even my hands got a bit warmer at the end - slower movement, but no more jarring pain.

I did get cold when I got out, unlike last time, and took about 25m to warm up.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-28T20:13:54+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-28T20:27:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19618>

No, not numb, and I forgot to mention, for the first time with water alone, my legs & arms got pink, so ya! It was a very blotchy pink, though, wonder if that's because I'm very blotchy colored - lots of freckles and some white spots.

### ealachan — 2012-04-29T03:18:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19704>

_(apologies for the cross-posting, if it bothers you)_

Holy suffering SeaMonkeys!! Fat girl not only got her fat butt INTO the tub tonight, but she GOT IT OUT. Awwww yeah. Victory points for me! For those who don't recall, the last time I tried sitting in the tub for CT, I had a really hard time getting out because, frankly, I'm too damn heavy to hoist myself back up without grab bars, pulleys, blocks and tackles, a team of draft horses and a suspension of the gravitational force itself. But tonight? TONIGHT...I just gave it the ol' heave-ho at the end of my 20 minutes in the water, and had WAY less struggle than last time. It's not like I weigh significantly less, so I'mma chalk that up to a strength gain and be overjoyed about it.

Anyway...the pertinents from tonight:

Water temp: 52 deg F Duration: 20 min, though it took me a while to actually ease down into the water, so I'll call it 15 minutes of actually sitting in the water fully.

Before CT: oral temp 97.7, skin temp 82

After CT: oral temp 99.0, skin temp 60 on lower legs, 70 on butt where I was touching the sides of the tub / didn't have good water circulation

Notes: Think my O6:O3 is still out of whack because my toe went white again. Just one toe, doesn't hurt, just turns waxy white. It looks pretty zombie-ish still, 15 min later. I did try to keep my toes out of the water once I got settled, but it didn't seem to matter. Tons of goosebumps while I was in the water, but no actual shivering while I was in. Slight amount of shivering started about 10 min post-CT, but not actually noticeable shaking...just muscles feeling tense. Felt like I'd climbed a mountain again (all in my legs) when I got out of the bath.

20 minutes post-bath, oral temp is 98.7...WHEEEEEEEEE!

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-29T03:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19711>

CT is getting easier, but my appetite has gotten out of control...

Maybe because I never used water that's too cold for me, I never got numb. And I use a washcloth to massage my arms/legs/all over to help the circulation. My tummy always got pink, my arms would look really tanned (more so than when I'm not cold) while my legs stay the normal color. Once I got the water really cold for me, 56F, and that was when my whole body got kinda pink.

I'm hoping the CT induced hunger will go away soon.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-29T14:32:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19749>

Last night's CT was better then the day before but I am still struggling with time. Can't seem to make it more than 40 min. tops when I expose the arms and girls. Water is around 53. The question still remains for me if length of time is more critical than amount of skin exposure or vice versa. I could go a little longer when bundling the upper half up. I would like to keep the backs of my arms in to try to get rid of the globbly little fat thats there. You all know, "old lady arms!"

### Lyndra — 2012-04-29T17:30:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19783>

I have a similar experience. I can take 45 minutes when I am immersed to the bustline and only 30 when I am in up to the neck, including hands & arms. I'm thinking that more skin exposure trumps duration. Detox effects have returned with the deeper exposure so I think I'm on the right track.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-29T17:35:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19790>

I'm thinking that more skin exposure trumps duration. Detox effects have returned with the deeper exposure so I think I'm on the right track.

Same with me Gals, something about arms and ladies that does me in quick. I too have had trouble with it last couple of days. I worked outside yesterday and did not CT. I'm trying to start today right with a big CT plan. . .already purchased ice.

I was wondering if it's just bottom half is more cold trained because it's in the water every time - so it's simply had more CT.

Perhaps there is some crazy environmental reason like the magnetic pull of the earth or the amount of ozone or some such stuff. Lyndra - we've had such glorious weather this weekend perhaps nature is calling us out of our caves to forage for greens and get some sun instead of CT.

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-29T18:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19825>

65 minutes in cold bath, started at 61F, down 56F. Full submersion up to my neck most of the time, and I moved my arms, lifted my butt, etc to get water circulating. Hands and feet outside about half the time. I feel the colder the water is, the "better" I feel - must be related to the release of some feel-good hormones when body is under acute stress (coldness). I need to read more on this. But 61F didn't make me feel good as 58F did. Then 56F was a bit too cold. Shivered a bit at the end. Otherwise I feel great!

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-29T18:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19828>

Inspired by those of you who have been getting your CT in Mother Nature, I finally went in the ocean! So excited and proud of myself. Drove an hour + to Avon, to sheltered area that has little wave activity, even at high tide.

air temp 63, water temp 51.

stayed in for 25 min, up to neck, was in a very sheltered area called the cove, right by Shark River Inlet. I wanted to be safe since I was going alone, I also checked tides, it was low tide, so cove + LT = I felt safe. I was so excited on the drive down. I marched right down to shoreline, dumped sweatshirt and ran whooping like a lunatic in a tank top and booty shorts right into the water. THe guys fishing on the jetty were laughing. I swam, treaded water, bodysurfed, and sometimes just stood still. It was wonderful. There was a nook by the jetty where I could sit with the water up to my chin, but that was boring, I wanted to move around and play. AWESOME. I will definitely do this again!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-29T18:43:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19838>

TL, I love the river. Your experience sounds awesome! I love the double-takes of people who realize that I'm actually out there \*swimming\* in a river almost no one will venture into even in the summer (b/c of cold, not ick, lol). I'm not sure what all is realistic as far as what could go wrong, but I right now do not plan to do it unaccompanied at all. DH & I think that I can go safely without him (the water is 50, btw), but I will have my oldest child with me (13), and a life jacket I can hold on to, so worst case (so far as I can anticipate), I would get totally confused and still have the ski belt around my waist or strapped to one arm or something, that would keep me afloat as dd dialed for help.

I don't anticipate that happening at all, as I really doubt the water is cold enough to get me confused enough that I wouldn't be able to stand up and walk out (it's only 1.5ft deep where I go, and no more than 4ft in the middle), but since I don't really have a full grasp of the potential risks, I figure it's better safe than sorry.

Obviously you have to weigh your own risks, but I'm not sure I'd do what you're doing without someone who could call for help if you got confused and worsened hypothermia by going out deeper or further.

### AmyJ — 2012-04-29T20:04:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19871>

Today was my first!!

I've been spot CT~ing--sitting on the icecube sheets from Wal-Mart since mid-March. I just sit on them when I'm on the computer. Figured it couldn't hurt. I do that a lot, an hr almost every day and sometimes twice on the weekend if they freeze back up fast enough. I've done face dunks, no problems.

I've been hesitating with the full bath because I don't have a spotter and my crp is HIGH, but after reading CT11 I realized that I was looking at that backward--neolithically, I guess. So I decided to go for it. Unfortunately my tap water is 79 degrees. That's disgusting, actually. I have been collecting 2 liter and smaller water bottles and filling them with water and freezing them in preparation for this day. It still only got the temp down to about 73. But I'm all over this now. It felt GREAT. 45 minutes. My skin temp was 66 on the parts I had just spot CTd before the bath and 68 on the tops of my thighs. Not sure how that can happen?! Two different thermometers? Anyway. It felt great so I'm buying some ice on the way home tomorrow and will use the 10 gallons of water that I just put in my fridge and see what I can get the temp to.

I'm one of you!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-29T20:05:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19873>

The Chattahoochee is a very lazy river. Once we canoed in it and accidentally started going the wrong way because we couldn't tell which way it was flowing! The flow varies, but that fact is definitely one reason I don't want to go alone. Whether there's a flow or tide or not, though, hypothermia can be deadly - even in your tub - because it takes away your reason. I'm just not sure what the best precautions are, or how remote the dangers are, when the river literally never gets below, or much above, 50 degrees.

I haven't gotten off the boat ramp yet, lol. It's submerged a good way in. If I were walking across, I'd wear shoes, because of all the trashy people who throw bottles in when drunkenly floating down in inner tubes.

DH would happily go, but doesn't get home in time during the week. I want to do this more than just on the weekends. He's happyto go with me on the weekends.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-29T22:33:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19920>

> **MamaGrok;19911 wrote:** TL, I love the river. Your experience sounds awesome! I love the double-takes of people who realize that I'm actually out there \*swimming\* in a river almost no one will venture into even in the summer (b/c of cold, not ick, lol)....Obviously you have to weigh your own risks, but I'm not sure I'd do what you're doing without someone who could call for help if you got confused and worsened hypothermia by going out deeper or further.

Hi MG, I received many, many double takes, and the fishermen had a hoot with me, and I with them! Thank you for your concern, I think I am out of the woods re: hypothermia, I've been taking cold baths at a lower temp and longer duration than the ocean was today, my main concern with ocean swimming is always current and tides. I knew it was low tide, and I picked the cove because it is protected and no chance of riptide, so I felt safe.

I had PLENTY of company, there were at least 20+ people that were on my side of the jetty, and I was talking with a few of them, telling them about CT. I saw some people on the boardwalk pointing at me. "crazy lady!"

The cove is pretty special to me, my parents live in the next town south, and my parents always walked us up the mile north to the cove (because it is sheltered and hardly any waves, current) We kids hated it and would always grumble on the walk there "why do we have to walk ALL this way to the beach, why can't we just go the one right HERE (a block away) ?!?!" Smart lady.

I dropped in on my parents after, they were excited to hear about my arctic dip, as they called it LOL and my mother (80) says she will stand in the water when I go swimming next. Yippee!

And I hear you on the "ick" factor of rivers, I live very close to the Hudson River, you could not pay me money to swim in that. Yuck.

### Lyndra — 2012-04-30T02:40:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19964>

> **Cavemam;19863 wrote:** Same with me Gals, something about arms and ladies that does me in quick. I too have had trouble with it last couple of days. I worked outside yesterday and did not CT. I'm trying to start today right with a big CT plan. . .already purchased ice.
>
>
>
> I was wondering if it's just bottom half is more cold trained because it's in the water every time - so it's simply had more CT.
>
>
>
> Perhaps there is some crazy environmental reason like the magnetic pull of the earth or the amount of ozone or some such stuff. Lyndra - we've had such glorious weather this weekend perhaps nature is calling us out of our caves to forage for greens and get some sun instead of CT.

You could be on to something. For me, it is getting harder to enter the tub and could be the lengthening days and nice weather. I also wonder if it's because I'm upping my carbs. I do notice that if I get in long several hours after a meal, I am much more uncomfortable.

Kevin's experience as related on the blog gave me more inspiration to keep at it, though. I have messed up hormones, bad sleep, and high hsCRP and now I feel much more hopeful about getting things in order. Today I went with two 10# bags of ice and everything my icemaker could produce. Temp got down to 47 and it was noticeably colder, especially on the hands & toes. Because of my arthritis, I kept my thumbs in for all 30 minutes but had to bring the toes out periodically.

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-30T04:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-19991>

Today I have experienced the deeply calming effects of CT. I found out 5 days ago that my sister is dying. She has inoperable lung cancer and has kept her secret since before christmas. She is nearing the end, and I am going to see her today. I am so empty.

So I got in the bath for an hour. I can't say that I feel good. But it's certainly more bearable. I think I'll get back in the bath tonight.

### Birdy — 2012-04-30T08:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-20027>

Today was my first day at the ocean and it was a bit disappointing for CT. I have never seen such a high tide at this beach (Miramar in Montecito), and onlookers said that all the sand had just washed away. The waves were coming all the way up to the rocks near the steps, so I waded in and only got waist deep. There were a bunch of kids with surf boards and wetsuits out farther. I forgot my water shoes, and had to wear crocs, and they kept coming off. I really didn't want to get farther out because I wasn't sure where all the rocks were. I thought I would spend all my energy trying to keep warm, but I spent more energy trying to keep my balance and not get all banged up. This beach is supposed to be 55 degrees, but I am not buying it. There were a couple of guys on paddleboards without wet suits, but I was the only one actually trying to be in the water. Next time I go, I'll try to get there at low tide so I can walk to the sandy areas. Maybe I am not a good judge of cold anymore. I was in a pool for a long time yesterday, and feeling a little warm. Others who tried to get in were shrieking.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-30T10:50:00+0300

I did feel like the Loch Ness Monster getting out...I wonder if Nessy is cold adapted..has to be.

Frustrated that I wasn't able to do any CT this weekend. Between numerous birthday parties for my daughter and multiple other obligations I just couldn't fit it in, so I decided to go deep tonight. I had been doing 30-45 minutes in about 55 degree water. Tonight I did 25 minutes at 49 degrees. I wasn't expecting 6 degrees to make such a difference, but wow . . . it did! I had some major detox reactions as soon as I got out of the tub.

oral temp before: 97.7

skin temp before: 85

duration: 25 minutes (working my way up)

oral temp after: 99.3

skin temp after: 52

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-20399>

### ealachan — 2012-05-01T17:30:16+0300

### quelsen — 2012-05-01T19:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-20462>

25 minutes @ 32f just didnt have time for more. darned tap water keeps getting warmer. took 4 buckets of ice to get the temp down. it is amazing to note what gets stiff when cold and what doesnt.. and while this is normally TMI i think it is scientifically valid to note that the penis stopped responding to the cold in any meaningful way weeks ago. I go naked and always have, they dont make compression garment for my size. first several days YES i thought i may never see my poor testicles again, literally they went so far up i thought i had lost them. now the scrotum does draw up but nothing ascends. The glans sometimes feels like it is getting pin pricked but sometimes not, no rhyme or reason as far as i can tell. Meanwhile feet and hands, they don't like the cold, does they precious, no they doesnt. Curiouser and Curiouser

### AmyJ — 2012-05-02T03:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-20587>

45 minutes at 64 degrees. That took 10 gallons of water from the fridge (34deg) and 4 floating 2 liter bottles of frozen water. It stayed at 64, though, I'm encouraged. I wore booties but they filled up with water, is that supposed to happen? My feet weren't particularly cold although they cramped up toward the end. Next time is a bag of ice in addition to the above. Should get me into the 50s. It seriously feels awesome, although from somewhere I heard those strange (@#$%) words that others have mentioned.  It's tough for about 30 seconds but I just jumped right in. I don't think it's possible to croak soak with 80deg tap water. Looking forward to getting acquainted with the 50s!
