### Charlotte — 2013-01-03T13:08:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69148>

ok Bobbi, here goes, just took it out of the oven to cool down.
You need for the cake:
16 eggs
400 g dark chocolate (possibly with Stevia as it brings down Carbs)
550 g peeled almond flour
peel of one fresh lemon and orange (substituted this for candid lemon peel)
2 tablespoons of cinnamon (any other spice is great too such as pumpkin pie spice but I like the cinnamon)
Stevia

separate 11 eggs and add 5 whole eggs to the yolk. Beat until very fluffy and add Stevia, (as much as you see fit) lemon and orange peel and cinnamon. Before prepare chocolate by putting it through a shredder or grater. Then add the chocolate, keeping a little bit for decoration (3 tablespoons)
let it mix for a while and then add the almond flour, 500 g. Let it stir until the batter gets dark. Meanwhile prepare the pan (european style with the side to take away) by greasing it well with butter and take 50 g of almond flour to cover the floor and the sides.
Now beat the eggwhite and fold under the batter by hand, careful not to break the eggwhites.
Put in the pan and stick in the oven. Bake at very low temperatur (max. 150 Â°C) for app. 1,5 hrs.
Take out, take away the side of the pan after a few minutes, put it on a grid to cool down. After cooling down wrap it up and keep it cool over night.

I calculated the Nutrition facts for the cake: 622,65 g of fat, 346,15 g of protein, 63,2 g of carbohydrates

For the frosting you need:
700 g of butter (Kerrygold is best as is soft without salt)
300 g of dark chocolate (same recommendation as above)
500 g of Mascarpone
Stevia
2 tablespoons of freeze dried coffee

Beat the butter until real fluffy with Stevia. Melt the chocolate in a waterbatch and add to the butter, then add the Mascarpone and the coffee. (Instead of coffee you can use any aromas, that you like such as Nutaroma, vanilla, etc.)
Let the mixture cool off a little and meanwhile cut the cake into 3 layers.
Then spread the cream onto the layers, putting one layer on top of the other and keep some for the outside. Then spread the cream on and around the cake still keeping some for the decoration. Sprinkle the chocolate bits left over from the cake on top and on the sides of the cake. Now put the remaining cream into a Piping bag (am not sure if this is the right word) and decorate little stars or whatever you like on the cake. Decorate with Mocca beans or real coffee beans.

Nutrition information for the frosting:
Fat: 910,1 g, Protein: 31,58 g, Carbs: 47,05 g.

Total Nutrition information: Fat: 1.532,8 g, Protein: 377,73 g, Carbs: 110,25.

This cake makes easily 20 pieces as it is very rich. So per piece you have: 76,64 g fat, 18,9 g protein, 5,5 g of carbs. Ratio of fat to protein: 75% fat and 25% protein.

HEY! don't blame me! It was Ealchan (whom I miss daily and wish she'd come back!) who yelled at me to (and I quote) "Just get in the damn tub!" So I did. Jason ... you got some good advice. Put the scale in the closet and forget it. This program is not about losing weight. It is about optimizing your health. They don't care that I'm beginning to feel better than I've ever felt, have more energy, etc...well they do, but they still want to know THE NUMBERS!.

I guess it's because they've been taught to think along one line, or with one mindset, and a lot of them think that is the ONLY way to think, or do something, or that because they are "DOCTORS" that they know it all, and there can't possibly be any NEW discoveries, or ways of thinking, and if there is, it must just be some "phase" or fly by night theory. Maybe the first "rule", or "commandment" of the Leptin Reset should be, "Disregard, and forget everything you've ever been told, or have ever thought were the "rules", and guidelines about becoming healthy, because the majority of them are all a bunch of bullsh!t!"

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-05T02:14:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69396>

I need a bit of help.

I'm new to face dunking. I get my water down to 53-55 degrees F but find I can't keep my face in for very long. The first time my face is fine though my need to breathe drives me to take my face out long before I would when I swim. Then when I put my face back in the water seems colder and colder each time. By about time five at 2-8 seconds each time my whole face hurts so much I can't bring myself to continue any longer. Is this normal? I'm on day 4, 5th occasion of doing it.

What am I aiming for? I don't seem to be adapting as I'm no better at it than I was on day 1.

### furface — 2013-01-05T03:00:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69398>

> **Aussie nana said**
>
> I need a bit of help.
> I'm new to face dunking. I get my water down to 53-55 degrees F but find I can't keep my face in for very long. The first time my face is fine though my need to breathe drives me to take my face out long before I would when I swim. Then when I put my face back in the water seems colder and colder each time. By about time five at 2-8 seconds each time my whole face hurts so much I can't bring myself to continue any longer. Is this normal? I'm on day 4, 5th occasion of doing it.
> What am I aiming for? I don't seem to be adapting as I'm no better at it than I was on day 1.

I had similar problems when I first began face dunking. My water was around 46 degrees F, and I could only hold my breath for 18 seconds the first time, then worked my way up to 28 seconds, each of the next five times. Yes it does leave your face with a sort of "burning" sensation, and very red, but I actually found that to be rather invigorating, energizing, and exhilarating. So refreshing.

What I found made it easier was I used the timer on my microwave oven, started at 40 seconds, began to "dunk" around 30 seconds. I didn't take a really deep breath, and hold it "HARD", instead, I took a few long, slow, breaths, then for the final one before dunking, I took an even longer, but slower, more "calm" or "relaxed" breath, almost like putting myself into a "trance" nice and relaxing. I then slowly began to count...intentionally extra slow, so that by the time I had only reached "18" or so, the timer on the microwave would go off, and 30 seconds had actually passed by. (I estimated that I had wasted 2 seconds before I actually dunked my head, so that's how I got the 28 seconds.)
A few sessions later, I was up to 45 and 50 seconds using the same method. Then about a week or so after I began, I was up to 60 and 70 seconds. What I found even "easier" instead of setting the timer for 1:10, I'd set it for 50 seconds, dunk at 43 seconds, then when the timer rang after what would be approx 40 seconds, I'd begin to count nice and slow either 20 or 30 more seconds. Much easier than to wait a full minute (which I also did a few times) for the timer to "ding".

It just takes time, and getting used to the cold water. I always make sure I have a nice cold glass of ice water before I dunk, and also make sure my mind is not on anything else. If it is, I won't last even 10 seconds.

Just breathe (before you dunk) nice and slow, deep, calm, relaxing. Don't inhale really deep then hold your breath REAL HARD. (At least that doesn't work for me).

Just give it more time, and practice, you'll get the hang of it.

### Dali Dula — 2013-01-05T09:21:36+0200

The point here is not to ever weigh yourself. The point is not to be weight obsessed. Every three months I get weighed when I see my endo. It's usually a good moment. You should see some positive progress with weight every 90 days until you stabilize close to your ideal weight. My weight has not fluctuated more than a pound or two for six months.

### freesia — 2013-01-06T05:00:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69558>

I've still been bathing around 45 minutes @ 47 F.
I can only manage that every second night due to some serious pressure on freezer real estate, and the fact it's mid summer here!

So I've listened to a couple of webinars the last 2 bath nights.

The prostate cancer one and the women's fat loss one. Neither one applies to me in any obvious way, but I got a lot out of both of them! I got the message loud and clear that CT is the best form of exercise for anyone with inflammation and whose mitochondrial energy balance is still off. It's a bonus to get that message about CT while you're actually doing it!

One of my kiddos is down to a 57 F bath! He's the one with a pioneering spirit. It was his idea to get cold adapted. He was as aware as anyone else around here that I made incredible progress last year and a lot of that was due to regular CT. He kept asking me all winter "When can I have a cold bath Mum?" and I said "Well....I don't think you'd like it but you can try one some time". Then while we were travelling somewhere in the car I heard him start a sentence from the back seat with "When I'm cold adapted..."
It was obviously something he'd been serious about! So we decided it was time he could try a bath.
My other son (10) really got started because he's so competitive and he would hate his younger brother to be out performing him in some way. He struggles more with every aspect of it, but he's done 21 baths now and he hasn't really looked like missing a single one. He has to steal himself a bit more to get into the cold water - he has a little ritual he goes through. I sense it's empowering him to be overcoming this little hurdle by himself every bath night.

They both need extra care and attention as they get out.....warmed towels at the ready, and someone to help them into their pyjamas and down to bed, then to sit with them 5 - 10 minutes until they stop shivering. My hope is that they're associating cold with being well cared for. They've both mentioned the great sleep they have afterwards.
...Nice to go to sleep feeling well cared for, and that all is right in your world! If you're ever wavering, treat \*yourself\* as you would your small person, or as you would have liked to have been treated at 8 years old.That small person is absolutely worth making your greatest effort for.

My kids are so sure they're going to be taking 50 F baths next winter, and doing the local Polar Plunge in June! I just say..."Well if you want to, you know I'll help you."
No expectations from me, but I sure am enjoying seeing my small people so keen to continue something they seem to feel is beneficial...even though they couldn't explain how it's so!

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-07T02:16:06+0200

CT yesterday included a swim at the beach, face dunking (twice), then I found myself at night time thinking about a cold bath. Now it isn't cold here at all. In fact my bathroom was about 30 C, 85F, and I felt just too hot. So without thinking I turned on the taps and without questioning hopped in the bath. I felt I needed to cool off to go to sleep in my warm bedroom - I dropped the temperature to about 26 (75 F) with air con. The water wasn't that cold, but it worked making me ready for bed.

For the first time in a long, long time I slept through my usual midnight and 2.30 wakeup times. I slept from 9pm till 4am - an hour after dusk till an hour before dawn, a much deeper sleep than I've had for a long time. Something must be working.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69796>

Yesss!!

I only get flushed really pink in the high 40's - 50F.
I watch this lovely phenomenon through ice, as it occurs on my legs. Then I check the mirror for signs all over when I get out of the water.

I came into summer with a light, all-over tan, I knew that was linked to the leptin-melanocortin pathway, and alpha MSH.
Unfortunately the hole in the ozone down here means even best winter practice (CT) does not make us completely immune to the sun's harmful effects...

But wow.

### caroline — 2013-01-08T02:42:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69820>

I have got the most amazing dark brown tan this summer. I have never, ever had a tan like this .... I don't burn and I don't peel .....awesome!

### freesia — 2013-01-08T18:08:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69944>

Presumably the longer you stay in the cold the more a-MSH you make too.
Really looking forward to increasing my CT application next winter. Ha! Looking forward to winter is not something I've had a lot of exerience with. My birthday is the first day of winter (Sthn hemisphere) and if ever asked what I'd like it was always something along the line of possum merino socks....sheepskin slippers....anything at all to keep me warm. I hated feeling cold with a vengeance!
I did re-read CT 6, and more makes sense every time I revisit it. Some of the biochemistry aspects of it I'm still struggling with. Wondering if pomc genetic downregulation is strongly associated with my illness. Have seen some suggestion (just googled) this is why people may respond well to LDN. Am I getting some of the same benefits of LDN with CT? I think maybe I am. I did actually suggest this to someone the other day.
Heat shock proteins is another potential biggie. I hope I'm going to be able to increase my understanding further in that department sometime too! { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### endless — 2013-01-08T21:23:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-69987>

I feel for you Ct-ers down under....it's abnormally hot there now, is it not?

My CT today was tough to get into....I was not looking forward to it at all. BUT after the first 4-5 uncomfortable minutes sitting I started to lean back. Took me a full 15 minutes today to get neck deep, but I stayed in for 30 so at least the upper bod got SOME chilling. Water is down to 43F-ish.

### diane — 2013-01-08T22:04:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70003>

For the first time I actually filled up the tub then got in! I'm usually a slow-fill kinda gal, but took the plunge. Plus, once the tub starts filling, I'm much more likely to get in the tub.

There were a few "words" when I first got in, but I rocked it! Stayed in for 30 minutes - a shorter one for me, but my goal was just to get in and do it!

Congratulations! Did you feel awesome afterward?

My CT was a bit shit today, no idea why, but it didn't make me cold.. So I got naked in the sun on my balcony again.. (Sorry plane passengers) I figured if the cold wasn't doing it for me, maybe the sun would. This time I only stayed out for 20 mins and only did the back half of me. I don't think i got burnt.

CT last night was awesome however.. I was still a bit damp from the beach, and when i put away all the ice and sat outside in the wind, i shivered for 30 minutes! Yay! It was exhilarating.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-09T14:21:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70058>

Yesterday was a banner day for me. I warmed up bashing two inches of ice off of my CT hole in the pond. It was sunny here in Vermont and 20 degrees...no wind. Perfect weather! I managed to stay in for four minutes in 36 degree water. My new feet protection is a pair of felted booties and another pair of woollen socks....they get disgustingly dirty, but really helped my feet and I got the full benefits of grounding on the pond bottom. I was very happy in the water soaking up the sun and the electrons. I didn't need to hurry when I got out. I put my bathrobe on and walked around outside for another ten minutes feeling warm and comfortable.

Later in the afternoon I went for a long snowshoe in our woods, found a stream and did a little foot CT sitting in the snow. That was great too. When I got home I was really warm so I had a cold shower!! I have a vague recollection of what having this kind of energy used to feel like....but it was long ago. Today, I'm not feeling up to much, but I know it's a balancing act; building vs stressing my mitochondria. However, I'm not stiff from the exercise and I was out for almost two hours going up and down hills (slowly)...clearly another sign that CT builds endurance and muscle. Apart from the barn chores I do everyday, I've been keeping a lid on exercise waiting for my adrenals to heal...and now learning more about the mitochondria, I see it is cellular energy that we are working with all the time too.I think the ATP in my cells was doing a new kind of dance yesterday!

### freesia — 2013-01-09T16:45:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70079>

Diane, that slow fill method, I always had another name for it!

...t....o.....r....t....u....r....e

30 minutes is great! Overcoming any hesitancy to just get in takes some mental strength. Once you've mastered that bit I reckon you're away laughing.
My personal choice words - courtesy of ealachan - "Holy suffering seamonkeys!" Haven't used it in a while but it's nice to know it's there if I need it. Last night as I got in, and I still have occasional slight hesitancy, I just thought "need more alpha MSH".

Ashryn - it's kind of tricky meshing winter and summer stuff in the one day. I think you're going to hit the ground running by the time our Southern winter comes around!

Seahorse, 36 degrees...bashing through two inches of ice. I just do not know what to say! That's like those (mostly older generation?) people do in Eastern Europe. I read somewhere, the next generation down prefers gym workouts instead, and insulation from the elements....but it's doing them no favours.
I'm going to tell my kids about you. They'll think you're Superwoman.
My neoprene socks have been irritating me - actually I really dislike them. I love the idea of felted wool booties instead.
I learned feltmaking last year. It's the first fun thing I did after I got well enough to put energy into something creative. I'm inspired after reading your post.....can't find water 36 degrees...but I might have a go at making some booties!

I had an interesting experience grounding in the bath last night. For the first time I forgot to connect the grounding strap before I got in the water. 15 minutes in, I realised I wasn't connected so I snapped the cord which is grounded via the cold tap, onto my wrist strap. There was definitely a subtle change, felt first in the peripheral receptors. It was enough to convince me there's a difference.
(I have had significant effects already from grounding in general but will write about that sometime in another thread.)

### Shijin13 — 2013-01-09T18:36:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70098>

It's a bit nippy here in my stock tank today. Why you ask? My tank is a lovely 41 deg. The ambient air temp is a schorching 47 which I did like. I'm in for at least 20 maybe 25 if I delay picking Chaos & Havoc back until right at 6!

Getting my mitochondrial exercise

### Optimalbound — 2013-01-09T19:13:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70104>

Due to Christmas etc., I hadn't done any CT for three weeks until last Saturday. Also, some wheat and refined carbohydrates found their way into my diet during these past weeks and that was all it took for some bloating and reduced mental acuity to be back to a noticeable degree. It felt almost as if I was back to the state I used to consider "normal". Yuck. Ah well, at least this was an educational N=1 that will make me much less resistant to social pressure (real or perceived) in the future.

Friday: Filled up my tub to the rim, only to discover that the tap water temperature was down to a chilly 6 Â°C (43 Â°F ). Stared at the water for a few seconds before I decided it was too cold and also too late in the evening to have the time to warm it up a bit. Chickened out and instead recharged my motivation by re-reading some of Dr. Kruse's CT blogs and also this thread. Thanks to everyone for sharing your experiences and lovely pictures and recipes!

So, with renewed determination; these are my cold tub CT sessions this last week:
Saturday: 30 min at 9 Â°C (48 Â°F)
Sunday: 35 min at 9 Â°C
Monday: 55 min at 8 Â°C (46 Â°F)
Tuesday: 45 min at 8 Â°C
Wednesday: Came home late and only did facedunks..

It feels really, really good to be back on track!

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-09T22:42:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70116>

I've been really enjoying this thread lately.
I haven't taken a cold bath since the fall, but I spend lots of time outside in the freezing cold, with minimal layers. I also take cold showers after my swims several times a week. This morning I was getting myself a glass of water when I noticed how cold the water was. All I could think was, "mitochondrial exercise!" So, while my daughters were happily busy, I stole away to my bathroom and filled up a tub of 42-degree F water. I have a lovely, huge tub, so I let it fill most of the way up and then just went for it. (I figure since I'm doing the cold weather MovNat in a couple weeks, there is no reason for hesitation at all.) I soaked for 30 minutes and it felt fantastic. Later, I went out on my deck and soaked up the sunlight for about 25 minutes, enough to get tan lines. My daughters have dance twice a week, tonight being one night, so I hung out with other Moms (who are unknowingly CTing) in the freezing cold for 3 hours. I do not wear long underwear or wooly socks. Just light pants and my light tech jacket with a tee-shirt underneath. I have been trying to make the most of the cold temps we've been having here, and it shows. While ice skating 2 weeks ago, most of us could barely handle the frigid 7-degree F temps for the several hours are kids were down there, but the next week, when it warmed up to 24, I was sitting outside for over an hour in my light clothing and it felt balmy. I had let my CT lapse for a while, and now I'm back on and it feels so good!

Also regarding the mention of kids and CT, I just wanted to add that for generations children have been told to "put on a jacket, hat, mittens, etc." but I never do that with my kids. I always make sure to bring all their warm clothes, but I let them choose when to wear it or not. They are definitely cold adapted as they wear mostly tank tops and shorts all winter long. They will run around for hours in very little clothing and never complain, plus it's their choice! I love how cold hardy my kids are.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-10T07:43:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70138>

I think that CT is natural to kids. They never like to wear shoes or socks no matter how cold the floor. I CTed all my childhood years in the winter, never knowing. The temperature comes down around here to easily to -4 F and even -20 F. Still we kids would be outside until after dark skiing, snowsledging, playing, throwing off our jackets because we were sweating, then cold standing around watching others being wet from the sweat and every night I came home with blue lips, white skin and my numb toes were in pain, after they defrosted.

### caroline — 2013-01-10T16:42:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70206>

I was just in the mountains in Kyrgyzstan and our gorgeous, young guide kept peeling off layers of clothes until he was down to a lite t shirt and shorts and rolling around in the snow. I am positive he would never have heard the words C/T. He also always swam in the cold rivers. He was amazingly fit and strong - did I mention gorgeous?? and very lean with very low body fat .... I really miss the mountains

didn't remember liking snow that much before either

### caroline — 2013-01-11T18:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70344>

It is a funny thing - I used to love the beach and ocean when I was younger - we spent so many years \[when the kids were young\] going to the Bahamas a couple of times a year - thought I was in heaven. It was a magic combination of the ocean and the sun and the great calypso music..... but then I didn't used to go - mostly I think because I looked disgusting in a bathing suit....pretty sad. But now I LOVE it so much - the ocean kind of empowers me or something and I go everyday...
Gretch - the kids would love it - they would think they were in paradise! We became friends with some guys in Nassau who owned a supper club and we used to take the girls from the time they were really little and we would dance the nite away - absolutely pure magic!. People used to stop us in the street and say hello because they had seen the girls at the niteclub dancing. My youngest was known far and wide as the limbo queen! Those are such treasured memories for our family...

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-11T20:18:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70355>

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-13T20:32:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70605>

I'm back to ordinary cold (tepid) baths again. I had my first bath with ice on my belly on Friday and though my belly was fine my upper arms came up in horrendous urticaria - 10cm across. I had gone from face dunking to ice on trunk, to cold water baths at 80F and then to adding the ice and felt great till the next day when the itching started. Now I can't do any more of that for a while - well not with my arms in anyway. Now perhaps if I'm really careful...? I had hoped with all the fish oil I've been swallowing over the last five years and with eating mussels for breakfast and fish for lunch for four weeks that I'd be OK. But obviously not. Humph...

I rode a few waves but between the full head dunking at that temp and the muscle effect my ass was kicked pretty quick. there was strong undertoe also so I only lasted about five min. It was really fun while it lasted.

### caroline — 2013-01-14T01:17:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70623>

That is amazing Dali! I am trying to plan ahead for winter here - do you take extra clothes with you and change? I have about a 20 min walk to the rock pool - so I can't think what to do when it is cold - take a track suit and just pop it on over wet gear?

### Charlotte — 2013-01-14T05:49:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70639>

this website is doing strange things today. When I click on the CT in Forum, it takes me to the CT blog. As if it wants me to read it again. Maybe I will.
I had a funny CT yesterday. Actually I had two, one in the late morning and another in the evening after dark. The first was uneventful as most (if you want to neglect the fact that it is brutally cold), but the water is getting real cold now. It's around 27,3 Â°F now, so a slight freeze over now in the morning despite having an air cushion on top. After I had written to Dr. Kruse about my skin looking wrinkled and like jelly beans in a bag on my underarms, he wrote back that I need to do CT. So, back into the pool. I was just starting to shiver real bad, when my neighbour blew off a rocket (probably a left over from New Years eve) and I first wanted to get pissed because it was so loud, then there was this really beautyful shower of colored stars and I started to laugh. "Thats just for me" I thought and that he could not have chosen the moment better.
I am now trying to fit 2 CTs into my schedule, which is difficult. The early mornings for me is not a good idea, as I need to get to work early. So I am thinking about the lunch break. My office is only 4 minutes by car from home, so it would be manageable. Since my doc has said it is time to exercise a bit (LR has been broken, at least thats what the labs showed), so I started with light cardio on my crosstrainer and CT afterwards. This I plan to do in the evening after work, if it is not too late, as otherwise there is no thought of sleep. So far, I did maybe 2-3 a week, mostly on the weekends, but that is not enough, I realize.

### Dali Dula — 2013-01-14T08:45:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70648>

> **caroline said**
>
> That is amazing Dali! I am trying to plan ahead for winter here - do you take extra clothes with you and change? I have about a 20 min walk to the rock pool - so I can't think what to do when it is cold - take a track suit and just pop it on over wet gear?

I like to air dry for a bit after a swim.I never feel cold immediately after CT so i extend it for a while in the cool air. At the ocean because it s public I keep my wet shorts on, but would prefer not to have wet clothes on. After I am dry I remove shorts put my regular clothes back on and go about my day feeling amazing.

### Linz — 2013-01-14T12:45:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70672>

CT after dark, I find so much easier just lying in the cold wet (nearly freezing) grass. Maybe not as intense as an ice bath but such a calm feeling afterwards.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-14T14:33:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70686>

We've had a Jan thaw this week and for the first time in a while I haven't had to break ice on my plunging spot. The air was a balmy 48 degrees but the water was actually colder than it's been before. I took my thermometer in (I have to entertain myself) and it came to 31 degrees! The ice is melting into the pond and making the water colder than it has been before...this colder water will then sink to the bottom. Since I'm in a shallow area the water is cold from the top right to the bottom now. It didn't feel colder though and I was able to stay in for four and 1/2 minutes...my new record. Feet are doing so much better in their felt boots and socks and I'm not getting worried about the white numbness anymore....so now I just going to try to increase my time very slowly. My arms feel the cold first.

I am doing a lot more running around outside to get some more earthing and then dashing up and down the stairs inside once I come back in the house. I stay in my bathing suit until the shivering starts which is usually about 20 minutes after I get out. I can shiver without really feeling unpleasantly cold, so I just go about my day with a hat on and forget about it. My legs turned a deepish purple for a short time once they stopped being bright red... this is good???

I used to love running sprints and swimming sprints before I hit puberty....it's going to be fun to revisit that feeling as my mitochondria get it together!

### furface — 2013-01-15T00:42:17+0200

After taking a few days off from CT...too much on the mind, and waiting for a possible Skype video call from someone special. However I noticed that for the first time since I began CT, whenever the cold water hit my chest tonight, it felt like dozens of needles hitting my chest. Not painful so much as annoying. **Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70819>

I was CTing too close to bedtime though. I've moved it back so I can warm up earlier, and I seem to sleep better.

### ashryn — 2013-01-15T09:14:40+0200

Woohoo! CT today for 3hours belly ice, CFB, aircon on 21C and damp bathres from swimming.. And I'm actually shivering right now in ambient (but humid) air temp of about 26C.. And I'm feeling sleepy enough to go to bed early and have a chance at 8.5 hours sleep..

I'm back baby!

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-15T12:20:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70864>

The water is 42 degrees coming out of the tap, so I've been taking advantage of it. It never got above 12 degrees F yesterday, and my family definitely saw the crazy in me when I filled up the tub and soaked for 30 minutes. When I got out, I realized I only had about 40 minutes before I had to go to a meeting, and I needed to shovel the snow on the deck and stairs before leaving. So immediately after getting out, I threw on my clothes, a hat, gloves, and a jacket and went out in the 7-degree F weather to shovel for 30 minutes. I realized it was a nice way to actually transition out of the tub because it was so much colder outside that it made the tub feel warmer, if only psychologically.

### furface — 2013-01-15T13:21:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-70871>

Thanks for the info on that, Anne. As I said, hopefully it was a one time occurrence.

I'm such an emotional based person that if there is any little thing bothering me, it seems to throw everything out of whack. Esp if it's something very personal or emotional. Not that it will cause me to throw my hands up and give up (at least not yet), but it does make me begin to think at times, "Why bother if nothing else in my life seems to be working out?"

Maybe I'm just being too impatient and want to see some actual results for the effort and sacrifice I'm putting in. (And I don't consider "how I'll feel in the "long run"" etc as results.) I want to see some results NOW!! I am not impatient, I just hate to wait!

Funny thing is, when everything else seems to be going okay, I feel great! But if one little thing goes wrong, or things don't work out the way I would like them to, then it just seems like a waste of time and effort. I realize that things aren't always going to run smoothly, but it's like just when I think things are headed in the right direction...even with the odd monkey wrench thrown in, which I fully expect, something always happens to screw up my psyche.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-16T12:59:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71048>

First thing this morning, my 7-year old daughter and I took a hike with our dog in the 7-degree F weather. The sun was rising and we hiked up to the top of hill. We found a great patch of untouched snow and I plopped down in it. She soon followed suit and proceeded to do snow angels and roll arounds in the snow. We both had very thin clothes on...she was wearing light cotton leggings, and I had on a pair of thin cotton pants. We both laid on our backs and looked at the sky, and she said, "this is so cool, I feel like I'm part of the sky." Our dog thought it was truly great and was leaping over us and playing.

We walked back down the trail and observed all the different tracks in the snow: mice, rabbit, fox, coyote, dog, people, chipmunk.

### freesia — 2013-01-16T13:26:40+0200

Your environment sounds magical.....how lucky is your seven year old!!

Lauren's example, back there too, with the kids and the clams in the sea.

The kind of connections they make and carry forth after experiencing something like that..I can't even imagine.

Future proofing kids...that's what it sounds like to me!

I wish there was more snow here. Because I loved rolling around a laying out with 12 inches on the ground a few weeks ago. I have been stealing electrons today at my local starbucks. Working inside and it just so happens that they have a tile floor. Grounding can happen anywhere.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-17T17:18:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71268>

I had the coolest CT session yet! I work in an old school building that has been converted into offices. The building sits on the edge of a gorgeous canyon. Since May, I have been sitting cliff-side 2 to 3 days a week where I meditate and journal. Today, I went to my usual cliff-side spot to CT. There is about 8 inches of snow on the ground, and I happen to be on the shady side of the canyon, so I knew there'd be enough snow to work with. Dressed in a short running skirt that I hitched up, I sat down, legs out, and buried my entire lower body in the snow. (Because I'm still close to the building, and today was my first CT experiment at this location, I decided not to bury my full body, but I think I can find a more secluded location to try a full-body snow burial tomorrow..) I sat there, meditated, observed all the avian life around me, and stayed buried in the snow for 25 minutes. Once I got up, I went to another rock where the snow had melted completely, and sunned myself to dry off for another 15 minutes. I have to say that I felt such an odd variety of things: slightly mischievous, exhilarated, energized, calmed, giddy, tingly, cold, proud, and generally awesome. Now I'm sitting back in my office, with my shoes off, skin still cold 1.5 hours later, and I can't wait to do it again! I hope we get more snow so I can keep this up all winter.

### Claudia — 2013-01-17T20:51:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71314>

I'm having trouble getting back into CT. I do live near ocean and bay (closer to bay) on Long Island. Last year when I started CT in March, I started with face dunks, then cold showers,and then submerged in the tub with some ice packs...I got hives really really bad twice where the ice packs were so I stopped all ice and just did cold water. I went in the bay twice in April, once by myself and once with some fellow forum people who live near by. Then, I kept that up in May and up until October, sometimes daily swimming. Summer was easy, of course, and not very cold. I haven't done any CT since then. I just don't feel up to it. When I think of the boring, cold tub, it depresses me and I cringe. I can't even do the cold shower part. Yet, I do feel somewhat cold adapted. I don't have to blast the heat, I wear less clothing outside in the cold and keep the house at 65. Sometimes I am hot while sleeping and shake off the covers, this was never the case before starting to CT.

How to get back in? I would feel so much better if I could go to the beach with others to do it. It just doesn't feel safe to be alone and I think it might be too cold for me having not done it since October.

### Dali Dula — 2013-01-17T22:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71326>

Just go to the beach. Go to the beach be ready to swim. You don't have to swim. Take off your shoes walk to the water.Walk along the water up to your knees for awhile and see where you want to go from there. I was swimming at Jones beach last weekend. 41 deg. Avoid head dunking at that temp. If the water is calm go about waist deep ad dunk down. If you do any part of this you will be happy. The ocean is different everyday. Visit often.

### caroline — 2013-01-17T23:34:08+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71362>

### Glen — 2013-01-18T08:02:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71368>

Cold-Adapted? Yes
CT Baths or Icing or Both? Baths with ice
Duration?45minutes 5-6 times a week
Temperature? 53-55 Degrees
When do you CT? After work
How long have you been doing CT? Since Christmas, say 4 weeks

I listened to Jack a few nights back talking about grounding in the ocean. I happen to live on the Atlantic coast in Yarmouth, Nova Scotia. I planned on doing summer swims, after adapting in my tub for the winter, but decided I needed to get in the ocean now. Took my Kindle down to the beach and walked around in the water (knee deep) while I read. Jack said 20-30 minutes was optimal, however, with the water at 40 degrees I decided to exit at 12 minutes. No pain, but a lot of shooting electrical sensations. Figured it might be best to work my way up to 20 minutes. Plan on doing 4-5 ice baths and a couple of ocean walks per week. Mind you, school is cancelled today (snow day, I'm a teacher), so maybe I'll do both. Kids at school are very interested in what I'm doing and love to talk to me about it, a few have tried the cold showers. I had my daughter take a picture of my feet after my ocean walk and I plan on projecting them onto my screen Monday morning to greet students as they come in. Feet were very red and my veins were popping out a good quarter inch, very scarry looking. By the way, I'm a P.E. teacher so can't put a 100% guarantee on my spelling.

### Inger — 2013-01-18T14:09:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71422>

Yesterday there was a article in the local newspaper about 3 old ladies doing winter baths in a sea close by. The hole in the ice is hold open by the government so people can ice bath there. The article told that they swim 1 minute every morning at 8 AM, with gloves, shoes and hat. After starting doing this the arthritic pain is gone, rheumatic hurting hands are just fine, no flus, great health. It was nice to read! There are many people doing this here, and they eat a SAD diet and it helps anyways!

I did 3 minutes today in my own ice hole, I did it with socks, gloves and a hat. My feet did not hurt, I made sure they were warm before I got in. But my elbows started to hurt instead..

! I really wonder how AKMan does so longs icebaths.. The water is 32 degrees F. The air temp was -0 degree F!

### jenafraserrmt@gmail.com — 2013-01-18T16:54:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71470>

Awesome!! I live half a block to the ocean in the heart of the city (Vancouver BC) (so not ideal to swim in) so I drove 10 mins to long sandy beaches and just walked along the waterline in 46F/8C ocean water - then without realizing it I ended up just past my knees!!! It was so beautiful and so envigorating (energizing!!!) I didn't realize I stayed in walking up and down the beach for 40 mins!!! Walking back to the car, my shins were burning warm! Took only 20 mins to warm up!!

And I haven't done CTing since May last year! Back on track though

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-18T18:20:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71491>

Jena...that ocean water must have felt great.

Inger...I stayed in over four minutes today in 32 degree water and the outside air was just around zero...so we're have almost exactly the same set up despite being on different continents...that's kind of wonderful. Don't you feel it is easier to get in when the water is warmer than the air? To be honest getting in isn't ever a problem once I've decided I'm going to go out and do it. I've been sitting on the bottom of the pond the last few days because my hole is getting smaller and smaller so the water is shallow..It takes about 1/2 hour to break up all the ice and scoop it out if we're getting temps below zero. Earlier in the week it was really warm and no ice formed.

I always wear socks and wool booties now...they've been working really well. Inger... I think AKMan and Dahli Dula and Dr. Kruse (of course) are benefitting from being men. They are all doing amazing CT all the time....it's just going to take longer for us women to adapt to the cold at that deep level. That's my take away from the Women and Fat loss webinar.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-18T22:19:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71525>

Repeated the snow CT today. I went to my usual Friday heavy lifting, then headed straight to the cliff edge and buried myself in the snow. I had less time today than I had yesterday, and ended up fully buried from the waist down, and then laying down in the snow from the waist up, put snow on my belly and chest just to make sure I had a lot of coverage. CT session was awesome. My head was under a baby ponderosa and the sky was amazingly New Mexico blue. The temps today were much, much warmer than they've been all week. I think the temp was 45 when I ended up outside. I managed to stay under the snow for about 15 minutes. At the time, I felt concerned that it wasn't long enough. However, after shivering for 2 whole hours afterwards, I'm not so sure! Today, I hurt. This is the first time I've I've ever experienced any challenging side-effect from CT. My skin hurts everywhere I was in the snow for about 1 to 2 cm deep. I feel like I am bruised everywhere where the snow was covering. It is very interesting. It has decreased substantially, since earlier, but still significant.

### ashryn — 2013-01-18T22:57:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71527>

Chrysanthemama, I do my CT with ice as well..(I assume its the same as snow) and I have the same bruising feeling you mention but only in the muscle areas.. Chest, neck and upper back.. It doesn't happen to my belly.. I assume thats all the fat. The feeling is similar to the way I feel after deep tissue massage, so I guess it is ok. I must say that my chronically tight shoulder and neck muscles don't seem as tight since doing regular CT but I am hoping the bruised feeling is me activating the brown fat and converting white fat.. And that it will stop feeling bruised as time goes on. I'd be interested to know how yours goes.. And whether anyone else who has been in the game longer has experienced this... Does it go away eventually?

### freesia — 2013-01-20T19:24:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71915>

So, evening icebaths continue...and I feel warm in them. Body's doing what it should.

The only problem is (since adding earthing?) the baths seem too stimulating for me to be taking them in the late evening. So I'm changing my schedule and planning to take them earlier...more time that way for warm up before bed.

I also added some 5 star CT to my weekend!
40 minutes wading in the sea (South Pacific Ocean) yesterday and today. One of my kiddos joined me, and he loved it too.

### caroline — 2013-01-20T19:31:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71918>

What was the temp Anne? bonding in the ocean - what fun!

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-20T21:01:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-71939>

I tried swimming at our beaches today - twice, but the currents and undertows made the sea treacherous. I went both morning and evening. In the evening I was with my two grandchildren and one of their friends who was much younger, only 6 years or so. We nearly lost him when a wave dragged him out. It was so strong I wouldn't have been able to save him. I was so scared by it I demanded we all move up the beach where we built sand castles for an hour at the point where we only got the big waves wash over us. Then we sat on the grass while the children ate fish and chips while the sun went down. So the CT wasn't cold like those of you in the northern hemisphere know cold, but we all ended up chilled by our standards and no doubt thoroughly grounded.

today I began adding CT to my morning routine - stock tank was a brisk 29deg, ambient air temp was 18 w/a windchill of 5deg. I lasted 6mins... going to try to hop in tonight after work... I also plan to go lay on the ground outside tonight before bed...

Holy cow - that is nippy!! You are one strong motivated woman - Gretchen - you are always going to land on your feet and your new surroundings will surprise and excite you beyond belief.......everything will fall into place for you - absolutely no freakin doubt about that!!

### Inger — 2013-01-23T08:05:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72484>

Endless.. inspired by you I tok a pic from my hole too..lol
I did 7 minutes. But it must have been too much because after I had to take a warm shower as my heart felt not so good somehow..huh! I was a bit chilly already going in.. what is not so good as I warm up much worse then I realize. IDK I have this sensitive broken heart too and I have no hearts right now to eat I in fact had not for weeks.. So not as much Q10 as I need. But I do it slow and I do some hot shower after if I feel like it..lol Felt awesome though!
I had to put on a bikini too, as on the other side were those workers working on some digging in he earth...

I had socks and gloves and a nice lambswool hat I found in my mothers stuff that nobody uses.. great for CT!

Some days it is so cold the hole has frozen with thick ice and I have no time (going to work or something) to break it open. Then I roll in the snow. I really like it! I do only short but it makes me feel so very energized every time! I munch on some snow too when there is new snow.. there is something I like very much about snow. I love the taste too..

I've gone in when no one was around, usually I tell my husband when I'm about to go in. I'm staying out of the pond for a few days...we are having -30 degrees with wind chill. That's the kind of cold that gives you frost bite really fast! The water would be fine, but my exposed skin would suffer.
I've tried to get out and walk around in my new moccasins for grounding/ct.

Love the pic Inger!

### freesia — 2013-01-23T23:19:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72620>

Last winter I never got in if my body temp was low or my fingernails blue - both indications to me that I was already struggling to thermoadapt to ambient temps in that moment... let alone ice water ones.

I'm not sure about the feeling around the heart -- is it a "gripping" feeling? Make sure you don't push too hard girls....I can't help but worry about you if you are having those feelings, even though my worry may be misplaced!
Of course, I think you're all probablky much much stronger than I was last Winter (Sthn hemisphere) but just a reminder to be watchful, and careful. xxx

Caroline, I'm not in Gretchen's league here, but if my water temperature is under 42 I leave the bathroom door open so my husband can be my spotter. Usually I close the door because -- confession time -- the noise of him doing the dishes in the kitchen interferes with how well I can hear my laptop!!! (Yes I know I am spolit.)

I love the pics you have shared......everyone looks like they're in their element.

Beautiful, inspiring nature pics!

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-01-24T00:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72625>

Inger, have you ever heard of the herb Hawthorn? The latin genus is Crataegus and it is specific to heart issues, and considered safe for many conditions, including a broken heart, figuratively. A respected Finnish herbalist, Henriette Kress, has some basic information about it: http://www.henriettesherbal.com/blog/hawthorn-heart.html

It's possible you can read her work in Finnish and get a better understanding rather than translation.

Hearing you speak about your heart, it has come to my mind several times.

### Inger — 2013-01-24T04:05:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72633>

Oh yes, Chrysanthemama, we have hawthorn berries in the garden and I ate them almost every day in fall and late fall! I love them! No more there though, all are gone.. I think. Lets see.. I am going outside and checking under the snow.. there might still be a few there! The birds cannot eat them as the bush is so thorny! Thanks for the reminder! And thank you for the link, too! Wow.. so many Angels here on this forum..

It is just in this dark winter as I have gotten to feel the issue with my heart again at times. Some additional emotional stress might be a factor but I do suspect very strong all the time I am inside now, and my wireless computer. In summer I always use my leptop laying or sitting on grass in garden.. I did not know about grounding but I do not felt bad from it like I do now when I use my leptop. I thought it was the raw butter I was eating but now I slowly start to think it must be that I am more depleted from electrons because all my computer time (this is the first winter I use wireless internet too..). In summer I am outside barefoot all the time.. now not.. it is just too cold. I can do better though! My heart as my weak spot I feel it there first. But I MUST read all the stuff and learn..lol I try to limit the computer time though, all I can. At least until I get the grounding done!

The cold and my heart.. I guess when I am a bit too long it feels like my heart never warm up, it feels not good.. a bit like "panic" even if I have no panic. so weird. But it goes away when I do a hot shower and am warm again. It is the same feeling I got when I had a glass of Malbec and then did quite long CT right after last summer.
And yes, I think Anne is so right on that it is better to do CT when warm. I have it quite chilly in my appartement (right now 57 degree F) and sometimes I am already a bit cooled when I go into the hole. It is much easier to warm up when I am warm before! I do have taken a hot shower a few times before going in..lol it felt great too! First hot shower and then cold icehole. Made it much easier! Or after doing housework and getting so warm. I really think one should be warm when jumping in, or it might be a too big stressor for a body that already is stressed out. At least such low temps. Maybe i am totally wrong though..lol But I listen to my heart and it says like Anne says too.. to be careful and baby steps. At least as long as I cannot indulge in raw heart..lol

But, I already know what to do!

So I am not worried one bit as I know why! And I know the solutions.
When I get my next pay, I will get some earthing done, so I can be on my computer earthed, and I am going to try to talk my parents into getting a landline-internet as they already have landline phone. It is just they kind of believe Internet is from the devil and have not wanted it until yet but I am sure I am going to get their mind turned around.. slowly..lol (I am quite good in doing that..ha I do it slow and with much love and all of the sudden I get what I want..lol)
I will buy some heart! Heart saves me always. I will get some CoQ10 too, next weekend I am visiting Charlotte in Germany - she has invited me!- and she have buyed bulk CoQ10m and I get it so much cheaper from her she is an Angel..! Is always good to have if you run out of heart as I have done last months. Or I might take both..lol

I just had some weird smoothie for breakfast. Raw elk liver and kidney I found in my freezer! It was yuck yuck yucky but I bet there was lots of CoQ10 in it so I do not care! I just hold my nose and took some sips of my delicious coffee right after. I have forgotten I has quite a lot of it from fall, I am going to make me such a smoothie every morning now! When on a tight budget, it saves me totally!

### diane — 2013-01-24T09:29:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72644>

I hope you and Charlotte have a lovely time together! So jealous!

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-24T09:30:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72645>

I second the Hawthorn berries! I've been drinking Hawthorn tea all fall because of some Atrial Fib. issues. What you guys are talking about with your hearts...I've had that same feeling too, I do think we need to be careful.

Now that my feet seem to be able to take the colder waters (as long as they're in wool) it's my upper arms that start to feel weird. I start by getting in up to my neck and then rise up a bit when my arms get that strange supper quick tightening feeling in them at the skin level....that's the only way I can describe it. The rest of me feels fine, but I watch for the hypothermia all the time. I know I'm getting a lot of benefits from my CT and when I get out I feel great, but there is a long way to go...I HAVE to be warmed up to even consider going in these days. So feeding the horses, shovelling poop and running up and down from the barn gets me in the mood.

Today is another super cold day here, -20 with wind chill colder than that....the ice is getting thicker and thicker but I'll work on that once it warms up a bit. Just being outside is a nice way to CT on a day like this. We had a high wind earlier in the week and there was a light powder snow ...the wind picked up the snow and made hundreds of little rolling snow balls on our field... the scientific name according to the lads down at the village store: ...."snow rollers"

### endless — 2013-01-26T00:57:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72681>

I guess I could try some hawthorn tea as well. This

> The cold and my heart.. I guess when I am a bit too long it feels like my heart never warm up, it feels not good.. a bit like "panic" even if I have no panic. so weird.

totally describes what I was feeling! Except I also get it at other times as well. Often when I am driving, so I wonder if it is related to higher EMFs.

### RayB — 2013-01-27T01:50:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-72788>

Hi all,
We been having beautiful weather lately. 70 and sunny for the last 3 days. I tried something different with my CT. I did full immersion (up to the ear lobes anyway) for 1 hour in 46 degrees. I mounted my bike before the shivers set in and did sprints for about 45 minutes. Never shivered. So, I came back and did it again. Same result. I was weird at first, my legs were a bit stiff and it was hard to get them going. When I did warm up it felt like I could rip the cranks off.

### Cowboy — 2013-01-29T01:47:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-73093>

I had a wonderfull CT session today. 25 minutes at 48 degrees. All but my head, hands and toes in the water. Been out of the water 15 minutes and my feet are cold, but my keyboarding is ok. Last session was on Friday and slept great both friday and Saturday night. Last night not so good. Very dark room and nothing to eat after 7PM, and still poor sleeping. Unusual for me not to be able to get back to sleep after waking at 3AM.

### freesia — 2013-02-03T07:45:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-73899>

I've been away, and limited to cold showers for CT the past few days. Nope. Didn't like that. Not one bit.
But I'm not going to dwell on it because I'm home now and there's 80lbs of ice with my name on it in my freezer. Happy day!

We just drove home through the geothermal centre of NZ. It's midsummer so today the idea of sitting in a sulfur hotpool didn't appeal...but come winter we'll definitely be returning for a holiday up there.
I'm determined to try and get some of what those snow monkeys have got going on. Bet I smile more than those guys while I'm getting it too!
Cold adapted, sitting in a sulfur pool with freezing ambient temps chest up -- in my case it will have to be an ice vest. That's going to do something special right? I think Jack mentioned the effect the other day. What I'm wondering is whether the same effect comes regardless of whether you're cold adapted or not. And is it just the heat/cold contrast or does the mg sulfate add something extra special....hmmm...
Can't wait to try! ETA: OK I found it - it's a Fourier effect? I'm going to try and read up about that.

I've been on the receiving end of some _gentle mocking_ for my efforts to continue CTing through summer. Some people, I think by nature are so quick to look for whats wrong with something....that they completely miss what might actually be right...
If you're chronically unwell, and neuro or other inflammation plays a part, a few good winters could really speed some serious healing. The ways and means by which it does so are in the CT blogs.
But the real proof is in the actioning of it.

Hope the _REAL_ winter CTers are doing well too!

### ashryn — 2013-02-03T08:22:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-73907>

Went in the ocean for a couple hours this morning, and now spot icing chest shoulders, back and belly.. It is quite uncomfortable on my back.. Like there's not enough fat for it to go numb any more.

Anyone have stories/recommendations for heavy lifting with CT? Maybe I could just do a few push-ups and squats to get the muscles working..

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-03T14:43:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-73920>

Anne...I think fournier effect sounds really wonderful. I wish I could duplicate it somehow. I missed more than a week of CT outside because of the weather being too brutal, but went in again the day before yesterday. Facing into the wind with frozen snow pellets hitting my face.... I've been feeling colder in general so wondering if my thyroid needs some more help. I'm pretty sure my core temp. is getting too low which is why I can only do short CT sessions. I did a longer session in an indoor bath at 44 degrees and my core temp still went down a lot....I'm not getting the furnace turned up properly yet...

I've tried lying on my grounding sheets afterwards just to increase my time. Now that they are connected to the earth I'm not feeling the weird painful aches. Slept well tool

### freesia — 2013-02-04T01:40:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74005>

Seahorse that sounds rough. There's obviously something going on. Getting in to really cold temps (incl 44 F) when your body temp is low is more than I'd be willing to do.

I can't remember how long I'd been CTing before I consistently got the furnace switching on. I even got warm anticipating a bath. I think it was maybe 3 or 4 weeks in, after ice bathing every one or two days.

I did have some really terrible afterdrops, 6 - 9 hour warm ups and other fun things associated with the whole adapting shenanigans, but I still moved forward a little bit each day. (I needed a lot of support though.)
Maybe I was lucky that my furnace kicked in so readily, or that my own particular set of problems didn't get in the way too much? I bought bitter melon but found I didn't need it.

I don't know if it's relevant, but I came down in temps _very_ gradually, using a relatively clinical approach because I was pretty unwell, over 2 - 3 months from 60 F to mid 40s F.

In that first couple of months I used to yawn so much and so intensely when the afterdrop hit, that I felt like I was nearly passing out! I asked at the time and people suggested it was likely a thyroid issue.
The good news is that whatever the yawning indicated, it _completely_ resolved.

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-04T21:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74121>

On another thread I just read that acetyl l carnitine can change thyroid med absorption...I'm thinking this might be whats going on...but JK said to wait for EMF4.

I got good and warm yesterday doing some active chores and then went in the pond for five minutes. Then I kept being active afterwards and I didn't get that really core cold feeling...in fact I felt great yesterday, so maybe it was the lack of CT that set things spirally down. It could be all that's keeping me together!! So I'm going to get in every day and see what happens in a week....plus I'm going to increase my thyroid a tiny bit.

I had the furnace going this summer and fall, so I know what it feels like and I just kept going in the pond as it got colder and colder, so I didn't do any massive jumps in temp.
The yawning things is oxygen isn't it...your brain is trying to get more...that's what I've heard.

### freesia — 2013-02-04T21:42:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74123>

Well oxygen was my first thought there at the time but someone liked to a discussion elsewhere that suggested it could be thyroid. Oxygen still makes best sense to me.
I'm glad to hear your furnace "issue"  is much more likely just a temporary setback SeaHorse!! I didn't realise you'd been at this so long.
I remember last winter I picked up a virus from one of my kids and my body temp went lower for several days making CT too difficult...then when I got back in the water it was hard to pick up at the low temps I'd been doing. It hurt right down to my bones, but only the once.

I wonder what EMF 4 contains then? I'm picking up ATP recycling - OxPhos maybe. D-ribose? Hmmm.
Interesting about the possibility of l-carnitine interfering. I hope you get to the bottom of it soon and get to enjoy your pond and it's magic again asap!

### freesia — 2013-02-06T22:07:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74479>

I spent 40 minutes wading in the sea with my dog and one child yesterday afternoon. There had been a storm and we had to wade through thick seaweed. That took a leap of faith! I kept imagining all kinds of creatures were wrapping themselves around my legs! Regardless, we came back from our sea walk feeling more energised.
There was a guy on the beach harvesting wild green lipped muscles that had also been washed ashore in the storm.
Spent an hour in a 46 F icebath later, and re watched the Jan webinar. Realised I could have been sitting in a warm bath for all I knew. (Except warm baths make me drowsy.)
....There's the slight prickly sensation on the skin and unless you really analyse things, it could be hot or it could be cold! Felt great after, but got out of the bath a bit too late. Slept all night cool.
Happy CTer here.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-08T00:12:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74712>

My CT today was unintentionally cold. It is unseasonably warm today, and so I bought twice the normal amount of ice. But I put a bit too much water in the tub, and was dismayed to see the temp was only 46F. So I brought out the frozen bottles of water and such, and cleaned out the ice machine. And I put in the epsom salts I had forgotten. The temp was now in the 30's, so I got in. But I noticed that it seemed a bit cold, and I checked the temp again, and it was 26F! I took out the frozen bottles, they all had a thin layer of ice on the outside. I was fine, however. But after 20 minutes I got a little nervous about being in unknown waters, so to speak, and got out.

I was surprised that the temp zoomed down from the 30's to the 20's so fast. I guess adding the salts allowed the temp to go below freezing. Gee, high school science is wasted on the young!

edit:
Hmm, evidently it was wasted on me, anyway. Only the form of water is affected by salt, not the temp.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-02-09T00:19:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-74941>

Magical CT today! I hiked up a canyon I hadn't been in for a couple of years, called Water Canyon. Two summers ago, a wildfire raged through the canyon and the mountains above the canyon, and two seasons of severe flooding closed the hiking trail until it could be rehabilitated. Before the fire, the canyon was lush and had a perennial stream that flowed through the canyon floor. There are several natural artesian springs within the canyon, and it used to be the source of water for my little town decades ago. An old pond exists up there, and I thought, I bet I could dig out some neat little soaking holes so that I can CT up there all year. So, out I went today to check it out. The fire damage is extreme and sad, but the area where the pond is located was left nearly unscathed.

As I hiked up the trail, I found some owl feathers, which really made me happy because before the fire I knew a pair of owls nested in up there. The trail quickly disappeared into a tangled mesh of extreme flood wash-out. Serious debris, including HUGE logs and boulders were strewn about everywhere. There used to be an old steel vault around the spring that was at the terminus of the canyon, and I really wanted to see if it was still up there. So I pressed on through the overgrowth of vines and prickley shrubs and made it to where the spring source was. The vault box was totally gone, and the spring was just pouring out of the hillside. I'd seen some really nice pools a little lower, so I headed back down to find one I liked.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" } [](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/water-canyon-pool-jpg.17/)

The little pool was set amongst some beautiful boulders that had fresh watercress growing out of them! The water must keep them warm enough despite the frigid temps we get. A little waterfall was pouring into the pool. I stripped down, got in, settled down, and had the absolute most enjoyable CT session ever. It was magical!! I'd seen mountain lion tracks on the way in, so I kept my eyes peeled for a big cat, but never saw one. As I was walking out, I felt very light and movement felt effortless, although my toes were seriously numb. I felt like I was tiptoeing, but gently running on the way down the trail. As I came to the pond area I heard some blue jays squawking, so I stopped for a minute to watch them. Right then I heard an owl! I couldn't see it, but I could hear it. Truly, that was the icing on the CT cake for me, today. I'm going to go up there a few times a week, if possible.

[](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/grotto-jpg.16/)

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-09T15:24:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-75017>

Anne, what gains did you see that you can specifically tie to CT? I have really enjoyed becoming cold tolerant as its own gain, but I have not seen any health changes at all from doing it twice/week at 49-51 degrees for 30m each.

### freesia — 2013-02-09T19:31:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-75041>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=75017)
>
> Anne, what gains did you see that you can specifically tie to CT? I have really enjoyed becoming cold tolerant as its own gain, but I have not seen any health changes at all from doing it twice/week at 49-51 degrees for 30m each.

Hi MG,
I was doing the Leptin reset at the same time so it's impossible to distinguish what cold adaptation did all on it's own! Best guess is that the two worked together. I came from eating a GAPS/Paleo diet so it's not like any changes were due to cleaning up my diet...only changes in timing.

I was pretty sick with ME/CFS when I started...not the worst I've been (I was bedbound for several years)...but still mostly housebound with often severe fatigue, constant viral reactivation symptoms, post exertional malaise....cortisol pattern messed up. So it's all relative too....I had a _lot_ of obvious ground to make up!

...Anyway, every one of those things improved within weeks. and body temperature was mostly normal from a few months in. At 4 weeks in I found I could walk up a steep hill with no need for breaks, and no payback after. Exercise tolerance and much better oxygen use....or capacity - not sure if I'm using the correct terms there. Immune improvements - no more swollen spleen, regular sinus infections etc.. Sleep improved _very_ quickly too and cortisol pattern.
I had initial modest weight loss, but an increase in muscle offset that pretty quickly. So, a change in body comp too. Oh, also worth mentioning a 3 week cycle changed back to 4 weeks within a couple of months.
I also took low dose gcmaf for 6 months - I don't feel that actually helped much but need to mention anyway.
I think it's most likely restoring LS and CT worked _together_....so all in all I haven't been much use have I!

I've been CTing 10 months solid, through summer too, and I guess it might be interesting to see if I lose any ground if I take a break next summer.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-10T03:46:35+0200

Ugh I don' know how I have slacked so bad on CT these days.

Face dunks and going out in the snow for past month have really been the only CT I've been doing lately. wow....why? I need to find a nice stream/river to dunk myself into. I will feel more refreshed. Maybe the d-ribose will make me feel more inclined again. lol

I discovered that I only like to do outdoor based CT. I think indoor CT helped a lot in the beginning and I did this a lot last year. I can't knock it since it helped me a lot. But now where I'm at I just prefer to be outdoors instead. So this means for me CT is more seasonal too. Face dunks though no matter what I will keep even in summer, b/c dang I'll be danged it is just refreshing on the face when it's hot.

Happy CT'ing!

### freesia — 2013-02-12T06:49:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-75495>

My kiddos are down to bathing in 53 F now, just thought that was worth a mention. They're getting pink skin at that temperature. We're doing it earlier, so they get home from school and into the tub one at a time for 1/2 hour, and still have enough sunshine time to warm up a bit outside. There will be no warm up option in winter so I guess this is an adaptive phase.
What I noticed after they got to mid 50's is that they go hours and hours without asking for meals, snacks or whatever. Then when they do eat they eat really well...and enjoy plenty of fat. Interesting!

I've been getting baths and time in the sea on alternate days.

### freesia — 2013-02-15T01:01:43+0200

I did actually go icebath => sea the other day. I got dressed after my bath to make the 1/2 block trip to the beach, but get this - I did _not_ bother with shoes! 45 mins in 46 F then a quick sunsoaked, barefooted sprint to the beach for 1/2 hour walk in the water's edge.
Yesterday was a sea only day, and today I'm hoping to get both in again.

### freesia — 2013-02-16T02:27:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-76049>

@Caroline, I only took a leap of faith that my body would actually respond the way it should when exposed to cold stimulus! Getting "winter-fit" sat well with me intuitively.
I found Dr Kruse's blog through my GAPS community and I and started leptin reset and CT **pre** Paleosphere-knickertwistfest-2012.  So I never needed to question because I was already seeing results.

My husband wasn't sure about the wisdom of me taking on CT initially, but he was super supportive, and my family didn't actually think I'd lost it! \*Phew\*. They're pretty ecologically aware...already tuned in, for example, to eating seasonally. When I talked about Winter biology they didn't switch off - they asked good questions. They saw early results in me too. I think my husband's jaw was on the ground when he saw me head fast ahead of him up a steep hill and need no rest stops or recovery time at the top! To understand the significance of _that_ you need to know I was only able to be on my feet 2- 3 hours a day 6 weeks earlier, and I had pretty severe autonomic dysfunction and very poor capacity for any exercise.
So I have come a long way, but in reality I'm probably only at where a lot of others are now. I'm 100% comitted though because of what I've experienced already.

ETA Just realised another leap of faith. Eating stuff from the Ocean every day, not because I didn't like it before... but I limited it due to concerns about pollution. That was another _great_ leap of faith.

### ashryn — 2013-02-16T06:40:30+0200

### diane — 2013-03-03T20:22:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-78770>

Today I became the crazy lady that goes in Lake Ontario when it's -6 outside (about 20 degrees). Took the dogs down to the lake, walked a bit, then pulled over, took off my socks and shoes and played around in the water with my feet. Got the dogs in there too LOL. I wasn't in for too long - a couple of minutes - but hey, gotta start somewhere! Funny thing was it didn't feel that cold at first, then a minute or so in, I felt some pain from the cold and realized what I was doing. Such a mental game! So I went out and in a couple of times, but I could feel it way more after that, and wimped out. But I got in there!

### Cowboy — 2013-03-06T03:33:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-79247>

Missed a whole month of CT due to some long work days, some travel, moving a garage full of furniture and books, and getting some combo of the flu and a cold that ended up with bronchitis. I know that CT might have helped with the cold and bronchitis, but it just seemed to cold and too uncomfortable when I was feeling poorly.

Finally got back in today, and it was brutal for the first 4-5 minutes then it felt pretty normal. Cold but comfortable. Read about half of a Sports Illustrated, and when I got out I was cold but not shivering, and seemed to warm up pretty quicklyl. My sleep had deteriorated without the CT. I am hoping that I will sleep straight through tonight like I had been doing when I was steady with the CT.

25 minutes at 50 degrees.

### freesia — 2013-03-13T06:14:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-was-your-ct-today.499/post-80318>

Cowboy, I got that first five minutes of brutal after missing 4 days...so i reckon you'e doing OK! A quick warm up after a month off is what shows your BAT's still alive anyway!
Hope you've been able to get back to it regularly!

Since late January (summer here) I've been eating more in season carbohydrates. I've also been out in the sun a LOT.
It took a while to sink in  but I'm pretty sure the recent increased carbohydrate intake (to around 60 - 80g per day) has had a subtle but cumulative effect on how well I'm handling CT. The ambient temp is usually a pleasant 85 F and I get to warm up in the sunshine afterwards. Despite the easier conditions a couple of uncomfortable symptoms I had way back in the beginning have made a reappearance. I've got blue fingernails in the bath, my temperature is only _barely_ rising while in there, I have a more pronounced 'afterdrop', oh...and my old friends the YAWNS are back with a vengeance!
Also when I look at the bath as I'm about to get in a feeling of disinclination hits me! What? I LOVE ice baths and I'm motivated too! Pfffft.
....So anyway, it's back to keto for me. I'm annoyed with myself for messing with a good thing but at least I learned a lesson. And I haven't missed a bath...just haven't responded to them quite so well. And now that I think about it my sleep hasn't been _quite_ so good.
Live and learn huh.

### SeaHorse — 2013-03-13T13:09:14+0200
