# Thread: Wim in the heat! This ought to answer our questions...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 54
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoMom — 2012-03-31T03:51:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9069>

copied from: [http://www.innerfire.nl/wim-hof-blog](http://http://www.innerfire.nl/wim-hof-blog)

> Dessert Run In Namibia â€œâ€￾Iceman goes heatâ€￾â€￾
>
>
>
> 25/10/2011
>
>
>
> After all the extreme cold endurance exploits of the Iceman in the cold he decided to go the other way around, â€œâ€￾extreme heat on the human bodyâ€￾â€￾
>
>
>
> Iceman becomes Heatman no, Iceman remains the Iceman but shows that if you can endure in the extreme cold you can endure in extreme heat as well !
>
>
>
> After the last marathon in shorts beyond the Polar circle in midwinter he decided he had enough seen in the cold, still doing icy records but simultaneously determined to challenge his physiology in the heat
>
>
>
> He had to look for a location and finally came up with the Namibian Dessert the Sossusvlei with the highest dunes of the world and a humidity of less than 5 %, which makes it really dry !
>
>
>
> Together with his son Enahm and his old friend Aat den Ouden and the physiologist Thijs Eijsvogels of the UMC St Radboud University they flew to Windhoek (capital of Namibia)
>
>
>
> They rented a jeep fully equipped with tents and necessary utensils to go to remote places and drove down to the Namibian Dessert
>
>
>
> Dr Thijs Eijsvogels equipped the Iceman with all kinds of measuring devices to control his core temperature, heart frequency, evaporation and more
>
>
>
> Enahm handled the camera points and filming, Aatâ€™s expertise was of moral support and advice
>
>
>
> Wim began the run with running but as the run progressed his sandals proved to be a very bad for the run in the dessert, his feet suffered from bloody blisters after twenty kilometers which slowed down the pace drastically
>
>
>
> The Iceman did not drink any water as that was a part of the experiment and challenge which resulted in dehydration of the extremities
>
>
>
> You could see that as the hands thickened and his feet as well, the blisters were nothing more than injuries because of the stamping weight on his dehydrated feet
>
>
>
> He succeeded however in completing a full marathon distance without drinking water and thus the experiment was succesfull, aswell as the Icemanâ€™s completion of his challenge
>
>
>
> The first step into the heat is completed and gave inspiration to follow up with a new dessert challenge going into more extreme temperatures coming February in South Africa
>
>
>
> The Iceman lost 5,2 Litres of water while doing the run in the dessert but maintained core temperature !
>
>
>
> A successful first step in the heat by the Iceman
>
>
>
> The team had a great time in Namibia, experiment completed, challenge done
>
>
>
> Life is challenging, if you challenge life

### Lexi — 2012-03-31T06:26:20+0300

### MonteD — 2012-03-31T07:13:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9119>

One thing that I noticed after becoming thoroughly cold adapted was a rather drastic change in my perception of hot and cold. Before, when I was severely leptin resistant I always wanted it cold. All the way to the point of wearing shorts and a t-shirt to school while it was snowing out. After cold adaptation I became very indifferent. Heat no longer bothered me at all. I kinda even liked it...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-31T14:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9175>

Wim is interested in performance and his abilities......and training people to cold adapt. He is not exploring the health angle at all because he does not eat the perfect cold adapted diet. His abilities might even be greater if he did.

### PaleoMom — 2012-03-31T20:10:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9296>

He isn't coming at it in the same way that you are, but he is very much interested in the health angle, that is his true mission actually. He allows many scientists to do testing on him to help bring attention to his method and it's health benefits. He teaches people in hopes of helping as many people as possible to strengthen their immune system, cardiovascular system and general ability to be strong, healthy and free of disease.

### JC2K — 2012-03-31T22:42:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9367>

In re. the marathon drinking, there's a book by Tim Noakes called Challenging Beliefs. In it, he talks about how before the 60's, marathon runners either never drank, or only drank a few times. No heat problems. After the 60's, runners were encouraged to drink till they sloshed so they didn't become "dehydrated", suddenly heat strokes, people dieing, etc. All because of drinking too much water during the race.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:22:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9388>

Love it, crazy man, lol.

Anyone know how hot that desert was?

> **Lexi;9170 wrote:** i wonder about the length of his telomeres.... are we really designed to run marathons?

I think the idea is that we aren't meant to run a bunch on a regular basis. Even the persistence hunters in Africa only do that once in a while. Far cry from running many miles a day, training for a marathon, and repeating that over and over again.

Me, I can't wait to get my energy back, start SimpleFit, and then after a few months of doing just squats, push-ups, pullups, walks, and occasional sprints, bust out a 10K with no training.

Then I'm going to do a triathlon with no training and laugh at the dropped jaws.

### SimonM — 2012-04-01T02:00:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9544>

Thanks for that, PaleoMom... v.important info...

I'm a masters runner and have been wondering how I am going to cope with the heat after doing CT. Given that something like 75 per cent of muscle activity goes in heat rather than propulsion, this is pretty crucial for competition. One theory (but there are MANY) why the Kenyans, Ethiopians etc are so successful at long distance events is that their engines seem to run cooler. Now, if The Iceman can run across the desert, I'm good

@ Croak and Lexi

> Originally Posted by Lexi .... are we really designed to run marathons?
>
> > Didn't seem to work out well for Pheidippides.
>
>
>
> We seem to be best designed for long walk-jog-runs at VERY mixed paces and over mixed terrain (as in persistence hunting). Running 26.2 miles at a steady pace on flat roads is an aberration and definitely not something were are designed for
>
>
>
> The modern marathon bears no resemblence to what Pheidippides did before dropping dead (**IF** he did - probably of heat exhaustion), but I would like to see modern marathoners try it:
>
> 1) Day one: Run 145 miles on goat tracks up and down mountains from Marathon (battle site) to Sparta to ask the Spartans for help (that must have been pretty nerve-wrackingly stressful in itself - 300 anyone?)
>
> 2) Day two: run back. 145 miles (it took the Spartans THREE days to do the same journey when they did eventually turn out)
>
> 3) Day three: fight a battle (outnumbered Athenians vs Persians). Beat Persians.
>
> 4) Day four: either a) march 25 miles over a mountain back to Athens to prevent attack by Persian navy; or
>
> b) as "legend" rather than historical record has it, run 25 miles over a mountain back to Athens to tell the city of the victory. Collapse and die.
>
>
>
> Remember, all this running was being done on mountain tracks, not on smooth flat tarmac!
>
>
>
> So, let's see modern runners do 145-mile mountain trail runs back to back, THEN fight a battle, THEN run a "marathon" and see how well they do
>
>
>
> Our ancestors were tougher than us...but maybe with CT we can reclaim this heritage. I for one am trying.
>
>
>
> Source: [Herodotus via Ian Kemp, "The Great Marathon Myth".](http://http://www.coolrunning.co.nz/articles/2002a007.html)

> **AKMan;9626 wrote:** I just read 'Becoming the Iceman' by Wim Hof and Justin Rosales. Wim does train. You guys should read this book. I guarantee Jack read it, a lot of what he says I recognize in the book. I bet I also know what Factor X is, too!

### AKMan — 2012-04-01T05:12:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9670>

In all his mountain climbing records, he spent several days acclimating at different altitudes, same as all mountain climbing teams do. Prior to his marathons, he ran barefoot around his lake in increasingly longer distances until he had run the equivalent of his goal. Same for swimming under ice, he trained a lot for that. To me, saying someone ran a marathon barefoot on the snow w/o training would mean taking a guy off the street who has never run barefoot, let alone in the snow and watching him run a marathon in the snow barefoot.

Page 104: discussing his polar marathon, "months before the run, I had prepared myself by sitting in a horse stance, with my knees bent to practice getting rid of acid build up."

Not to take away from Wim, he's a remarkable man, but he's very fit and active, cycling, swimming, rappelling, canyoneering, etc... He's hardly as sometimes portrayed as the average man who ran a barefoot marathon in the snow without training.

### AKMan — 2012-04-01T05:15:27+0300

### SimonM — 2012-04-01T06:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9698>

> **AKMan;9626 wrote:** I just read 'Becoming the Iceman' by Wim Hof and Justin Rosales. Wim does train. You guys should read this book. I guarantee Jack read it, a lot of what he says I recognize in the book. I bet I also know what Factor X is, too!

Yeah, thanks AKMan. He does train. And he didn't climb to 26.000 feet on Everest wearing shorts (as Jack said in his talk), either - he got to Base Camp, which is 18.000 feet. 26,000 feet is well into the "death zone" - summit is 29,000 feet. Moderately fit tourist backpackers make it to Base Camp, no probs. Wim Hof IS amazing, and he prepares very thoroughly for what he does, but he has got nowhere near climbing Everest, in shorts or otherwise.

Jack does have a tendency to exaggerate for effect. Like Michael Phelps training 12 hours a day in 50 degree water, which he also claimed in the talk today.

### JC2K — 2012-04-01T08:57:20+0300

### AKMan — 2012-04-01T19:27:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9848>

> **PaleoMom;9776 wrote:** He never says anything about running hours a day though, like most people training for marathons do. That is what I meant by not training. He is super active and what not but doesn't follow any sort of traditional training routine like others that do these events.

I know what you are saying. I'm a huge fan of Wim Hof, but to think that a sedentary, overweight, SAD person could soak in an ice tub a few times and then run a full marathon on snow barefoot is not the message that should come out of all this.

I've known several 'regular' people who ran marathons, including the Pike's Peak marathon in Colorado Springs. They ran progressively longer distances for several weeks/months, but didn't run 26 miles until the day of the race. One guy I know never ran more than 5 miles before running 26. He didn't set any records, but finished it. The guys that win marathons are a different story, but as we have learned they will probably die young.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:34:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9854>

No, my thought is that a "normal" (biologically, not culturally) male, meaning good physical condition, regularly active, used to going barefoot, used to cold, would be able to up and do it without the usual marathon training of running dozens of miles per week for months to make yourself able to stand it.

Thanks for the amazing story about Phidippides!

### AKMan — 2012-04-01T19:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9860>

> **MamaGrok;9916 wrote:** No, my thought is that a "normal" (biologically, not culturally) male, meaning good physical condition, regularly active, used to going barefoot, used to cold, would be able to up and do it without the usual marathon training of running dozens of miles per week for months to make yourself able to stand it.

I just walked barefoot 1/4 mile down my frozen driveway with 1" of fresh snow. That was tough! Not enjoyable at all! But I can see if I did it several times a week it would toughen up your feet, but the thought of hours and hours for 26 miles...trained or not, anyone who could do that is definitely super-human!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T19:59:06+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T20:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-9888>

Just saw a sad post - [http://sports.yahoo.com/top/news?slug=ap-missingrunner](http://http://sports.yahoo.com/top/news?slug=ap-missingrunner) Caballo Blanco (from Born to Run) is dead. I wonder if it's another marathoner (ultra-marathoner in this instance) killed by his "passion."

They really pump vegetarianism in that book, and I have to think that while the Tarahumara clearly know how to run a lot and thrive, vegetarianism plus ultrarunning is a recipe for early death. As a matter of fact, the T's are not vegetarians, and the author knew this (he notes bone broth & leather sandals ... where does he think they got those?), but pushes their beans instead.

### SimonM — 2012-04-02T01:22:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-10020>

> **PaleoMom;9776 wrote:** He never says anything about running hours a day though, like most people training for marathons do. That is what I meant by not training. He is super active and what not but doesn't follow any sort of traditional training routine like others that do these events.

As Akman said: "Prior to his marathons, he ran barefoot around his lake in increasingly longer distances until he had run the equivalent of his goal." That's training.

People training for marathons don't run hours a day; the most popular training program out there at the moment - check any issue of Runner's World - is a run 3 days a week schedule, and only one of those is a long run. ANYBODY can "run" a marathon - it does not require any special physical capabilities - it is all a question of speed. The elites cover 26.2 miles in under 2 hours 15 minutes. That requires severe, dedicated, telomere-shortening training.

The "average" person, minimally trained, takes 4.5 to 5.5 hours. If you "run" a marathon in 5.5 hours you are basically jogging. It is not physically hard, but mentally insanely boring

Of course jogging at 12.5 minutes to cover 26.2 miles at the Arctic Circle like Wim did is another matter!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-02T01:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-10029>

> **MamaGrok;9951 wrote:** Just saw a sad post - [http://sports.yahoo.com/top/news?slug=ap-missingrunner](http://http://sports.yahoo.com/top/news?slug=ap-missingrunner) Caballo Blanco (from Born to Run) is dead. I wonder if it's another marathoner (ultra-marathoner in this instance) killed by his "passion."
>
>
>
> They really pump vegetarianism in that book, and I have to think that while the Tarahumara clearly know how to run a lot and thrive, vegetarianism plus ultrarunning is a recipe for early death. As a matter of fact, the T's are not vegetarians, and the author knew this (he notes bone broth & leather sandals ... where does he think they got those?), but pushes their beans instead.

That was an awesome book. We totally went barefoot after that as much as possible, and tried to keep shoes off the kiddo. Of course, I also was motivated to run my 230 lbd fat ass for many many miles a week and train for a 10k. I did it. Telomeres shortened no doubt. My 45 year old husband ran a marathon and thought about an ultra, I finally beat that out of him

Did you catch the "legs in a cold stream" thing. That struck me for some reason. I think he probably had a passion, and ultimately who's to judge total quality of life if you are so rewarded by what you do you want to keep doing it? I'm sure some, may look at the Cold-Movement (tm) as a waste of good life as we all spend hours sitting in cold water, eating keto half the year, in the dark at sunset and know in their hearts that's not how they want to live their lives. Perhaps the rewards to an ultra runner are similar to their mental states as cold is to ours?

Each year I get older, I become less and less judgmental (unless I've walked in their shoes - then I'm a raging bitch) and Jack's philosophy of do it for yourself and teach by example really strikes a chord for me on these things.

I hope I get a nickname one day that cool!

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-02T02:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-10044>

>
>
>
> The "average" person, minimally trained, takes 4.5 to 5.5 hours. If you "run" a marathon in 5.5 hours you are basically jogging. It is not physically hard, but mentally insanely boring

Not anyone. I have never been able to run or jog an entire mile in my life. It is actually a goal of mine for the year.

### SimonM — 2012-04-02T02:32:32+0300

### SimonM — 2012-04-02T02:38:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-10054>

> **PaleoMom;9777 wrote:** He reached 24,280ft on everest. [http://www.wimhofmethode.nl/wp-content/uploads/2011/07/Life-December-11-2009.pdf](http://http://www.wimhofmethode.nl/wp-content/uploads/2011/07/Life-December-11-2009.pdf) In pictures I saw that he did have boots, gloves and a hat.

Thanks for the correction, PaleoMom. You're right. Wow, it takes a lot of checking! There's nothing about this on his blog, nor on any videos I can find. But yeah, 24,280-feet or so, Camp Three - which is traditionally the final pushing-off point for a summit bid, and about the last place you can go without supplemental oxygen unless you are supremely well-adapted. He went up from and back to Base Camp. I was reacting to Jack's statement of 26,000 feet - which IS in the Death Zone.

### SimonM — 2012-04-02T02:43:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-in-the-heat-this-ought-to-answer-our-questions.553/post-10057>

I'm speaking as a Total Wimp when it comes to wet and cold (although it IS improving)...but I'm really not sure about the super-human bit  Wim insists that ANYONE can do what he does - and appears to have the students to prove it. Isn't that what Jack is saying, too? These are normal human capabilities waiting for us to unlock them.

Hah! Just noticed that Wim is Wimp without the pee. MUST be a clue there, hahahahahahahahahaha.

The holier-than-thou vegetarian mindset. Know it well. I love it when I can look back and realize what a num-nutz (is that a word?) I was. It keeps me honest about all of my beliefs and reminds me that we all find ourselves on a path according to what our dogma is. When I can accept that everyone is right where they need to be the magic of the hundredth monkey principle takes off. We don't need to do any preaching. Our lifestyle speaks for itself. And in all my years of vegetarianism I was never very healthy so how could I possibly have the attitude that I was eating healthier than those high fat red meat lovers? My smug thinking wasn't very safe at all.

# Thread: Going 'off' wine on CT - and bruised feeling.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shelley — 2012-03-31T08:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-9134>

Hey there!

I have this thing for me which is extremely weird. Usually I love a glass of wine with dinner but since starting CT I've totally gone off wine. I mean - I have zero desire to have a glass! Last night I thought I'd have a small one. After one sip I handed it back to my hubby!

OMG! Whats that all about???

Anyone else had this?

Another strange thing is that the places where I am icing my body have this kind of bruised feeling. I can't see any bruising but thats exactly how it feels.

Comments??

Tx

Shelley

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-31T08:47:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-9136>

I don't know what it's all about, but yay for you! Dr Kruse has said that CT and wine don't go together; I believe he had to cut short a dip in the lake because he'd had wine the night before. So it's all good!

As for the bruised feeling; others have reported the same. Let's just believe it's fat cells dying

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-31T19:27:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-9285>

could be liver detoxing... and not wanting to deal w/processing the alcohol...

### mistigi — 2012-03-31T20:54:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-9321>

I do not spot ice, just ice bath @50F. I noticed some time ago that ice baths cause the "bruising" feeling you are referring to. It was in my "love handles" area. I do not notice it anymore, maybe I got adapted at this temperature and need to go lower.

As to diminished alcohol cravings, I noticed that too this week. Was thinking about how I did not have to have wine, which is awesome for me as I tend to binge on and abuse alcohol.

### Shelley — 2012-03-31T21:32:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-9333>

Thanks for your comments guys.

Great to know I am not alone in doing this, and that you guys don't think I've lost my mind like some people I have told about this!!

Shelley

### vwaggs — 2012-04-06T16:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-12127>

I can't drink at all anymore! I feel very sick the next day and sometimes throw up that night. I am talking 2 glasses of red wine! It is so weird and the peer pressure from friends is horrible LOL! ( Yes I am 40!!) I told them they can come take care of my family then when I am sick all the next day if they want me to drink. I try to have one glass only now if any. I am wheat free now but beer seems to be fine for me (when I sneak a few in). I am really trying to stay away from all grains now though so that is off limits. Not sure why beer is okay and wine is not...

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-06T17:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-12135>

I gave up my Argentinean Malbecs for lent (As well as chocolate) in support of DH going wheat/gluten free ... I don't drink much regularly - and I can usually only handle 1 glass of wine at most... I'm guessing If I have wine w/ easter dinner... I probably won't be able to drink more than a 1/2 glass... I'm probably going to keep the wine/chocolate at a minimum - maybe if I'm going to have chocolate it will be cacao nibs only....

### tweety — 2012-04-06T23:21:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-12272>

I gave up my wine over a month ago when trying to get a handle on my candida issues. I had a glass of wine while out with friends a few weeks ago. Immediately after I was fine, not tipsy like usual. So here I was thinking all was well. In fact I was excited thinking I could handle the wine! Then I slept horribly, tossed and turned. Woke up around 5:45, so early, and decided to get up and get going. Nope, horribly dizzy. Almost fell down the stairs. Then threw up. 8 hours after drinking one measly glass! I was so bummed, because I love a glass of wine. I spent the whole next day feeling a little woozy and off, though going back to bed after I ate seemed to help a bit.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-11T23:59:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-off-wine-on-ct-and-bruised-feeling.554/post-13932>

> **Shelley;9190 wrote:** Hey there!
>
>
>
> I have this thing for me which is extremely weird. Usually I love a glass of wine with dinner but since starting CT I've totally gone off wine. I mean - I have zero desire to have a glass! Last night I thought I'd have a small one. After one sip I handed it back to my hubby!
>
>
>
> OMG! Whats that all about???
>
>

>

>
>
>
> Tx
>
> Shelley

me too.......wine and CT dont mix

### Antonis — 2018-02-09T13:30:09+0200

# Thread: Avocado oil: do or don't?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Maria Anna — 2012-03-31T12:35:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9145>

How about avocado oil during the Leptin Rx? Do or don't?

### hazyjane — 2012-04-01T23:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9955>

I would focus more on sat. fat like butter and coconut oil and on omega 3 fats but I think a little avocado oil now and then is ok (it's monounsaturated, similar to olive oil). Don't cook with it, though- save it for salad dressing or mayo.

### Maria Anna — 2012-04-01T23:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9966>

That was exactly I wanted to use it for! Thanks so much!!!!

### hazyjane — 2012-04-02T02:55:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-10070>

I really love avocado oil as a skin moisturizer! I use it on my arms and legs and in the winter, on my face

# Thread: CTing your pets
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-03-31T16:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9204>

I live out in the country in cold climate. It's still snowing here now, this wk its been around 20 degrees. We have 2 lge dogs that are free to roam our property and can go outside whenever they want to. They swim in the lake almost every day when the water is open, ice melted. Now spring is coming and the work for us starts! They'll be full of sand almost every day from diving into the creek. We hose them off with cold water shower outside, sometimes multiple times in a day.

They are both 7yo and the vet is amazed. They've never had to have their nails cut, teeth are white and healthy, they're slim and ripped with muscle.

I think they're cold adapted. I want them to stay healthy and live a very long life !! Learning alot here to apply to my pets. I plan to make their showers extra cold and be sure they get fresh meat in their diet everyday to preserve their heath.

### vwaggs — 2012-03-31T16:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9208>

I thought about this too! My Chihuahuas HATE any temp under 70 though. I have to put them outside myself to get them to go. My dogs would hate me forever if I stuck them in cold water! I would be reported for animal abuse for sure LOL! Amazing results for your dogs though! Makes complete sense that it would work for them too!

### Adria — 2012-03-31T16:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9212>

vwaggs, suggestion for your little dogs, maybe you would maybe start with putting their paws in cold sink water then giving yummy treat like some chicken afterwards, then each time in cold a little longer. They might start to like it because they want that treat!

### Kirstin — 2012-03-31T16:42:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9215>

I've been thinking the same thing - how to leverage this for my dogs! My oldest collie is almost 13 and has some hindquarters weakness and knuckling and I've been wondering if I should be using cold packs on her back. I have been letting her stay outside most days, but because of her age don't want her to get too cold (MN here, so we are warmer than normal, but it's been fairly cool). I've also been trying to switch them from kibble to real food - anybody know if it is easier on their guts to go to raw or cooked food? Several episodes of diarrhea as I've tried switching over, even slowly.

### Adria — 2012-03-31T18:26:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9265>

Kirstin with your dog I would suggest making cooked rice w/meat then slowly reduce the rice until mostly meat. After adapted to that you could add some chunks of raw meat when you serve it, increasing raw slowly. Then when adapted to that add more fatty meat very slowly. One of my dogs took a long time to transition, probably a year, now hes doing great.

# Thread: CT for weight gain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jonnyh — 2012-03-31T18:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/post-9249>

Hi

Is there anyone who is using CT to gain weight? most posts I read are about CT being great for weightloss etc. but dont see many about gaining weight.

I'm 5'9", 140lbs, and could do with putting on a bit of weight. Since eating primal for the last 2 years I have lost weight and hover around the 140 mark.

The only way I seem to put weight on is to up the carbs, lots of sweet potatoes, fruit etc. and hit the weights. The trouble is Dr K advises against starches in the winter while CT'ing.

I cant just keep eating more fat surely?! my cholesterol is already through the roof, though my trigs and HDL are good, and the ratios are sound, the LDL is very high though!

I'd be keen to know of others in the same sutuation as me.

Thanks for reading.

### Destiny — 2012-03-31T19:47:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/post-9289>

I just have to comment - all I can say - I am jealous. Obviously, I am not in the same situation as you.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T22:06:15+0300

### Birdy — 2012-04-01T22:13:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-9922>

very cool. I ws going to barrel-shop, but now it looks like I need a 2-person set-up.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10216>

> **Claudia;9959 wrote:** That is an awesome idea. Love it! So, you are only staying in for 4 minutes? Are you making progress with such a short time? Your water is much colder than what Dr. Kruse recommends. I think he does 50 the coldest.

Yes I am staying for 4 minutes when the water is that cold. When the water was 41 degrees F as it was warmer outside, I did 10 minutes. I shiver about 15-30 minutes after so I guess it is effective enough.. I have no batthub so the alternativ is to use the shower what I did until hubby got me the barrel. The shower takes much longer time though to get that cold. This is short and effective (I hope..). THe water will get warmer as it is soon spring here so then I will stay longer for sure! I figure it will be okay to follow the temps in the nature.. I know it is too cold but that is why I stay so short. I do get adapted cause I can stand cold a little better now and I am starting to feel so warm at night/mornings in bed so I guess it is a good sign. But I feel I am adapting rather slow I hate to shiver that much after... The bath is a piece of cake but the shivering after is not too pleasant.. I also had detox, like sinusitis and cough... but they are gone now. I started to take milkthistle, K2, vit B, curcumin a few days ago too.

I do the bath mostly to times / day. Some days I shower warm in the morning and finish off with cold and then take the bath in the evening or daytime. I do facedunks too in the mornings and sometimes in the evening.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:49:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10217>

>
>
>
>
> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/9980=74-tummy_tub.jpg)

Yes, I curl up just like that.

And try to make my back not touch the barrel so it gets cold exposure too, it works fine.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10220>

> **Marsha;9977 wrote:** Inger, I like your setup! Do you empty this every day, or just put the lid on at night and add water as necessary? Also, is this hard to get into -do you have help getting out?

I do not put the lid on at all.. and I figure will change the water once in a while. Now when it is so cold, I guess very occasional like once in a month.. and in summer I will change it more often. I can use the water for the plants in the yard then. I do not need any help to get up and yes it is very easy to get in. I always stay for a few seconds with only feets and legs in the water and then I slowly sumberge..

### ste colombe — 2012-04-02T08:13:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10223>

This is to the ladies out there. Inger WONDERFUL photo, thanks for posting. Ladies, I worry that being fully emerged will melt away our lovely bits, so I lie in the bath and cover mine with my hands raising them out of the water, so I am nearly fully emerged only my upper chest is out of the water. Yours thoughts ??- Jack you are so busy, so I would never presume you to answer this, but if you have the time, am I wrong in thinking as they are made mainly of fat, they would also shrink in the cold?

### Casey — 2012-04-02T08:20:17+0300

### Inger — 2012-04-02T08:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10226>

I do not care if my boobs are small, if they are firm and beautiful and healthy. I do not need BHs at all anymore.. I can run, jump.. without too, no pain or discomfort at all!

I also had some strange occasional pain in my right boobie at the beginning of the CT. Not anymore. I guess there was some healing going on?

### Guest — 2012-04-02T20:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10408>

> **Shijin13;10454 wrote:** Ok here's pics of my last CT bath session as you can see I have moral support for ally CT sessions

Great pics!! I was thinking last night I sure could have used moral support. It was my first bath and I did not like it. Back to cold showers for a while before I do a bath again. One thing I have learned is not to bundle up with blanket or robe. My home was about 55 and I sat in my LR wih windows open and it felt good. Slowly but surely for me ...I won't give up. I was a smoker and it has only been 6 months since I quit. I wonder why Dr. Kruse said it would tougher for us? Do you know MamaGok? I also believe I need to eat keto paleo longer then I have. Just started about 4 weeks ago, steadily, eating this way. Not even cheating which is a miracle for me. I want to much to be healthy again.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-03T00:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10525>

> **Suhenna;10476 wrote:** I was a smoker and it has only been 6 months since I quit. I wonder why Dr. Kruse said it would tougher for us?

Dr Kruse (and others!) have said that smoking is the most inflammatory thing you can do to your body. Hence, inflammation recovery will be longer and adaptation likely slower. But even more important that you keep on keepin' on. Well done for making such big changes!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-03T00:33:08+0300

### Guest — 2012-04-03T00:48:07+0300

KiwiLauren

I am also wondering if the inflamation would make it so we are more apt to try and resist being cold? Inflammation must love warmth. Ugh!!! I am just grateful to Dr K that I have the chance to turn this all around if I chose to be keep good thoughts while making these changes.

If I could I would be on my way to NZ It would be great to live in an enviroment more suitable to our nature. It's like learning a language - put yourself in a country that speakss the language you wan to learn - piece of cake.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-03T15:40:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10740>

I love that, KiwiLauren!

caroline, you remind me of Weston Price's observation of the native Swiss children going barefoot to the knee in glacial streams in weather that had him and his wife wearing heavy coats (& hats or gloves, can't remember). He thought it was b/c of their nutrition, and I'm certain that played a huge role, but till now, I never considered that the fact they lived a mile up in the Alps without central heating may have played a huge role, too!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-04T04:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-11113>

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/11182=95-temp.jpg)
[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/11182=96-in the pool.jpg>)

Here we are CT'ing today in our pool! Don't know if you can see the first one, thermometer says 50 degrees. The second picture is my husband, 7 year old son, and me.

My son saw me perusing Dr. Kruse's facebook page and so I told him this is the doctor who taught mommy about about CT (he's been listening to me talk to my husband about it and really understands a lot of it). Anyhow, he said "That's cool, can we invite him over to swim with us?!" I told him no, that he lived down in Nashville and he was a very busy Doctor. THen he says, "Well, when we go on vacation in May can we go meet our latin teacher and Dr. Kruse and swim in his pool?" lol (my son and I are learning latin together using a course called Visual Latin and he really likes the instructor )

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-07T02:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-12328>

My new cold tub!!!!!

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/12397=105-cold tub.jpg>)

It is a 100 gallon stock tub, I got it for 60 dollars at a local farm supply store. I found a commercial ice machine about 2 1/2 miles from my house that sells ices for 1.75 for 20 pounds, so for 3.50 I can get 40 pounds of ice. I am going tomorrow to get a thermometer and a small aquarium pump to circulate the water. Should be interesting......

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-07T02:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-12330>

My new cold tub!!!!!

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/12399=106-cold tub.jpg>)

It is a 100 gallon stock tub, I got it for 60 dollars at a local farm supply store. I found a commercial ice machine about 2 1/2 miles from my house that sells ices for 1.75 for 20 pounds, so for 3.50 I can get 40 pounds of ice. I am going tomorrow to get a thermometer and a small aquarium pump to circulate the water. Should be interesting......

It allows me to get full submersion just by sliding down. I can sit in it with a mild bent knee and have the water come up to my shoulders.

The Kruse-protocol works!!! After a few Months of following it, I weigh the same as in the before-pics (taken about 3 years ago, eating a keto-diet, a very healthy kind of keto with organs etc. but having it warm 7/24 and lots of artificial lights..

Before are the left pictures, after, right side.

.

.

I cannnot believe really, that I weigh the same in both pictures, and I am doing no sports and living quite sedentary!

I eat a lot.. I got such a huge appetit from CT! It is better now, but I just ate as much as I wanted when I had the appetit. I and have started to add in carbs a few weeks ago - I eat them as organic or wild fruit like dried Figs, Beries, Mangos.., wild raw honey / honeycomb, occasionally a little vegtables like carrots and cucumber. I eat wild greens/herbs every day now.

My CT is mostly in the river by the house, sometimes I sit in there for 15-20 minutes, maybe 1-2 times a week, and other days I just stay in a few minutes. Every morning I go there for swimming (very shorthly, just a few minutes), and also evening if I feel like it. The water temp is about 52-55 degrees C. right now.

Inger

# Thread: Breastfeeding...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NotAnother30 — 2012-03-31T19:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9286>

I've noticed that I cannot follow the diet to a 'T' due to the fact that I was always hungry especially following a nursing. I have to have at least 2 starches in the day for my body to be happy and to continue breastfeeding. I'm a year into feeding and still have another year to go. I've seen results but would the extra starches effect anything much?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-31T23:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9386>

Hi NotAnother30. While I can't say for sure because I stopped breastfeeding my children long before I heard about paleo or Dr Kruse, I can't see any reason why you cannot follow the diet when you are nursing. If you are hungry, add extra fat or protein (or both!) to your diet. That will do far more for hunger, your health (and your baby) than starches will. A lot of people feel more 'happy' with the starches because they are still in old, unhealthy patterns. Once you get them out, the cravings go. That being said, I don't know what exactly you mean when you say 'safe starches.'

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-31T23:35:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9394>

Bfing a 2yo. Went primal 6 mths pp My supply went up when I added more fat/protein

Shoot for the BAB, lunch & dinner. Don't worry about being in keto. Unless that where you want to go

If you don't have any major weight to loose keep your carbs btwn 20-50 g/day but keep them on the low starch side until you hit midsummer

Right now you're still a sugar burner which is why you feel better w/the starches. Try adding 1TBs of coconut oil to every meal -you may have to work up to that.

Once you get used to eating enough fat at your meals you won't have the hunger issues after each nursing session

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9402>

Good advice about the CO, shijin, if she's not already doing that.

I've been nursing for 14 years continuously - yes, continuously! - and am right this very minute nursing a two year old through her hour-long nap while I catch up on the computer work. I eat only non-starchy veggies that aren't FODMAPs or nightshades, and usualy am below 15g carbs/day this time of year. I'm not having, nor have ever had the problem you're experiencing, so I'm not sure exactly what is going on, but I just wanted to toss out that it's not a normal experience for everyone.

### NotAnother30 — 2012-03-31T23:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9407>

Thanks for the responses. I should have said safe starches twice a day, which are usually sweet potatoes or plaintains on a very active day and one on non busy days. I will try to increase coconut oil at every meal. I can't tolerate too much animal fats, like ribs and pork...yuck they make me sick. I've been some what paleo since october when diagnoses with a wheat allergy and was fine before. All of this started when I decreased the nursing. And before I forget, I live in Miami...could that be an issue also?

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T00:07:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9412>

I don't know if you do grassfed meats, but the fat on them is worlds different from factory farmed meats. The fat on a nice pastured pork shoulder turned into cueritos, or on a huge slab of beef ribs smoked by my husband ... OH YUM!!!

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-01T02:58:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9609>

Come to think of it, could the rest start of my menstrual cycle cause of all of this since the onset was so sudden?

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-01T04:29:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9646>

Fwiw there ate no safe starches, unless you are truly eating seasonally & locally.

Right now for me the only thing locally is greens. I actually have out of control collards, kale & broccoli leaves in our garden that we planted last fall. Those are my carbs + broccoli, cauliflower & cabbage

As for your totm returning I was 1mth into the LR when mine came back (15mths pp). So yeah w/totm returning you can see a dip in supply and again when ovulating

As far as the hunger. I'm guessing your not eating enough protein/fat at meals

Ca you give us a sample day of what your eating

This am for bkfst I had 2egg omlette cooked in 2T of Kerry gold ghee & 4 pieces of bacon + bullet proof coffee. Lunch was another 4 pieces of bacon & dinner was a 16oz steak & greens

I'm post reset but it took me a whole 6 mths to get to this point.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-01T04:43:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9656>

I'm breastfeeding my 10 month olds and haven't had any problems being on a ketogenic diet. I have been on a ketogenic diet for a number of months, but just started the leptin Rx about about 5ish weeks ago. Like others said just make sure to have plenty of food at breakfast lunch and dinner. It's been hard to not snack, because I was used to doing it, but not impossible if you plan on big meals!

Unfortunately, I haven't been able to lose ANY weight despite the ketogenic diet, but I've finally stopped gaining weight. With each of my children I've breastfed I've put on weight. Very frustrating! Hoping once my period returns, maybe that will help my hormones normalize and I'll start to drop pounds? Who knows.

Good luck!

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-01T22:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9932>

I'm thinking its the whole change but on a typical day:

Breakfast 3 eggs scrambled with thin cut steaks cooked in coconut oil, multivitamin, cod liver oil

Lunch: 2 turkey burgers, asparagus

Dinner: Baked chicken with roasted carrots, cauliflower, broccoli

Yesterday and Friday:

Breakfast: Same

Between breakfast and lunch the shakes start (banana) in fears of hypoglycemia

Lunch: baked chicken, roasted sweet potatoes

Dinner:2 turkey burgers, 2 sunny side up eggs, asparagus

I'm not a diabetic but I know the s/s of it. I'm thinking it's hormonal changes.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-02T02:34:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10051>

Ooo, wow, seriously I'd be very hungry with that little fat, too. I'm to the point now where if I eat poultry, I have to make a special attempt to get more fat in, and I mean animal fat or maybe coconut oil.

More fat! I bet it will do wonders for your hunger \*and\* your hormones - they're made out of cholesterol.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T15:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10277>

Concur w/MamaG

NOT ENOUGH FAT! At bkfst I'm eating btwn 4-6 tbs of ghee and 2-3TBS of coconut oil! when I count my bpcoffee

DH loves chicken... but Now I'm like meh?? chicken... shit I'm gonna be hungry after eating that foul bird! And I usually will make some BPTea to have w/dinner to ensure I have enough fat!

I'd prefer to have beef and pork just for the fat! my fatrotein ratio is probably something like 60:35, w/the last 5 allocated to veggies when I feel like them...which is usually lunch or dinner...

up your fat....take some coconut oil, add some 100% dark chocolate and melt it over a double boiler and start having that at the end of each meal! it will make it easier to consume more fat...and you'll be satiated; and when your satiated - your body will go ok- not starving... I can start using any excess for milk production and you'll be surprise how much your milk increases...

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-02T19:07:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10372>

YAY! You guys were right! At 10:45 when my daily morning shakes began, went for last night's chicken and within a minute, the shakes had shimmied away. I'm totally going to increase the more fat factor. I was thinking I was eating enough already and I won't let the fact that sweet potatoes are $0.49/lb this week an excuse for buying some more. I'll let you guys know how it goes :-D

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T19:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10374>

glad to see you figured it out... Fat + protein in the right quantities = milk; happy mama and happy baby! I wish I had know about paleo eating w/my dd (she wasn't able to latch/nurse) - maybe I could have pumped enough to meet her needs and we wouldn't have had to supplement w/her - of course if that had happened...ds might not be here now!

# Thread: Green Tea to Supercharge Cold Thermogenesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kasra — 2012-03-31T19:55:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9291>

Hey guys,

Next time you take a cold shower/bath, try ingesting a good amount of green tea beforehand.

Yesterday morning, I mixed 4 teaspoons of [Matcha](http://http://www.amazon.com/dp/B000BVNSFW/?tag=nutribeast-20) into a bottle of ice cold water and chugged it. I felt my body turn into a furnace and was compelled like never before to take an ice cold shower. I will be experimenting more with this.

The explanation for why this worked is likely that [green tea is a potent stimulator of thermogenesis.](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v24/n2/full/0801101a.html) I'm getting the feeling that this effect largely explains the persistent epidemiological evidence that heavy green tea drinkers live longer.

I'd like to know what your experience has been with green tea in this context.

### PaleoMom — 2012-03-31T20:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9300>

I drink white and green tea all day and haven't noticed this effect, but matcha is quite different since you're actually consuming the plant instead of just steeping it.

I'll join you and pick some up at the store and report back. Thanks for sharing, this could be an exciting bonus!

### Birdy — 2012-03-31T20:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9305>

I'll have to try that. I also drink green tea throughout the day and love the way I feel when I have it regularly, decaf or not.

### JC2K — 2012-03-31T22:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9361>

Not too keen on that study. They screwed around with Ephedrine and Caffeine in it. Ephedrine/Caffeine stacks are a well known highly thermogenic combination. Essentially what I'm reading that they were saying is that Green Tea alone did little, caffeine alone did little, ephedrine alone did a bit. But green tea + caffeine + ephedrine did a bunch. We already know Ephedrine + caffeine does a bunch. I don't really see anything really thrilling here on green tea alone per se. Really the only thing I'm getting out if it is that if you are drinking green tea alone for thermogenic properties, you want the caffeinated version for the best bang for your buck, and not the decaffeinated version.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:18:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9385>

So you're saying we should eat tea instead of drink it?

### Kasra — 2012-03-31T23:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9397>

> **JC2K;9423 wrote:** Not too keen on that study. They screwed around with Ephedrine and Caffeine in it. Ephedrine/Caffeine stacks are a well known highly thermogenic combination. Essentially what I'm reading that they were saying is that Green Tea alone did little, caffeine alone did little, ephedrine alone did a bit. But green tea + caffeine + ephedrine did a bunch. We already know Ephedrine + caffeine does a bunch. I don't really see anything really thrilling here on green tea alone per se. Really the only thing I'm getting out if it is that if you are drinking green tea alone for thermogenic properties, you want the caffeinated version for the best bang for your buck, and not the decaffeinated version.

You may have misinterpreted the results.

According to the paper, a small dose of Ephedrine was used "as a pharmacological tool...to mimic a small increase in sympathetic activity."

Even if you ignore the trials that used Ephedrine, green tea caused a significantly greater increase in brown adipose tissue respiration than did an equal amount of isolated caffeine, suggesting that green tea is a uniquely thermogenic substance.

[This figure](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v24/n2/fig_tab/0801101f1.html) clearly illustrates this point (look at the top left graph in particular).

### Kasra — 2012-03-31T23:55:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9400>

@MamaGrok

[This study](http://http://144.206.159.178/ft/553/184587/4714287.pdf) found that "the concentration of

epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) available from drinking matcha is ... at least three times higher than the largest literature value for other green teas."

As you are ingesting the whole leaf, it seems very plausible that you get a substantially stronger dose of catechins in matcha than you would via water extraction (aka brewed tea).

The [original study](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v24/n2/full/0801101a.html) I linked suggests that the combination of caffeine and EGCG found in green tea is what gives it its thermogenic properties. Thus, matcha should pack more of a thermogenic punch than standard green tea.

You can also mix matcha with cold water (as I do), making it more convenient to prepare.

### Kasra — 2012-03-31T23:57:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9403>

> **MamaGrok;9447 wrote:** So you're saying we should eat tea instead of drink it?

[This study](http://http://144.206.159.178/ft/553/184587/4714287.pdf) found that "the concentration of epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) available from drinking matcha is ... at least three times higher than the largest literature value for other green teas."

As you are ingesting the whole leaf, it seems very plausible that you get a substantially stronger dose of catechins in matcha than you would via water extraction (aka brewed tea).

The [original study](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v24/n2/full/0801101a.html) I linked suggests that the combination of caffeine and EGCG found in green tea is what gives it its thermogenic properties. Thus, matcha should pack more of a thermogenic punch than standard green tea.

You can also mix matcha with cold water (as I do), making it more convenient to prepare.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9404>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-9894>

I have been drinking matcha daily for over a year. I used it to get off coffee. I do drink it with hot water but you will find you need a matcha whisk. Most of them are made of bamboo. Here is where I get my matcha. A pound for $71.00 is a great price:

### Jason Coates — 2019-04-30T11:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-269330>

**[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28275131](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28275131)
[Am J Clin Nutr.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28275131#) 2017 Apr;105(4):873-881. doi: 10.3945/ajcn.116.144972. Epub 2017 Mar 8.**Tea catechin and caffeine activate brown adipose tissue and increase cold-induced thermogenic capacity in humans.**Background:**

The thermogenic effects of green tea catechin have been repeatedly reported, but their mechanisms are poorly understood.**Objective:** The aim of this study was to investigate the acute and chronic effects of catechin on brown adipose tissue (BAT), a site specialized for nonshivering thermogenesis, in humans.**Design:** Fifteen healthy male volunteers underwent fluorodeoxyglucose-positron emission tomography to assess BAT activity. To examine the acute catechin effect, whole-body energy expenditure (EE) after a single oral ingestion of a beverage containing 615 mg catechin and 77 mg caffeine (catechin beverage) was measured. Next, to investigate the chronic catechin effects, 10 men with low BAT activity were enrolled. Before and after ingestion of the catechin beverage 2 times/d for 5 wk, cold-induced thermogenesis (CIT) after 2 h of cold exposure at 19°C, which is proportional to BAT activity, was examined. Both the acute and chronic trials were single-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled, season-matched crossover studies.**Results:** A single ingestion of the catechin beverage increased EE in 9 subjects who had metabolically active BAT (mean ± SEM: +15.24 ± 1.48 kcal, _P_ < 0.01) but not in 6 subjects who had negligible activities (mean ± SEM: +3.42 ± 2.68 kcal). The ingestion of a placebo beverage containing 82 mg caffeine produced a smaller and comparative EE response in the 2 subject groups. Multivariate regression analysis revealed a significant interaction between BAT and catechin on EE (β = 0.496, _P_ = 0.003). Daily ingestion of the catechin beverage elevated mean ± SEM CIT (from 92.0 ± 26.5 to 197.9 ± 27.7 kcal/d; _P_ = 0.009), whereas the placebo beverage did not change it.**Conclusion:** Orally ingested tea catechin with caffeine acutely increases EE associated with increased BAT activity and chronically elevates nonshivering CIT, probably because of the recruitment of BAT, in humans. These trials were registered at [www.umin.ac.jp/ctr/](http://www.umin.ac.jp/ctr/) as UMIN000016361.

### Brent Patrick — 2024-06-18T05:56:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-335859>

### [Effects of tea polyphenols on UVA-induced melanogenesis via inhibition of α-MSH-MC1R signalling pathway](https://www.termedia.pl/Effects-of-tea-polyphenols-on-UVA-induced-r-nmelanogenesis-via-inhibition-of-MSH-MC1R-signalling-r-npathway,7,46951,1,1.html)

 www.termedia.pl

Hello Brent, is the theory here drinking Green Tea helps with benefits of CT?

### Brent Patrick — 2024-06-20T10:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-335926>

I drink Matcha and Green Tea daily but as a hack last summer I consumed EGCG and I had issues developing a deep tan, which I am sure was due to consuming EGCG as I had not changed anything else...then I researched the literature, and my suspicions were confirmed.

**Consuming EGCG from Green Tea and Matcha is far more natural** than consuming isolated EGCG, well this is my experience concerning melanin renovation. My Tan is always very good with consumption of Teas... N=1

So now I rely on EGCG from Teas, which i was originally doing anyways

Green Tea is excellent for CT as it has thermogenic properties and assists with fat Oxidation as others have mentioned!

As with anything the dose makes the poison especially concerning ones N=1

### Jack Kruse — 2024-06-20T22:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-335939>

EGCG and ECG inhibit complex I inhibition by inducing a transient drop in cellular ATP levels and a temporary ROS burst. A drop in ATP production is required for endogenous light creation in my opinion. The ROS burst results in SKN-1 and DAF-16 activation in lower life forms like worms. This is has not showed up in higher eukaryotes as yet. In worms, through adaptative responses, catechins like ECCG reduced fat content which is why so many think its a part of a good CT stack. I am not so sure this is true in humans. I think the better idea is in enhanced ROS defense and use. Why? ROS is how humans make endogenous light and I think we will soon find out that increased ROS will be the best longevity hack for improved healthspan in the long term in humans. This is counterintuitive to the modern centralize paradigm in medicine.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are formed during normal metabolic processes in mitochondria. Mitochondrial metabolism occurs spontaneously on Earth. Genes amplify these metabolic pathways, and in this way, gene products are capable of making more or less light from how they amplify or de-amplify metabolic networks in cells. I believe ROS is the key to understand what genes are really doing.

ROS are magnetic signal created from excess oxygen in mtDNA. People forget molecular oxygen is the only paramagnetic gas on the periodic table. This makes it a unique magnetic signal used in cellular communication. All free radicals have one unpaired electron to make them magnetic sugnals. All ROS are called singlet oxygen signals that come from mtDNA actions. When electrons are added to a magnetic molecule this is called a reduction type of reaction. When singlet oxygen is reduced by the addition of electrons, the ROS magnetic signal shifts to a lower energy state and as a result emits photon.

The electronic transition of electronically excited species from the singlet or the triplet excited state to the ground state is accompanied by photon emission and this is how cells CREATE ENDOGENOUS LIGHT. This is how light is crerated ENDOGENOUSLY by life. In this mechanism only a few photons are emitted per second per square centimeter, the photon emission is ultra-weak in nature. But it is these photons that explain how light sculpts life.

### Ben Trussell — 2024-06-21T04:43:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-335958>

Thank you Jack and Brent for these insights. A bit to learn which is good. Studying older posts - its ok to assume that drinking Matcha provides a boost to CT by helping the body generate heat. Going to read all the CT blogs today and whiteboard it all. Thanks again

### Jack Kruse — 2024-06-21T19:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-to-supercharge-cold-thermogenesis.564/post-335980>

This post above should help you understand what high latitude living induced in biology: An uncoupled haplotype. When light is absent cells become hypoxic so what did they do? They used the hypoxia signal in L0,L1, L2, L3 mitochondria to make ROS to create endogenous light missing from the environment to run their internal biochemistry.

Few see where I am headed because FEW read my blogs well and even fewer ask the right questions.

### Ben Trussell — 2024-06-23T03:34:26+0300

Am I reading this right that the drop in ATP production ( which would otherwise be seen as something a human wouldn't want) and the subsequent burst in ROS , is a valuable mechanism / result of the EGCG - ? and - subsequently you feel that enhanced ROS defence and use - the presence of ROS - wleads to endogenous light production ...

# Thread: Excess Testosterone for a Man? Labs help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-testosterone-for-a-man-labs-help.565/>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-31T20:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-testosterone-for-a-man-labs-help.565/post-9293>

Just got the BFs labs back and need a little help. Can anyone confirm if this is too much testosterone? All the others sex steroid hormones look low ..... so where is that high testosterone coming from?

Anyone can hack away on anything on these labs. All comments and contributions are welcome.

46 yr old male

No meds

6'1"

160#

SAD diet most of his life. Completely organic for last 10 yrs. Grass-fed meat, gluten free for a year. Grain free since Jan 1. Leptin Rx for 2 months due to high rT3 (low ratio).

Cholesterol 260 (100-199)

Triglycerides 41 (0-149)

HDL 90 (>39)

VLDL Calc 8 (5-40)

LDL Calc 162 (0-99)

These ranges are from LabCorp

Pregnenolone 113 (180-200)

DHEAs 230 (500-640)

Progesterone apparently wasn't measured

Testosterone 848 (650-827)

Testosterone, Free 14 (6.8-21.5)

Estradiol, sensitive 15 (20)

Prior to these numbers his thyroid borders on hyper. Graves disease runs in his family. Son has Graves.

CRPhs .33 (.0-3.0)

IGF-1 157 (94-252)

PSA .9 (3-70)

Iron, serum 106 (40-155)

Iron Saturation 46% (15-55)

TBIC 229 (250-450)

Ferritin 283 (30-400)

HA1c 5.4 (4.8-5.6)

Fasting Insulin 6.2 (2.6-24.9)

2 Hour postprandial Insulin 5.8 (2.6-24.9)

LH 7.2 (1.7-8.6)

FSH 5.8 (1.5-12.4)

GGT 14 (0-65)

Thank you!!!

# Thread: Did I fall off the LR?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ginastarke — 2012-03-31T20:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9295>

I was on a mini vacation last weekend and as far as I knew, stayed on the LR, but I was relying on hotel food ( boil in bag scrambled eggs, sausage and bacon) coconut manna, and cocoa nibs. The morning after getting home I tested myself for ketosis (i know he says the sticks are meaningless, but I had them around and test out of curiosity) no ketosis. and I'd put on 3 pounds that took me all week to lose.

So my question is - Did I fall off? Do I need to start another 8 weeks?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-31T22:37:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9363>

My understanding is you do the Leptin Reset until you have the signs of leptin sensitivity, not for a fixed time. So whether you 'fell off' or not (though it doesn't sounds like it - keto sticks stop showing ketosis after a while, even when you are ketogenic), keep on going. Weight can definitely fluctuate...it's a diet for healing.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:14:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9381>

You probably did not set yourself back any as far as the LR, unless there was a ton of veg oils used to cook your food, in which case you might lose a couple of days. Keep on truckin'.

(believe me, I'm the expert in literally seeing how many days/weeks can be lost by going off-track, but it's usually sweet/wheat foods, not the oils, that really kill this particular form of progress. The oils do make me bloat and feel gross, though.)

### ginastarke — 2012-04-01T00:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9409>

> **MamaGrok;9443 wrote:** You probably did not set yourself back any as far as the LR, unless there was a ton of veg oils used to cook your food, in which case you might lose a couple of days. Keep on truckin'.
>
>
>
> (believe me, I'm the expert in literally seeing how many days/weeks can be lost by going off-track, but it's usually sweet/wheat foods, not the oils, that really kill this particular form of progress. The oils do make me bloat and feel gross, though.)

That's what I was worried about. I normally don't eat sausage and bacon because of the sugars that are in most of them. As ans far as the eggs, I can't get an ingredient list but i suspect some kind of wheat or corn starch :-/

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T00:11:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9418>

I don't think the sugar in the meats will set you back more than a couple of hours. The eggs, if there is starch in them, might put you back a day. Just don't make a habit of it.

### ginastarke — 2012-04-01T01:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9523>

> **MamaGrok;9480 wrote:** I don't think the sugar in the meats will set you back more than a couple of hours. The eggs, if there is starch in them, might put you back a day. Just don't make a habit of it.

No problem there. Now marrow in my eggs, that's a whole other story (I know if you're not used to offal it sounds well, awful, but it's really good!

### Tita — 2012-04-04T14:57:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-11226>

Gina, I wouldn't worry too much with what happened...8 weeks is no magic number anyway, so I would just keep going. Not every day will we have a choice of a perfect food and to stress about past is bad for your cortisol. Maybe you wont even need 8 weeks anyway.

You are doing your best and that's what matters.

### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T07:13:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-12396>

I'll probably need a lot more than 8 weeks, since I have 60 pounds to lose until I'm in the normal range.

### janagram — 2012-04-08T07:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-12598>

I don't think you really fell off the LR ....but.....I know I did!!!! I went on a 6 day trip and slipped until I slid...and now I'm pretty bummed out. Not quite depressed yet.....What to do??? Yes, I gained 3 lbs and maybe more...this is going to be so hard....but what is there to do but get on the old LR railroad again? This will have to begin tomorrow and we are going to a big dinner, but ...

so be it! Good luck to anyone who has gained weight and is going to get back down!

### janagram — 2012-04-09T21:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-13089>

Done waffling! Back on LR! Thanks to support in another thread!

### admin — 2012-04-02T20:21:04+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T01:17:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9464>

Here ya go, my fast-typed notes with lots of misses ... let me know what goes in the ellipses.

My name is Jack Kruse and I came to share an idea with you

2006 I as at a spine meeting in Birmingham AL … tore my right knee … needed helpl getting to the podium .to give my speech… humiliating. Friend said she knew why it happened, sent a book and six papers to read. Book. Then papers. In that order. Monk who sold his Ferrari.

360?something pounds, so I knew the reason I tore my meniscu was leptin. Nobel prize was given for telomere biology…soI just kept putting things together. I thought to yself, put everything synthesized in one place. Was it possible that obesity was an inflammatory brain condition? And wasit possible that I could do brain surgery on myself without a scalpel?

So I constructed something called the LR Didn’t publish till last year on my blog.

Cold – sherpas live in Himalayas, adapted to cold. 25 years ago, if someone told you a dea person would heaer, you’d laugh. Now we know about cochlear impants, rewiring … brain. My idea was to rewire my brain using circadian biology and cold thermogenesis to … 3 months, lost … pounds. 11 months, 133lb.

Huge impact on my practice, family. (fat lard pic) At Thanksgiving, they wanted to know what I did. I told them to give me four hours and I could change their livevs. Son lost 60?? Poundsin 6 weeks. He said, “Dad tell me what to do. “ I ruined him a a child; it was my job to fix him.

Nephew 268 lb now a Navy Seal

This longevity thing really got me. I went …. Drew my blood. Things kept happening. Started synthesizing all these things together. …. Other part of this pathway basically said that our ancestors that came out of the oceans were cold adapted mammals. Had to go into the water to get the omega 3 fish. Had to go numb – CNS ..

Tedx had Wim Hof on, the Icema, packed himself in ice, amazingthings tells people his brian does this. Has injected himself with bacteria, neve got infection with the cold. Coimbed everest in shorts and t-shirt, next day ran a marathon in Arctic Circle.

We know a couple people use use these pathways. Armstrong, Phelps, Sherpas, astronauts … they all use the cold. Armstrong thinks Bristol myer squibs helped his cancer. Lance had freezers in his Austin house and that’s how he trained and won.

Phelps swam 12hrs/day in 50 degree water. Sherpas ived on the top o fEverest 25000 years, taking people up … When Armstrong wen ton the moon, lost 30lb in 5 days, with no food left in the module. No one knew why, and went to the Sherpas to find out why.

There is some kind of pathway in all of us.

In 2011, one of the general surgeons at the hospital, dad is a …. Surgeon……. Thought creer over with disease, almost dropped knife mid-surgery, couldn’t operate any more.

Went home, had surgery, didn’t do well. Devasted. One of his partners, my good friend, was told to listen to me. He came in and I thought I was going to have to tear down some dogma, but didn’t need to. “I think I can change your lifef.”

In six weeks, he was running, able to use his arms, back at work, and a completely new person.

Another Tedx homerun. Terry Wahls, Iowa State physician? With secondary MS. Used diet that selects for …… “paleo diet” completely reversed her MS. When I saw her, I said you know what? I’ve gotto step up my game. Time to let the world know some of the things I’ve found.

Neurosurgeon – a lot of chronic pain out there. First evolutionary directed procedure on a human being. One problem - …. Would not let me give him anesthesia or inject with bacteria. So when I couldn’t find a patient, I decided to do the experiment on myself.

Had another problem. Two choices. Take my widsdom teeth out or do the surgery. Used to be a dentist, had my teeth out, didn’t need that surgery.

Decided I had to make myself a surgical candidate Went to plastic surgeon in Octoer. Hadn’t sen me in 10 years, asked why there. “I’ve worked really hard…” I kne I didn’t need surgery. Ate the diet that our government told me to eat. (I think) Ate it for two months, didn’t tell my family. Gained 25lbs, hid it pretty well.

Told my wife Jan 9, 2012. Went into a surgery center and let a surgeon operate on me without any anesthesia. Took MRSA before surgery. Injected it all over torso andbelly. 6hr surgery! It was rough.

At home, told wife to go into closet. I said throw that tub on the bed. My daughter helped her, ,wife asked what’s going on? I was screaming with tears in my face. “Freezer has 120lb ice there, get it. I put it there last night.”

She turned tub on. In 3 min, I was completely pain-free. I told daughter what I did and why. 12 incisions on my body, 30” long. Never any pain or pain meds.

What did I decide to do next? Now that I know this pathway is in us, every step, I need to use this in my practice. Patient in my practice, not a surgical candidate, 80yo type 2 diabetic. I told him that if you eat this diet I tell you to and let me freeze you nthe hospital, I’ll operate on you.

8 screws in his back Jan 27. 4 month recovery for a healthy person – they’re 4” long. Four hour operation. Frozen blanket 40 degrees 3 days. 3 days hospital, rehab 5 days, playing banjo in rehab for other patients. Came back 10 days later and handed me his Percocet, said doc, I don’t need this. AS a neurosurgeon , I couldn’t believe it. I realized at that point that I’d found something really important.

I now teach the Paleolithic diet & ct to anyone I can in my practice. It has entirely changed.

Sometimes, unconventional things lead to remarkable results that will help millions of people. My profession has lost its way. We need to stop preaching randomized controlled trials and go back to the North Star that helps us find our way.

Ideation without execution leads to … ?

The point that I make is that optimal health is no longer a mystery for our species. It’s now a choice.
