### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T01:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9503>

A non-screwed-up summary I made for another forum: (let me know if this is incorrect; I couldn't hear everything, like why the surgery)

So there's this guy named Wim Hof who is known for doing things like taking ice baths (literally, just ice, all the way up to his neck), climbing Everest in shorts, staying underwater under ice for 6m, running a marathon barefoot at the Arctic Circle with no training then running one in the Numibian desert, and injecting himself with bacteria and not catching any illneess.

Hoff thinks he's this way b/c he has learned some meditation and controls it with his brain. Kruse says he's this way because of cold. So after working on himself for 6 years, his third experiment (first was LR for weight & health, then CT for health) was this...

He planned to have surgery on himself, but needed no surgery, not even his wisdom teeth (already had them out), so he decided to eat SAD for two months. Gained 25lb doing it! He also became leptin resistant again and stopped CT'ing so he would have minimal pain tolerance. Then he went to a plastic surgeon friend and said he wanted a tummy tuck. Had the surgeon do it with NO anesthesia. But before the surgery, Kruse injected & smeared himself all over his torso & belly with ... MRSA! It was a 6hr surgery, 12 incisions totalling 30" on his body (not sure I heard that part right), but he had no pain. Afterwards (it was hard to hear, so I'm not sure about this part), he had tons of pain. Went home and had his wife get out 120lb of ice from the freezer and put it in the tub. All pain disappeared immediately. He never used any pain meds before or after that point. His abdomen swelled & looked awful for six weeks, but felt fine.

So he put it into his practice. Took an 80yo (wheelchair-bound?) diabetic man who wanted (some kind of) surgery. . Kruse told him he wasn't a good candidate for surgery (I assume because of his bad health?), but that he would do it IF the man ate paleo and would allow freezing at the hospital. He put in 8 4" screws into his back - usually a four month recovery - on a 40 degree blanket the whole 4hr surgery, and the guy was out of the hospital in 3 days (normal is 7-10 days), out of rehab in 5 days (normal is a month) (he felt so good he was playing banjo for others while there), and came back after ten days to turn in the Percocet he didn't need. The normal complication rate for someone with his co-morbidities is very high, and he had none.

He now plans to use this regularly in his practice, and believes cold can do crazy things for healing, infection, health overall, only if paired with an anthropologically correct diet. Otherwise it's kind of limited to the Tour de France & Olympic swimming type stuff (Phelps apparently trains in water much colder than he admits, and Armstrong trains on a bike in a freezer in his home).

He has taken what Hof does and proven that it's not something special to Hof (although Hof can perhaps take it to special extremes because of a genetic tendency). And then he took what he did and proved that it can be applied in every day medical practice, and even for people like us at home.

> Kpcst;9545 wrote:** So CT makes you invulnerable to infection?

> KP

Well, a strong immune system makes you invulnerable to infection. It's why our family has had almost no sicknesses in the last several years, despite lots in families around us. It's why I think it's stupid every time there's a TV show where some fictional disease has a 99+ fatality rate. As Pasteur is said to have lamented on his death bed, it's the terrain ... not the germ.

So CT strengthens your immune system (like a solid diet and lots of sun/D also do). Pair it all together and blow it away!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T05:07:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9669>

OK here is the deal.....I did the first ever evolutionary directed experiment on a human.......me. Because I did it on me and not a person there is no repercussions......I told the anesthesia people what I wanted and they thought I was nuts......but I told them about three weeks later what I did.....and why I did it and they all thought it was pretty cool then.......

I did it to prove the last two parts of the Ancient Pathway were in fact present in humans and one I could use to treat patients with chronic pain.......I am a neurosurgeon so I see tons of chronic pain all day in clinic. I also wanted to raise the stakes of poker with the paleo elitiests after Terry Wahl's and Win Hof's TED x talks, so I decided to add the MRSA infection to the mix.......i inject and smeared MRSA all over my torso and body before the surgery to make sure I coud show that the CT post op was indeed an immune up regulator and could be used to lower cytokines tremendously.....It worked for the blocking the infection and stopping all pain.......CT is quite powerful when applied properly......... I got no pre op or intra op anitbiotics at all and I got no post op antibiotics. I refused them all. I also refused all narcotics pre op and post op. In October I stoppped CTing and ate a SAD and gained 25 lbs in 8 weeks on purpose eating a SAD.......because I needed to become acutely LR to lower my pain threshold and have more pain induced by the surgery........like a diabetic would sustain.............

Then I had 12 incisons done that totaled 30 inches on my body........and I used no pain meds. I packed myself in ice right away and I had no pain at all after 3 minutes. I immediately began a program of deep CTing to overcome the pain and infection risk for the last 2.5 months........and to lose the extra weight I gained on purpose for the bio hack. My waist went up 8 inches from the surgical swelling alone post op day one. My wife witnessed the whole thing and it was not pretty......I am still not able ot wear my normal pants even today. I have two more inces of waist to regain and a total of 6 pounds left to still lose........my diet and CTing will remove here in the next month or so...........So after this third and final experiment I knew it worked on humans and I unleased it in my practice to everyone and I posted it live for the world to use themselves.......with the 2/11/2012 blog. Shortly thereafter the CT series began. The man I operated upon first on January 27th was the first person I used full blown surgerical CT on in my practice and it was a wild success......Now your up to date...........I put all my chips in the game.........and my patients got major benefits now.

he has the operation done with narcotics but used hardly any......and that shocked the anesthesia folks and the nurses on the floor......and when he left the hospital in 2.5 days with that operation everyone's eyes were like wide open. He went to rehab for only 5 days and when there he felt so good he began to play banjo for the other patients........in ten days he came back to me with no back or leg pain with a ten inch incision that went all the way from skin to the dural nerve sac and handed me his pain meds back and told me he did not need them!!!!......His operation way more invasive than what I did or was willing to do on me........but If I did not do what I did to myself on Jan 9 2012.......I would have never even considered him for surgery..........that is what made Jan 9th more special. His response to surgery really confirmed what my last experiment showed. Every case I have done since then I am now employing CT with surgery.......and those results are nothing short of amazing. What is happening in my clinic is stunning now. The power of CT was shown threw in that last experiment and that is why I had to make sure everyone adds to the their routine in getting back to optimal........now I have to go soak.......I have sick pounds and two inches left to remove.....

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T17:25:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9778>

Was he not a candidate for surgery b/c of his poor health//condition? Or because you didn't think he needed or would be helped by the surgery?

Did you ice especially heavy in the months before the surgery? Do anything special immediately before that led to no pain there? Why did you have pain after (until the icing) but not during the surgery?

One theory about the lack of pain from icing would be that it's just shutting off pain, but that that isn't helpful, as not sensing pain can lead you to do dangerous things (you know, b/c pain is an important signal to keep us from burning fingers off, etc.). Another would be that the cold is actually preventing that damage from happening, not just preventing the pain from the damage. I assume the latter is the case, since not only the pain was less, but the overall recovery quicker?

How long is one normallly in the hospital with this surgery he had?

I ask a lot because ... my grandfather is 80, diabetic, and in a wheelchair.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T17:31:51+0300

Another would be that the cold is actually preventing that damage from happening, not just preventing the pain from the damage. He was a NON SURGICAL Candidate for 18 months.......before my Jan 9th epic bio hack.......Normally he would have been in the hospital 7 -10 days with this on a PCA pump and rehab for a month.......total recovery in 3-4 months......and the likel complication rate very very high because of his medical co morbidites........

For me I did not CTing pre op while gaining weight......I was trying to get fat and trying lower my threshold for pain to make the surgery of Jan 9th MORE painful.......I even told the surgeon the day before surgery I wanted him to uber aggressive and boy was he......I looked terrible for 6 weeks......My wife still cant believe what I looked like but I went to work and did surgery without any discomfort......totally numb as I iced every single day when I came home.......

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T17:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9789>

Why, then, did you have no pain during the surgery? I'm missing that key. Was it icing \*during\* the surgery?

Thanks for the answers. This is so helpful. When you described the man, it just hit home. My grandfather was very active until about ten years ago, he started losing control of his legs. It's suspected it may go back to a cobalt treatment he had for (colon?) cancer 50 years ago, or some back thing that I can't remember that was a few decades ago, or maybe just 15-20 years ago?

He's not a paraplegic entirely, but cannot stand at all anymore, and almost has no use of his legs at all now. I don't know if I could ever convince my grandmother to do this for him (she's a trained nurse), but he loves the way I cook (tradtional, odd foods like rabbit, offal, broth, etc.) and I think he would be very open to it if only I could get past the gguard at the door, lol.

I wonder if he'd look at this if the whole experience with your patient was written up in a blog post by you or maybe by some other blogger or writer. A big newspaper would definitely help!

I had unreal pain from the surgery as soon as I woke up.......and had to travel 45 minutes home......in excruciating pain......got home and screamed at my wife and daughter to get me a homemade tub I constructed in our closet and to get 120 pounds of ice I bought without any of them knowing......and they packed me in it......and in 3 minutes I went from a 10 out of 10 in pain to a 0/10. It was stunning for them both to see it live.......we were all in tears.......they were scared and I was stoked and happy........I knew I just found something unreal in our biology.......and it could change the world. And in the last 2.5 months it has changed the fortune of many people I am dealing with........That is cool. My son Konnor still does not know this......but I just sent him the video to view.......and I am sure he is going to be stunned.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T18:29:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9808>

I had a two hour conversion with them to tell them everything........and it then I told them about Factor X and one small fact I taught my daughter when she was little........tied to Factor X.......and she was stunned. I then asked her to get my laptop and for 4 hours I wrote the last chapter of my book........and she was transfixed by the story.......she helped me write it........and my wife kept coming and checking on me and saw me joking and writing with story live down on my computer all while teaching my little one.........the next day the whole family became huge CT fans........They fully understood the implications.

The last chapter of my book will have to chopped into to several becaus eit is truly epic............and when you read the exquisite details of how I figured Factor X out you will also be stunned.......it was proabably the hardest thing I have ever figured out.......and what January 9th was really like in 3D.............I think you will understand fully why I did what I did. Without doing this my 80 yr old diabetic would still be in a wheelchair........now his life is given back to him.........new discoveries do that. And I did it all without an expensive RCT and millions of dollars.......i did it on a me a human with a plastic tub I spent ten bucks on and 120 pounds of ice........and 6K of money. That was the tools I used to prove Mother Nature's RCT of 2.5 million years was in fact built into our peripheral nervous system and central nervous system. Three weeks later while still bruisied battered and swollen.......i went straight to surgery to help Mr. Daniels........at 80 he has few years left to get to optimal.......well in ten days he got back what he never thought he could.........His response to this made all I endured........worth while. I drove home from the hospital that day with the biggest smile on my face I have ever had in my life.......I became the doctor I had always envisioned that day.........I changed someone's life using evolutionary biology and nothing fancy.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T18:29:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9809>

> **Jack Kruse;9866 wrote:** I had unreal pain from the surgery as soon as I woke up.......and had to travel 45 minutes home......in excruciating pain......got home and screamed at my wife and daughter to get me a homemade tub I constructed in our closet and to get 120 pounds of ice I bought without any of them knowing......and they packed me in it......and in 3 minutes I went from a 10 out of 10 in pain to a 0/10. It was stunning for them both to see it live.......we were all in tears.......they were scared and I was stoked and happy.

And you were posting on MDA that night.  Amazing. I don't know how you kept it under wraps; I could never do that.

BTW, I have new pics up on my journal at MDA (in my siggie) & the LR "monster thread" here. You're to blame.

yes I was........This will show you just how well CT works.......my wife remains stunned to this day. My daughter thinks what I did was totally cool........she cant wait to tell her friends at school. I told her not until the video comes out.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T18:40:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9817>

See TED is about sharing ideas........sharing ideas is not enough. Ideation without execution of those ideas......leads to deletion of the idea. I executed on all three of my thought experiments.......and now the world can see if I am mad.......or maybe I just found the answer for our species to get to optimal?

That is and was the purpose of this all.......6.5 yrs of trying to explain and prove it all.......well......my cards are now played. All my chips are in............and What ties this all together? FACTOR X is the reason Mother Nature has never extinguished this pathway in us.........and when you hear this......and why? Well you will know this known factor has had huge implications for every mammal on this planet........and our species of mammals is about the last one on this planet to realize it.......only because our own evolutionary development (the rapid brain growth) has kept it in our blind spot for 2.5 million yrs..........It is the biggest oxymoron in biology I have found and it is why I embrace all paradox and love chaos. Michealangelo, DaVinci, and Einstein all taught me as a young boy traveling in the Museums.......that I needed to think like this.......and I do. Without the lesson of their lives........I would have never been brought to Jan 9th 2012.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-01T19:24:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9844>

> **Jack Kruse;9866 wrote:** I had unreal pain from the surgery as soon as I woke up.......and had to travel 45 minutes home......in excruciating pain......got home and screamed at my wife and daughter to get me a homemade tub I constructed in our closet and to get 120 pounds of ice I bought without any of them knowing......and they packed me in it......and in 3 minutes I went from a 10 out of 10 in pain to a 0/10.

Aren't the temperature and pain pathways joined in the spinothalamic tract? If you change the threshold for one (temp by using cold) do you de-sensitize the other (pain) as well?

I think one of my neuro-psych professors said you can't feel both at the same time. There's another pathway that disables the pain gate through movement... so you can still run from the tiger even if you're injured - you won't feel the pain until you stop moving. Seen a lot in athletes.

### Jake — 2012-04-01T20:37:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9884>

Dr.K. I am the one who smashed my index finger unloading a new piece of farm equipment (bone fracture and 7 stitches). I followed your advice and immediately iced my wrist and forearm (I kept the ice on all night and as much during the days as possible). Within an hour of icing when I touched my forearm I could feel the nerve pulse all the way to the tip of my finger. Today (Sunday -- happened Thursday) I removed the bandage to wash and replace. I couldn't believe how good it looked. No swelling or black & blue. (Though I'm sure I'll lose my nail). And I've had very little pain. Took one vicodin the first afternoon which helped me sleep and ice (and listen to your past interviews). Amazing stuff. Listening to your TEDX talk, I can't imagine what you did though. Crazy & brave.

But thank you very, very much for your prompt repsonse. I am very grateful.

### Christi Lynn — 2012-04-01T20:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-9885>

Dr. Kruse,

You have taught me so much with your blog. Always so helpful in comments and so informative in the blog. I've not seen the video but just reading what was typed and what you've said had my heart racing with anticipation and excitement to see what was coming in the next sentence and then tears of joy (well in the corners, I'm at work but it's hard to hold back my excitement!). I mean to say thank you is not enough. I started CTing five weeks ago and have so many health issues but already am seeing signs of improvement in many areas, am off two meds and one left to get off of. You are so passionate, so caring and so helpful to anyone will listen and I thank you for that. You say it is a choice and mine was made after hearing you on the Paleo Summit. I dived right in, no looking back. You are unbelieveably awesome. To see what you went through, what you did, what you've proved, how you're helping people in your practice, people online and me.....I am just awestruck and more thankful than you know. I will reach optimal and my husband will too (he just started a week ago). I am ready to share with anyone who will listen whether they think I'm crazy or not will not matter but when they see where I end up after where I've been? Hopefully some will make the choice to change too! Thank you from the bottom of my heart.

> **Jack Kruse;9930 wrote:** I stopped CTing on October 15 until January 9th. At 3 pm on january I was packed in 120 lbs of ice. and it worked in 3 min. The reason......i spent 6.5 years making myself a cold adapted mammal before Oct 15th. That is why it works.

So I wonder how this translates into immune defense against chronic viral infections... like EBV, HHV6, Cytomegalovirus.... or heck - regular ole herpes of the STD type. Can one cold adapt and kick those, or would one need to kick it with antivirals while cold adapting to prevent re-infection?

I finally was able to get some alone time to just reflect on Dr. Kruse's experiment this morning. I went to Lowe's to pick up some things for my husband and I thought "I need to sit here for 5 minutes to process on this". An hour later.... my phone was ringing and husband was worried about where I was LOL.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-02T07:10:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-tedx-talk-to-be-streamed-between-4-5pm-today-sat-mar-31.568/post-10209>

> Sutherland Simpson has shown that during deep anesthesia a warm-blooded animal tends to take the same temperature as that of its environment. He demonstrated that when a monkey is kept deeply anesthetized with ether and is placed in a cold chamber, its temperature gradually falls, and that when it has reached a sufficiently low point (about 25Â°C in the monkey), the employment of an anesthetic is no longer necessary to maintain anesthesia. Pretty nifty trick for a surgeon to know. The animal becomes insensible to pain and incapable of being roused by any form of stimulus; it is, in fact, narcotized by cold, and is in a state of what may be called “artificial hibernation.” Once again this is explained by the fact that the heat-regulating mechanism has been interfered with. Similar results have been obtained from experiments on cats. I believe the same is true in humans as well because I tested this on me.

That is a paragraph from CT6. Doc, did you hibernate during your surgery?

# Thread: Guy parts too cold?

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Bill Strahan — 2012-03-31T22:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guy-parts-too-cold.570/post-9360>

I took a 45 minute bath in 45 degree water a few days ago, but since I was inside this time I didn't wear my shorts. I didn't think about the compression of the shorts holding everything up against me, and without the shorts the tip of things got so cold that it hurt afterwards for about an hour!

Next time I wore shorts!

So will that adapt too, or is it asking too much? As long as I'm here, is the higher T triggered by CT regardless or do the testicles need some serious cold to get the most effect?

### JC2K — 2012-03-31T22:44:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guy-parts-too-cold.570/post-9368>

# Thread: Confessions from a hot shower!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-31T23:59:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-9406>

Ok, so I did my tub today, proud of myself after hitting two sessions yesterday for a total of 45 mins. I wanted to bust out 25 mins before the grind.

Before today, I had been taking my normal hot shower to clean off while the tub fills with cold. This would leave the water clean for my evening dip without having to refill. The few mins between hot shower and cold tub SUCKED.

So I tried opposite today, came in from a walk and decided to go strait for the cold tub then I would shower to finish cleaning. I have NOT been able to do real cold showers. 2 mins max, gave it up for the tub.

Well friends, after CT I was shivering as usual, and when I got in that warm stream. . . B L I S S. I kept going hotter. I couldn't make myself get out. I stood in there in scalding water, deciding to stay till we ran out. What had to be at least 20 mins later I remembered we have hot water on demand so it doesn't run out! I stayed. AND STAYED. I stayed until I thought I was going to be late to a meeting. The whole time I fantasized about how nice it would be to go to the spa and hit the steam room or hot tub.

So, is this a net cold thing? Will heat exposure undo the CT? I soooo want the answer to be NO, sauna away ! I mean, over the past year I've given up all pleasures - is it sooo wrong to want one little teeny thing that I super enjoy?

I bet knowing I could hop into a sauna would make it easier to do a really long cold tub.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T00:16:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-9423>

I took a shower today for the first time in ages and couldn't stand the thought of it being hot. Lukewarm shower, yay!

How different we all are!

### Kasra — 2012-04-01T00:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-9428>

Do what feels right.

I've been intermittently taking cold showers for about a year, and the most effective way for me to cold-adapt has always been to work with my body and trust what it tells me. I have been compelled to take insanely long hot showers from time to time.

I truly doubt that your hot shower was anything but productive.

### Jim — 2012-04-01T00:25:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-9436>

I do a hot shower every now and then. I wouldn't worry about it.

### Adriana — 2012-04-05T17:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11742>

I thought Dr. K said in a reply elsewhere that it is not a good thing...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-05T17:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11744>

He did........but humans do not often do what Mother Nature asks.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-05T17:40:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11748>

I've found that I really can't stand a Hot shower... even luke warm is still too hot...

### chocolate — 2012-04-05T17:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11751>

My conditioner won't rinse without warm. I don't have paleo hair. Total mismatch, lol.

### Valorie33 — 2012-04-05T17:46:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11752>

> **Shijin13;11817 wrote:** I've found that I really can't stand a Hot shower... even luke warm is still too hot...

I don't enjoy a hot shower anymore either.

Also I am finding I don't care if my food gets cold. Eggs were a big thing, hated my eggs to get cold, now if everything is cooked and I have something else that needs to be done for a few minutes I just go do it and come back to lukewarm eggs and they taste just fine.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-05T18:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11757>

It's funny, a shower that just a month ago was COLD to me is now very pleasant. It's still hard for me to turn the shower on all the way cold despite the fact that it's about the same temp as my baths (50ish degrees).

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-04-05T19:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11795>

During daylight I am good with cold water, cold shower, ice....

When the sun goes down I just can't do it. If I force myself I regret it because I just don't warm up after getting out. It's like my body is shut down and ready to sleep.

So if I don't get clean before sunset I just have to wait for the next day....

It'll be interesting to see if and when this changes as I heal.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-05T19:33:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11799>

I use warm water to rinse when showering and end with cold shower or just on my face depending on mood.

As far as shampoo and conditioner use is concerned. I tried going without both last December and haven't looked back. My scalp is healthier and hair healthy. If it gets dry I may add a little CO. Other than that, no more shampoo or conditioner, just don't need or want it anymore.

### dkenward@yahoo.com — 2012-04-05T20:35:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11817>

For years I used to get out of the shower feeling hot. But I didn't think a cold shower would get me "clean enough". A few times my hot water was out, so I took a cold shower and it felt miserable. Now I thoroughly enjoy it. In fact today I was thinking "too bad I can't get the water any colder!"

### ealachan — 2012-04-05T20:48:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11824>

I've always been a cool- to-lukewarm shower kinda girl, even as a kid. Shortly after we moved into our new place, I was in the shower and my husband decided he wanted to join me. He got one leg in, felt the water, and shrieked like a girl! Needless to say, I now get to shower in private.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-06T00:28:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-11951>

I still take a warm shower (not as hot as before) and then switch to cold after I'm done washing and just hang out for about 5 min. in the cold water.

If you have vascular issues like I do (or even circulation issues), there is an advantage to a hot/cold combo. Hot relaxes the blood vessels and then cold abruptly constricts them. This is great for the elasticity and strength of the blood vessels. Classic hydrotherapy calls it "blood vessel gymnastics". I've seen a big improvement in my vascular condition.

### Birdy — 2012-04-13T23:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-14865>

Choco, try cooler water and no cream rinse. I find the heat of the water greatly affects my hair texture.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-14T00:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-14913>

> **RiverNeighbor;11864 wrote:** During daylight I am good with cold water, cold shower, ice....
>
> When the sun goes down I just can't do it. If I force myself I regret it because I just don't warm up after getting out. It's like my body is shut down and ready to sleep.
>
> So if I don't get clean before sunset I just have to wait for the next day....
>
>
>
> It'll be interesting to see if and when this changes as I heal.

I know what you mean, RiverNeighbor. I've moved my CT sessions back to mornings because of difficulties warming up. It did make me sleep like a zombie, though, once I finally fell asleep.

### Guest — 2012-04-14T01:52:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-14945>

I have only taken cold showers and one bath (hated with a passion). Will still try again and hope someday it will happen, like it has for you guys. Ever since my first cold shower, I never went back to warm or hot and just can't see the benefit in it.

While the warmth of a shower might feel good when in it, the cold shower has benefits after. Like sitting naked with the window open and feel the cool breeze, only I am warm and toasty all over my body. The rest of my day is very good.

### Larry — 2012-04-14T02:38:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-14951>

> **Trisha;11826 wrote:** It's funny, a shower that just a month ago was COLD to me is now very pleasant. It's still hard for me to turn the shower on all the way cold despite the fact that it's about the same temp as my baths (50ish degrees).

I laugh everytime I get in the shower now for the same reason. Two months ago I would been thinking that the water was freezing but now it just feels downright awesome (after the initial water to skin contact ).

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-14T14:53:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-15033>

I forgot to turn the temperature down from my OH's shower the other morning, and realised that it was way too hot. It used to be that I would turn the thermostat up ...

### nonchalant — 2012-04-15T19:30:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-15334>

Ah, Other Half! I thought for a moment that OH meant ornery husband.

### growltgc — 2012-09-21T05:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53115>

I guess I'm going to cut the warm/hot showers. Maybe it's taking me longer to adapt this way. because I still hate the idea of a cold shower and love warm. Also when i swim and it's cloudy out - almost always under 60 degrees air temp - I usually go to a warm cafe and sometimes get a hot coffee to help warm up. so I guess I will try without these things.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-21T23:37:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53301>

> **growltgc;53750 wrote:** I guess I'm going to cut the warm/hot showers. Maybe it's taking me longer to adapt this way. because I still hate the idea of a cold shower and love warm. Also when i swim and it's cloudy out - almost always under 60 degrees air temp - I usually go to a warm cafe and sometimes get a hot coffee to help warm up. so I guess I will try without these things.

You may try actually getting the water significantly colder. As ironic as it may sound, I find that my 'internal furnace' kicks on (my skin feels like it's on fire and turns lobster-red, and I feel comfortably warm) at the colder water temps (mid to high 40s), but not when the water is any warmer. In fact, if I soak in high 70s water I will actually go pale white and develop severe fatigue and a deep chill that even a hot shower can't shake (hypothermia).

The warmer water temps don't trigger your brain to induce a winter-mode response, so you just insidiously lose heat until your core temp drops--that's very bad. When the water is sufficiently cold, your brain switches over to winter mode and actively turns on the cold thermogenesis pathway--which is exactly what we're trying to do. We're not trying burn more calories or just temporarily activate BAT, we're trying to tell the brain that it's Winter out and to shift to a the cold-adapted hormonal regime. You only do that with a really cold stimulus to the periphery while your core temps remain stable.

Anything warmer and you're just torturing yourself at best, and most likely making yourself hypothermic.

### growltgc — 2012-09-21T23:53:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53302>

I agree that I like the colder temps more (after the initial plunge). I swim in the bay and the temp ranges 53-63 degrees. Usually I try and swim at high tide, when the water is in the lower range.

So, what do you do after you get out of the water? When its cold

### nonchalant — 2012-09-22T01:06:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53316>

**growltgc;53937 wrote:** So, what do you do after you get out of the water? When its cold

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T01:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53317>

> **Nonchalant;53951 wrote:** If you are able to, try drip drying for an hour or so. Don't towel off. You will find that you will not start shivering until you put on clothes. Thermogenesis shuts down, when the skin feels the warmth. Then the muscles have to take up the slack and shiver to keep you warm.

I concurr, the shivers and uncomfortablness is after i start to warm up my skin.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-22T05:51:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53361>

My experience with showers is a bit different. I started taking my regular shower to wash and rinse, then start turning down the temperature. I now find that I start the shower out cooler than I used to.

I still enjoy an occassional hot shower. This house was built by a plumbing contractor, so it has crazy good water pressure and endless hot water. In fact, after we moved in, I almost gave up CT showers and I have trouble getting in and mostly out of the tub (getting better).

### Endless — 2012-09-23T03:16:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53539>

> The warmer water temps don't trigger your brain to induce a winter-mode response, so you just insidiously lose heat until your core temp drops--that's very bad. When the water is sufficiently cold, your brain switches over to winter mode and actively turns on the cold thermogenesis pathway--which is exactly what we're trying to do. We're not trying burn more calories or just temporarily activate BAT, we're trying to tell the brain that it's Winter out and to shift to a the cold-adapted hormonal regime. You only do that with a really cold stimulus to the periphery while your core temps remain stable.
>
>
>
> Anything warmer and you're just torturing yourself at best, and most likely making yourself hypothermic.

I know that in the webinar Dr K suggested that colder temps can speed up the adaptation, but I'm assuming that 50-55 is cold enough to provide the trigger....since that is what is suggested in the easy start guide. If all I'm doing is torturing myself and inducing hypothermia at that temp I'm not super inclined to continue!

Anyone else have an opinion on the maximum temp at which benefit is recieved?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T03:18:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53540>

I cant do the shower........but in deep cold water.......I am Orca like.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-09-23T07:47:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53564>

Interesting thought: Perhaps those who are cold adapted are actually warming a boundary layer of water around them. I've noticed in my limited experience that moving around in the tub is alot more intolerable.

### Endless — 2012-09-23T18:55:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-53611>

I know that is true for me, although I don't really consider myself cold adapted after so short a time. I start at tap water temp (56) and after 30 minutes the water is up to 59-60 if I don't add ice. I know if I stay too still it is warmer, I often 'sway' a bit to move the warm layer away.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-25T20:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54178>

> **Anna K.;54135 wrote:** Do you still shiver after you get out?
>
>
>
> thanks.

Yes, violently.  I'm not sure if that means one is not fully cold adapted yet for that duration or if that will always be the case.

Now if the water was warmer and I didn't get my lobster on, I don't shiver much but rather feel extremely fatigued and desire to cover up in something warm and to sleep (or perhaps lay down and die, haha).

If it was cold enough to evoke the CT response, on the other hand, then I feel the need to get active somehow to generate heat and avoid covering up (that traps in an insulating later of cold air and prolongs the cold exposure, which may be more than I can handle at the time).

### growltgc — 2012-09-25T20:31:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54183>

I figured out that I don't really need to go to a warm place after a swim. both days it was cloudy. water temp 54 and 57 respectively. I let myself shiver, and afterwards I felt quite warm. My walk back to the bus, felt warmer, than my walk to the bay.

The day it was 54 degrees, I laid on my stomach, and most of the shivering concentrated into my legs, which I liked. The day it was 57, I had less shivering, and mostly did it standing up. my legs wouldn't get into a good rhythm while lying down. I dunno if this happens to most people, but I notice while laying / sitting down, the shivers can cause some aches or a temporary soreness feeling in the hip area.

It's interesting how you do how some control over the shivering process. sometimes i let it go full speed, because I think it will end sooner if I do that. Other times I try holding it back. When it gets to a certain strength I can shut the shivers off, and only get a few sporadically afterwards.

I took a mild shower yesterday not warm, not cool. this at least alleviates potential risk of a hot shower offsetting the the CT (I don't know if there is or not - but just to be safe). I don't plan on cold showers for now. unless I come to like it like some people.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-25T20:32:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54184>

> **Endless;54181 wrote:** I know that in the webinar Dr K suggested that colder temps can speed up the adaptation, but I'm assuming that 50-55 is cold enough to provide the trigger....since that is what is suggested in the easy start guide. If all I'm doing is torturing myself and inducing hypothermia at that temp I'm not super inclined to continue!
>
>
>
> Anyone else have an opinion on the maximum temp at which benefit is recieved?

If you saw the webinar then you probably have more info than me. This is just my reading of the CT blogs and personal experience.

My understanding was that skin temps--not just water temps--need to be in that range (I measure skin with an infrared thermometer, and measure water with a cooking/meat thermometer as the infrared gives misleading results for water). In my experience, the water has to be a bit colder (like high 40s) to get my skin temps down to that range.

Don't give up before you try colder. Forget the mid-range stuff and try the high-40s water, even if it's only for a few minutes and you have to take a warm shower afterwards. I don't think it's helpful to try to work your way down gradually through lower and lower temps, as I think that is what it torturing and endangering yourself.

Like Dr. K said, ironically it's easier to take the really cold water than the warmer stuff. I'm willing to bet that you'll find that your body responds to the high-40s water in a qualitatively different way than anything warmer. You'll feel a piercing pain like a million ice-picks stabbing you at first if you're not used to that cold of a water temp, then you'll feel your skin start to burn like it's on fire and take on a red tint, and after a bit you'll get the sensation of wamness all over your body.

Scary, I know, but it's one of those all-or-nothing things for this response. Warner will still get you BAT activation and other goodies, but this particular response just needs that level of cold.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-25T21:28:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54215>

My bathtub has jets that will self-turn on to push out residual water in them. OMG when those jets self-turn on and I'm CTing... !!! Now that's some serious cold!! They stay on about a minute, and I just hold on and breathe! Now light reading through that kind of experience. Then when they turn off and I'm back to just the still cold water, I feel all warm and burny, at least for a little while.

> **T Field;54206 wrote:** Interesting thought: Perhaps those who are cold adapted are actually warming a boundary layer of water around them. I've noticed in my limited experience that moving around in the tub is alot more intolerable.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-25T22:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54238>

> **T Field;54206 wrote:** Interesting thought: Perhaps those who are cold adapted are actually warming a boundary layer of water around them. I've noticed in my limited experience that moving around in the tub is alot more intolerable.

The moving water speeds up the heat transfer. that's why if you fall in freezing water, you're better off staying put than trying to swim. That's assuming of course you're not right next to shore! If that's the case, swim!

### teller — 2012-09-27T03:13:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54648>

I've been taking some cold showers recently just to warm up to the idea of cold...I thought it was pretty darn uncomfortable, so I checked with my thermometer and it was only about 70 degrees F. I didn't think 55 degrees sounded all that cold, but this started me wondering about my perception.

Tonight I began face dunking, and this time I made a sure it was below 55 F. Well...HOLY MOTHER OF GOD 50-55 IS FREAKIN' COLD.

What have I gotten myself into? LOL

Seriously, though...I'm doing this. Bring it on.

### caroline — 2012-09-27T04:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54668>

> **Jack Kruse;54182 wrote:** I cant do the shower........but in deep cold water.......I am Orca like.

I find it hard to believe that there is something you can't do!

### growltgc — 2012-09-27T04:22:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54670>

I don't do face dunks. but basically I do the same thing. After I get in the bay, I swim like mad for a quarter minute or so with head above water, until the shock wears off. then I slowly adjust my head to the water, by dunking multiple times. then I start to do relaxed swimming... or floating

I wrote this in a another thread, but the cold water actually hurts my head until my head becomes use to it. That's why I do the multiple dunks because its easier transition without pain. at 55 degrees it starts. And each degree colder its a magnitude more painful. Do you feel this or no?

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-27T05:18:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confessions-from-a-hot-shower.571/post-54681>

> **teller;55469 wrote:** I've been taking some cold showers recently just to warm up to the idea of cold...I thought it was pretty darn uncomfortable, so I checked with my thermometer and it was only about 70 degrees F. I didn't think 55 degrees sounded all that cold, but this started me wondering about my perception.
>
>
>
> Tonight I began face dunking, and this time I made a sure it was below 55 F. Well...HOLY MOTHER OF GOD 50-55 IS FREAKIN' COLD.
>
>
>
> What have I gotten myself into? LOL
>
>
>
> Seriously, though...I'm doing this. Bring it on.

I live on the shores of Lake Erie. Back in the late 1970's, my dad took a 21' boat on trade (we owned a new car dealership). Memorial Day weekend, we would start our water skiing. Some years the water was maybe 60 degrees. We thought this was normal. No wet suits or anything, just a bathing suits. Wet suits were for wimps. The warmest the water would usually get was 80ish after a lot of sunny hot days around the beginning of August.

I too started taking showers this year. I have trouble getting out of the tub alone. When I shower, I start out warmish to wash up and rinse. Then I start turning the water toward cold, this is called 'The Scottish Shower' or 'The James Bond Shower' because Ian Fleming wrote abone it in one of his early James Bond books.

I had at my old house a hand held shower head attached to my regular shower head with a worn bypass valve. This allowed me to spray front and back simultaneously. I believe I got the most out of my CT showers then.

In my new house I do not have the luxury of well used equipment. I turn as I am adjusting the water and that makes it less harsh. Mostly, I let the water spray on my back. It is not as optimum as full body immersion, but it works.

Ray Cronise is a proponent of the lower than body temperature showers. He believes they are beneficial even at 80 degrees. That is based on his own experience when losing weight. Dr. Kruse gives good evidence for swimming in the cold water and below 60 and 50 degrees. On this forum, you will find many people who actually crave the 40 degree water.

All I can say is keep at it. I find you do crave the colder water. My own experience is, I am starting my shower cooler and turning down the water sooner. You will surprise yourself.

# Thread: Cold mice affecting drug test results?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-mice-affecting-drug-test-results.575/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SimonM — 2012-04-01T03:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-mice-affecting-drug-test-results.575/post-9613>

Holy cow! "They" are finally noticing what Jack has been saying....cold biochemistry is different from heat biochem...

Comfy Mice Lead to Better Science: Are Cold Mice Affecting Drug Testing?

ScienceDaily (Mar. 30, 2012) â€” Nine out of 10 drugs successfully tested in mice and other animal models ultimately fail to work in people, and one reason may be traced back to a common fact of life for laboratory mice: **they're cold,** according to a researcher at the Stanford University School of Medicine.

Laboratory mice, who account for the vast majority of animal research subjects, are routinely housed in chilly conditions, which may affect their well-being as well as the outcome of research studies, said Joseph Garner, PhD, associate professor of comparative medicine.

"If you want to design a drug that will help a patient in the hospital, you cannot reasonably do that in animals that are cold-stressed and are compensating with an elevated metabolic rate," Garner said. "This will change all aspects of their physiology -- such as how fast the liver breaks down a drug -- which can't help but increase the chance that a drug will behave differently in mice and in humans."

In a new study, Garner and his colleagues report finding an easy solution to the problem: Simply provide the animals with the proper materials, and they'll build a cozy nest that allows them to naturally regulate their temperatures to a comfortable level. These thermally content mice would be more physiologically comparable to humans and thus might serve as more meaningful research subjects, Garner said.

"Why not let them do what they do in the wild, which is build nests? Mice can happily infest a meat freezer, with temperatures far below zero, but they survive and breed because they build these wonderful nests," he said.

The study, part of nearly seven years of work with mouse nesting behavior, is the first to "ask" mice to rate the value of nesting material in terms of temperature savings, which is an important first step in setting standards for nesting material, said Garner, whose work has focused on the well-being of the mouse. He is the senior author of the study, which was published online March 30 in PLoS ONE.

Mice, which Garner calls "one of the most fantastic animals on Earth," have evolved in the same environment as humans for thousands of years, making them remarkably adaptable, able to live virtually anywhere. For that reason, they make excellent research subjects, with hundreds of millions of them populating laboratories around the world.

Given the option, mice gravitate to temperatures of between 30 and 32 degrees Celsius (the equivalent of about 86 and 90 degrees Fahrenheit). But based on federal regulations, U.S. research laboratories are routinely kept on the cold side -- between 20 to 24 degrees C. There can be advantages to these cold temperatures. For instance, mice have aggressive tendencies that are suppressed in cooler climes. Female mice also lactate better in cooler temperatures, though their pups don't do as well in the cold.

When kept in temperatures toward the low end of this scale -- between about 18 and 20 degrees C (64-68 degrees F) -- the mice begin to show changes in immune function and their growth may be retarded. "So we're housing them right at that threshold," Garner said. "That means the mice may be compromised physiologically, potentially affecting research results."

Simply raising the temperature in the lab isn't an option, not least because the mice would then become unmanageably aggressive, he said. Rather, Garner and his colleagues looked to other options in their study, which involved 36 male and 36 female mice of three common strains. The researchers created sets of two cages linked by a small tube so the mice could move between them. One cage in each set was maintained at a chilly 20 degrees C (68 degrees F) and was equipped with varying quantities of shredded paper, which the animals could use to construct nests for shelter and warmth. The other cage was kept at one of six temperatures: 20, 23, 26, 29, 32 or 35 degrees C (68, 73, 79, 84, 90 or 95 F), but without nesting material.

The mice then had the choice of staying put and tolerating the cold, choosing a balmier cage, eating more to add fat and elevate metabolic rate, or building a nest.

Each strain and sex had slightly different preferences, the researchers found. None was content to simply sit out the cold, either moving to a toastier location, if available, or building elaborate, dome-like nests to warm themselves. The more nest-building material they had, the more they were willing to settle for a cooler clime, as the nests served to temper the chill, the researchers found.

In fact, the nest-building drive was so strong that the mice often would spend hours collecting strands of paper, bit by bit, from the chilliest cage and then transporting it to a more comfortable spot in another cage to build a sturdy little home.

Garner said these mice decided they wanted to have it all, choosing a warm spot and building a nest as well. "Naughty little rascals" is how he described them. "They would go on holiday somewhere warm AND take their nest with them," he said. "Some people like to take a pillow on holiday and some don't. These mice were packing their own pillow."

The fact that some mice moved nesting material to the warmer cage means that the nests serve a function beyond warmth, argued Garner, perhaps providing physical comfort, or a form of protection that decreases the animals' anxiety and stress levels.

The nest-building mice tended to eat less, as they didn't need the extra calories to satisfy their higher metabolic demands, the researchers found. In general, the females preferred warmer temperatures than the males -- by about 5 degrees: they are smaller and have less fat to generate heat.

The researchers concluded that the mice could manage with 6 grams of nesting material but sometimes could use as much as 10 grams, suggesting the larger amount be supplied routinely in research labs.

Another benefit of the nests is that they facilitate researchers' work with the mice -- it's easier to pick them up as well as observe them. "The shape of the nest tells an experienced person whether the animals are too hot or too cold, whether they are sick or whether they are about to give birth," Garner said. "Once you learn how to 'speak mouse nest,' the nest is a wonderful tool that anyone can use to assess the general state of the mouse."

The study's first author is Brianna Gaskill, PhD, at the Charles River Laboratories. Garner and Gaskill collaborated with colleagues at the University of Calgary, Purdue University and the Environmental Protection Agency. The study was supported by a grant from the Universities Federation for Animal Welfare, the Charles River Laboratories (which donated the mice used in the study) and Fiber Core, which donated the nesting material.

Story Source: the above story is reprinted from materials provided by Stanford University Medical Center. The original article was written by Ruthann Richter.

Journal Reference: Brianna N. Gaskill et al. Heat or Insulation: Behavioral Titration of Mouse Preference for Warmth or Access to a Nest. PLoS ONE, 2012; 7 (3): e32799 DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0032799

Source: [sciencedaily.com](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120330205921.htm)

### shilohman — 2012-04-01T23:16:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-twitch.577/post-9949>

> **Trisha;9828 wrote:** Doing the Leptin Rx. Eating lots of grass fed beef, lots of eggs, some pork, a bit of chicken. Veggies mainly consist of lettuces and spinach. Occasionally I have cucumbers, avacado, cabbage, and broccoli. I use coconut oil, olive oil (for salad dressings), the drippings from bacon, and butter.
>
>
>
> Supplements/meds - Multivitamin, K2, B complex, pqq, fish oil, krill oil, bitter melon, magnesium, D3, iodine, armour thyroid
>
>
>
> I've been doing CT for about a month and progressing along well.
>
>
>
> No testing.

Trisha, if you can, get your omega 6/3 tested. Why? Because you probably have an high 6/3 ratio (we all do considering what we have eaten in the past). Even if you can not get tested, just assume that yours is very high. Why? Because of what you have eaten in the past and some of what you are eating now.

1\. eggs. Are these from free range fed chickens? If not, then the eggs are loaded with high omega 6 because they are fed soy or corn feed. You gotta have free range.

2\. pork. Is it grass fed? If not, then it is very high in omega 6. Cut the pork.

3\. chicken. Very high in omega 6, cut the chicken.

4\. lettuces, probably O.K. except iceberg lettuce. Iceberg has high preclorate (spelling?) which is a toxin. No iceberg.

5\. Krill oil & Fish oil? Go read Dr. K's blog on fish oil and do a search for what he says on krill oil. Actually, I just remembered what he said about krill. Krill oil is the #1 Rx that he prescribes at his office. My mind is fuzzy right now, but I thing the omega 3 in the Krill oil is in a phospholipid (spelling?) while the fish oil omega 3 is in triglyceride form.

The body has more uses for omega 3 from krill than fish oil.

You really need to get tested of omega 6/3, because you do not want to have a higher 3 than 6, because then that causes other problems. But for now, Its probably safe to assume you are high in 6. So, eventually, you will want to stop the Krill and get your omega 6 and 3s from your diet.

So, yes the eye twitch is probably from your CT detox and you are releasing a lot of estrogen and omega 6 into your body. Dr. K recommends a lot of mushrooms, which are high in B vitamins and counteract the estrogen. I eat a lot of mushrooms and take extra B complex.

Dr. K, says that excess omega 6 can cause nerve problems, which I am generalizing to any eye twitch.

Get your omega 6/3 in balance ( 4/1 or better) and get tested at sometime.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-02T06:07:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-twitch.577/post-10173>

shilohman - thanks for the input!

The eggs are free range eggs. I get them from a couple of local farms in the area, it's nice that it's one thing I don't have to pay much more than eggs from the store!

The pork is just from the store. We recently purchased part of a grass fed cow and are hoping in another month or so to do the same with a pig. Probably will just cut it out until we can buy our own pig.

The chicken we eat is very minimal, but I'll try to just cut it out for now.

I never buy iceburg - yuck!

Now I'm wondering if another thing I've been experiencing is also a nerve thing. I've just the last few days been having trouble with my wrists when I CT. They are fine after they get warmed up, but when I move my wrists in the water and as soon as I get out it almost feels like electricity shooting up my arm. It also causes me a lot of pain in my wrists when I move my fingers in the water. It's incredibly bizzare. I thought maybe it was just my wrists/arms needing to get used to the water, but it also strikes me as odd that it's just recently started happening and I've been doing CT fully submerged for a while now.

I'll be requesting my omega 6/3 checked next month when I'm at my doctor. It will only be the second time I see him, but he struck me as a very progressive thinker and willing to work with me when I saw him for the first time!

# Thread: When The Apple Is Ripe It Will (Fall) Be Optimall

### Honey Crisp — 2012-04-01T15:43:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-9745>

Iâ€™m forty-four, 5â€™4″, wife and mother of seven children. (Yes, I gave birth to all seven. I know you were wondering.)

I effortlessly maintained my weight at 132 pounds most of my adult life eating SAD. Iâ€™ve struggled with my weight since the birth of my last baby in 2005. I ballooned to 223 pounds in pregnancy. I easily gain fifty or seventy pounds with each pregnancy, but easily lost it with calories restriction, avoiding sweets and eating salads.

The day I gave birth to my last child I was 223 pounds, and remained there post partum. I had to seriously diet and exercise to get back to my prepregnancy weight. It took nine another months to lose it all. But, I couldnâ€™t maintain my weight and gained back fifty pounds in just a few months. My doctor said that because I was older I wouldnâ€™t bounce back the way I had before, though I had in fact lost the pregnancy weight (but gained it back).

Two years later my family doctor gave me a complete physical. He said I had hypothyroidism and put me on Levothyroxine. I wes diagnosed with insulin resistance, too. I learned about HCG and started Simeonsâ€™ Protocol in 2009. I did three rounds and reached 125 pounds, and can maintain my weight following a paleo lifestyle. If I deviate even a little I can gain ten pounds in a few days.

I've developed binge eating and long cardio session trying to get healthy. Iâ€™ve reached the point where I just couldn't do it any more. This apple needs to get optimal before it rots.....

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T17:39:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-9786>

### vlynnb — 2012-04-01T18:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-9801>

I applaud you and hope you are able to restore yourself, feel fresh again. This could be my story, 5'3" after losing some height to due to age or osteoporosis. Always lost all of my pregnancy weight after 6 pregnancies and 2 miscarriages. After a third miscarriage or fetal demise I didn't loose and gained 40 pounds in just a few months. Took half of it off with low fat, high fiber and daily vigorous walks. The other half I used fen-phen to lose back to 132. Gained it back over the next 26 years on whatever was considered a healthy diet at the time. Felt depressed, had multiple allergies and developed osteoporosis and high blood pressure, in general felt rotten and couldn't look forward to a productive future with good quality of life. After using hhcg to take off 28 pounds, and feeling much better, I searched for something to help keep it off and found Dr. Kruse. I was especially interested as I really am frightened of the osteoporosis that plagued my mother.

And I want to be healthy enough to work in my apple orchard (no Honeycrisp but a lot of delicious apples I hope to be able to add back into my diet at some point).

Glad you are able to start your journey back to optimal 20 some years earlier than I. I also hope your family cooperates. My deceased husband, who was a physician, would be able to be open minded and behind me on this but my very health conscious, athletic, children who try to follow a good diet and hard exercise schdule according to CW as I have taught them don't have time in their young lives to read the literature and are skeptical at this point. I'll be following your progress and cheering for you.

### Honey Crisp — 2012-04-04T15:56:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-11245>

Today's Weight: 131.4 pounds

I lost the weight gained from Sunday's night indulgence. I did it with strict protocol eating and CT. The extra long walks with dogs and water might of helped, too. My fasting glucose was 73 this morning. The lowest it's ever been since I've started testing it. My keto strip showed large this morning, another first for me.

I soaked in the tub for 20 minutes last night. That's a record for me. I had shivers for about an hour after my bath. That's a first for me, too. Good or bad? This morning the arthritic feeling is back. Its less intense than the first week.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-10T20:45:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-13425>

Yup, it's OK if you shiver...just means you're not fully cold adapted, but you are working your way to it. One day you might notice less shivering and feel very hot on your face or overall body. Also, with the arthritis, if it seems more painful some days than others, it is the non-linear progression of CT. Overall, you will notice less pain.

Don't worry about the body composition too much as that will definitely come in time. You will look more toned and for no exercise. You say you're in your 40's, and since you haven't been CT'ing since your 20's (I'm lucky since I get this advantage) then you might consider BHRT as Dr. K suggests depending on your labs. He DID say that CT and keto can circumvent the need for BHRT, but I'm not sure if he meant for someone that's been using this protocol for years (so for someone like me in 20-30 years).

Good luck and keep up the good work. You know you're not 231 lbs anymore and you WILL get to your condition from last summer WITHOUT the excessive training. Walking is still good to do though no matter what. That's the 'exercise' I do too and instead it looks like I swim a marathon or something.

### Honey Crisp — 2012-04-11T14:04:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-apple-is-ripe-it-will-fall-be-optimall.579/post-13686>

Today's Weight: 126.4 pounds

Meausements

Bicep: 11"

Chest: 32 1/2"

Natural Waist: 26"

Waist (Navel) 30"

Hip: 37"

Thigh: 21 3/4"

I stopped doing CT baths and showers for a few days. The aches in my joints became to much for me. I continued with the face dunks. I plan on resuming the baths today.

It's hard for to believe the flatness of my stomach and a bit of muscle definition developing in my arms, without any exercise!

# Thread: Children  the Leptin Reset
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:15:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-9834>

So this is a thread for questions and experiences with kids doing the leptin reset. I don't know of any doing it! I have lots of questions about it, like

\- How young can you become leptin resistant? Are some born that way?

\- How young is it practical to start the leptin reset? Not for newborns, obviously, but what about 5yo's? Better guideline?

\- Should the protein amount be tweaked? I know that the 50g worked for 15yo girls, but what about pre-pubescent children or even toddlers, with smaller bodies?

\- Are the signs of LR different for children? I have one who doesn't have a fat belly, but he's always borderline on the BMI-for-age charts, and very fluffy overall. 10lb at birth (I wasn't GD, but that alone seems a miracle). Is physical size the only factor?

\- Will the leptin reset benefit other children (like my paleo 13yo with occasional headaches, body pains, & usual low energy)? Or should they do the "regular program" like non-LR adults? (I'll start a separate thread for that.)

\- Are the meal timing rules (not just no snacking, but distance between sleep & meals) the same?

I know I need to work much harder on meal timing for my children, which is a challenge to get them finished eating 3.5hrs before bed, since some of them sleep so much!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:17:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-9837>

Here's how I pulled details from the LR for my children:

The rule for my kids is that they all have to have protein that is at least 1/6 of their body weight (I started this rule when I did Radiant Recovery) for each meal. Sounds picky, but what it really means is that all have to have 1 (or 2, for the big kids) eggs (or equivalent in meat) for each meal, including breakfast, which is the one they most resist. (And since I started the LR, I moved them all up to 2 (or 3) eggs/oz-meat.

They may not ever skip breakfast, but they may skip lunch (but will not get a late afternoon snack just b/c they're hungry if they do).

What I've seen is similar to what I saw in me adding the BAB - the little ones who were melting down each afternoon stopped as soon as I required them to have an egg each morning. Just like that. Cravings started to diminish.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-02T14:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-10257>

I use the 2-3 oz rule for protein at each meal. 3 oz for the 9yr old (severely underweight at 55lbs) and 2 oz for the 6 yr old (47lbs). The do eat this at breakfast and about the same at lunch at school. Sometimes the lunch comes home unfinished. They are ready to eat dinner at 5. Regrettably dad doesn't get home 'til 6. So I do indulge them with a snack as for the most part it is an appetizer they are snibbling off the food we are preparing. With late afternoon snack/dinner, I am using a time zone approach. Dinner is just a leisurely 2 hours from about 5-7 where they are eating, getting homework done, helping make the dinner and eating it. There may also be some playtime in there. I just don't stress that it isn't regimented at the end of the day. Too much insanity happens between 3-7 pm in most American households and I strongly believe that one of the keys to health is eating real food, in your own home, staying connected to the people you love.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T15:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-10274>

excellent Thread Mama G!

W/the kids They have to have a protein at bkfst... sometimes this is hard b/c DH still hasn't given up cereal.. (ughh - he won't read the literature, and thinks his diet is fine and that wheat is good for you!)

every morning the kids have eggs for bkfst - dd usually eats one, ds will eat 1-2 depending how hungry he is... sometimes my HAB becomes a SAB (Small Ass Breakfast) b/c he'll polish off 2 eggs + 1/2 my breakfast!

I'm considering getting the kids food allergies tested (IgA and all) via blood work to see what they really are allergic too... I was looking at DD's tongue and I think she might have some candida over growth (DH as well) but the challenge is getting DH/MIL/my parents & DCP on board to limit her sweets....

some days I feel like I'm fighting a loosing battle - and I just want to throw all the crap (DH has) in the house.... grrrrr - it was nice these past 40 days not fighting him regarding giving the kids wheat - b/c he wasn't eating it...

### JasonLC — 2012-10-03T15:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-56051>

[](http://http://vfl.ru/fotos/b98c122e999589.html)

Application letter of the parents

When you hear about someone's illness, in private, you hope that the trouble will avoid you and your relatives. Alas, fate was not kind to our family. It suddenly hit someone, who has not even had time to understand life, our son.

The christen-name, we made meaningfully, because the child was long-awaited, and, of course we predicted him a bright and happy future. Say more, and now we are sure of it, in spite of the terrible diagnosis of doctors to Yaroslav.

Left adrenal neuroblastoma with para-aortic lymph nodes lesions, multiple lesions of bones, N-myc amplification, 1r36 - positive ... It is difficult to express in words the horror, which we experienced after learning about all this.

From the date of diagnosis, our little son was carefully examined in the Cancer Center, was conducted an operation on tumor elimination, followed by six courses of chemotherapy and transplantation of bone marrow stem cells. But fate is still unrelenting. Short remission was replaced by redevelopment of tumor. After the first surgery was the second one. However, as the next chemotherapy.

Unfortunately, our medicine is unable to overcome this disease, because the tumor affected the skeletal system. And we can not see all hardships of our little son, and therefore are looking for funds to conduct an extra examination and treatment in Germany. This is the only chance for Yaroslavushka to survive.

It is a vast sum of money, even for wealthy people, which we are not. And so we can do nothing except relying only on the compassion and generosity of everyone who knows our history. Our child is weakening day by day, but he is one head elder than his coevals, because he understands that he must fight for every moment of his life.

We do not just ask, we entreat – offer us a helping hand! Support our young and very friendly family, do not let us lose the only chance to save our little son! Each of your Every dollar may play a decisive part in the struggle for life of Yaroslavushka!

Sincerely Yours,Natalia and Vasilij Javorskie

In his two years small Yaroslav has experienced much. The child has the most insidious form of cancer - left adrenal neuroblastoma with para-aortic lymph nodes lesions, multiple lesions of bones, N-myc positive and a lot of obscure medical terms. Insidious, because very often children get in the hospital only at the III or IV stages. This disease affects only children, the most dangerous age - up to 5 years. But about namely this horrible disease is said: "Childhood Cancer is curable!"

In the case of Yaroslav, unfortunately, short remission was replaced by redevelopment of tumor. At this time doctors had predicted two variants of the situation development. The first - the child recovers from next chemotherapy, and he will need only a special course of MIBG therapy. The second - the bone marrow of the boy will have to be transplanted, with all attendant procedures and preparations for surgery. A visit to a German hospital is necessary in both cases, which means a corresponding amount to pay for treatment. The only difference is that in the first case – it is $30k, in the second case –it is $50k. The miracle did not happen and at this time – it is necessary to transplant the marrow bone….

Friends, Yaroslav's life and his chance of recovery are in our hands. We ask everyone very much, who reads these lines, not to remain indifferent to the fate of the boy and to help him to win the disease.

[More information...](http://http://igg.me/p/246090?a=848746)
